title: Error Correction Aware Dependent Task Offloading in Satellite Edge Computing
authors: Jian Zhou, Anxu Huang, Lu Zhao, Anfeng Liu, Qian He, Fu Xiao
abstract: Satellite edge computing (SEC) extends the capabilities of task offloading from the ground to near-Earth space by leveraging the wide coverage of satellites. However, the high-speed movement and long-range communication of satellites lead to Doppler shifts and signal loss, which can result in a high bit error rate (BER) and degrade communication link quality. As a result of the high BER, error correction is required, inevitably incurring additional delay during task offloading. Therefore, in this paper, we focus on the problem of <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">E</u>rror <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</u>orrection aware <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">D</u>ependency <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">T</u>ask <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">O</u>ffloading (EC-DTO) in satellite environments. By jointly considering task dependencies and BER-induced error correction delays, we formulate the EC-DTO problem as a constrained optimization problem with the objective of minimizing the makespan of users' tasks. To solve this problem, we propose APOS, which is an event-driven offloading framework based on task-<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">A</u>daptive <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">P</u>rioritization and <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">O</u>ptimistic-finish-time-based node <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">S</u>election. Specifically, APOS adopts an online offloading framework that triggers scheduling upon task arrivals and completions with task dependency awareness. It iteratively updates task readiness and performs offloading decisions to reduce the overall makespan in the SEC environment. Simulation results show that APOS outperforms the representative methods, lowering the average makespan by 20.7% and the deadline violation ratio by 84.5% on average across all cases.

title: Distributed Finite-Time SoC Estimation for Lithium-Ion Battery Packs
authors: Heng Li, Haiya Yu, Ren Zhu, Chen Le, Yue Wu, Weirong Liu
abstract: Accurate estimation of state of charge (SoC) for lithium-ion battery packs is crucial for ensuring system safety and optimizing energy management. Most existing SoC estimation methods are centralized, relying on a central node to unify computation, which tend to lead to a heavy communication burden and poor reliability in the event of node or communication failure. Therefore, this article proposes a SoC estimation method for lithium-ion battery packs based on a distributed consensus algorithm. The method consists of two functional modules, the first part of which employs the Luenberger observer for SoC estimation of each battery cell. The second part utilizes the signed gradient descent flow algorithm to realize the distributed estimation of the SoC of battery cells, and ultimately obtains the SoC of the battery pack. We demonstrate that the proposed method converges in a finite time. Experiments verify the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method. The results show that, compared with the centralized methods, the proposed approach exhibits superior fault tolerance, maintaining estimation errors within 1% even under communication failures.

title: Current Trends in Ultrasound Wearables: Spotlight on System Architecture_supp1-3664011.pdf
authors: Christian Kupsch

title: Integration—A Major Driver of Power Electronics Innovation
authors: Jonas Huber, Thomas M. Jahns, Johann W. Kolar
abstract: Integration is a fundamental innovation vector and features prominently among the X-Concepts of power electronics (modularization, decentralization, digitalization, hybridization, integration) that facilitate x-times performance improvements, utilizing basic scaling laws. After briefly introducing these X-Concepts, the paper takes a holistic perspective on integration in power electronics and highlights the various aspects beyond the typical focus on maximizing power density of components and building blocks by means of advanced packaging and manufacturing methods: Integration concepts exist on all hierarchical levels of power electronics functional elements and units (materials, component, building blocks, converters, systems, and systems of systems), and encompasses not only the hardware but also the software/control domains, especially on the higher hierarchical levels. Salient examples for integration on all levels are provided. Moreover, we discuss benefits and barriers of integration in power electronics, and challenges and opportunities for academic research. There is an immediate need for research to drive integration on all levels in combination with the other X-Concepts. Thereby, new tools for the multi-domain design of highly integrated systems are necessary, which should also inform digital twins of hardware realizations to enable augmented-reality experimental analysis, i.e., enhancing measured terminal waveforms by estimates of non-accessible inner quantities, possibly using artificial intelligence and/or machine learning. Finally, we consider power electronics integration in the wider context, i.e., complementing technological by economical and environmental aspects, which leads to the “Integration Trilemma” representing the three key competing requirements in the context of integration: ultra-compact realizations of units with specific functionality, low costs/economies of scale through standardization, and, importantly, the dismantlability at the end of life, which is crucial for facilitating repair, refurbishment, and recycling, i.e., compatibility with a future circular economy: Power Electronics 5.0, which refers to the general next step of power electronics development where X-Concepts etc. are implemented in an environmentally compatible form.

title: Explanation-Guided Adversarial Training for Robust and Interpretable Models
authors: Chao Chen, Yanhui Chen, Shanshan Lin, Dongsheng Hong, Shu Wu, Xiangwen Liao, Chuanyi Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved remarkable performance in many tasks, yet they often behave as opaque black boxes. Explanation-guided learning (EGL) methods steer DNNs using human-provided explanations or supervision on model attributions. These approaches improve interpretability but typically assume benign inputs and incur heavy annotation costs. In contrast, both predictions and saliency maps of DNNs could dramatically alter facing imperceptible perturbations or unseen patterns. Adversarial training (AT) can substantially improve robustness, but it does not guarantee that model decisions rely on semantically meaningful features. In response, we propose Explanation-Guided Adversarial Training (EGAT), a unified framework that integrates the strength of AT and EGL to simultaneously improve prediction performance, robustness, and explanation quality. EGAT generates adversarial examples on the fly while imposing explanation-based constraints on the model. By jointly optimizing classification performance, adversarial robustness, and attributional stability, EGAT is not only more resistant to unexpected cases, including adversarial attacks and out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios, but also offer human-interpretable justifications for the decisions. We further formalize EGAT within the Probably Approximately Correct learning framework, demonstrating theoretically that it yields more stable predictions under unexpected situations compared to standard AT. Empirical evaluations on OOD benchmark datasets show that EGAT consistently outperforms competitive baselines in both clean accuracy and adversarial accuracy (+37%) while producing more semantically meaningful explanations, and requiring only a limited increase (+16%) in training time.

title: HURC-MM: Holistic Uncertainty-Responsive Control for Mobile Manipulator Grasping_supp1-3669764.mp4
authors: Qing Tang
abstract: We present a holistic uncertainty-responsive control system that enables mobile manipulators to perform human- like adaptive grasping by dynamically coordinating base-manipulator motion and uncertainty-aware decision-making. Our key innovations include: (1) a continuous visual monitoring strategy: Distant Watch Close Grasp (DWCG) for dynamic object grasping that maintains persistent visual tracking throughout the entire approach-to-grasp trajectory which ensures uninterrupted target observation from initial detection to final grasp execution; (2) a strategic active retreat mechanism that optimizes withdrawal trajectories based on uncertainty quantification to address Blurred-View Clear-Grasp (BVCG) challenges; and (3) a unified control framework that integrates DWCG and BVCG strategies via quadratic programming. By weighting distance-to-target and uncertainty metrics, our method dynamically optimizes end-effector pose - DWCG guides orientation while BVCG determines position. This enables seamless approach/retreat transitions within a single control loop, eliminating decoupled planning. Experimental validation demonstrates that HURC-MM significantly improves grasping reliability and adaptability in dynamic environments through its human- like “observe-while-moving” capability and intelligent retreat from tracking uncertainty.

title: Electrothermal Design of Silicon-Doped Microheaters for Optical Phase Change Memory
authors: Francis Vasquez-Aza, Hongyi Sun, Chuanyu Lian, Yi-Siou Huang, Chih-Yu Lee, Steven A. Vitale, Ichiro Takeuchi, Juejun Hu, Nathan Youngblood, Carlos A. Ríos Ocampo, Georges Pavlidis
abstract: The growing interest in optical phase change materials (PCMs) has spurred the development of programmable photonics systems with zero-static power. Their optical properties make them well-suited for nonvolatile retention and reversible phase switching. PCMs can be switched using optical pulses or integrated microheaters. Microheaters provide Joule heating to indirectly induce the phase change. This mechanism offers the most promising route to achieving scalability through CMOS integration and reliable multilevel modulation. However, optimizing the design and modulation of microheater devices has become challenging due to the complex electrothermal dynamics, which require high temporal and spatial resolution techniques for accurate assessment. This article presents an electrothermal co-design of silicon-doped microheaters through advanced thermal characterization, which can reveal temperature distributions, on the submicrosecond scale, not readily obtainable through computational methods. We evaluate the geometrical and electrical parameters dependence on the transient thermal transport to maximize the hotspot temperature and quenching rates, as well as the impact of the pulsewidth on the multilevel response and energy consumption. High resolution transient thermoreflectance imaging (TTI) was used to measure and evaluate the temperature distribution under pulsed biasing (0.2–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s). Reduced doped region lengths (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lt 6~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m) enable higher current densities to maximize the temperature and quenching rates. Additionally, shorter pulsewidths (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lt 1~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s) enable well-defined multilevel temperature profile responses and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx 13$</tex-math> </inline-formula> times reduction in energy consumption.

title: Nearly tight bounds for testing tree tensor network states
authors: Benjamin Lovitz, Angus Lowe
abstract: Tree tensor network states (TTNS) generalize the notion of having low Schmidt-rank to multipartite quantum states, through a parameter known as the bond dimension. This leads to succinct representations of quantum many-body systems with a tree-like entanglement structure. In this work, we study the task of testing whether an unknown pure state is a TTNS on n qudits with bond dimension at most r, or is far in trace distance from any such state. We first establish that, independent of the dimension of the state, O(nr2) copies suffice to accomplish this task with onesided error. We then prove that Ω(nr2/ log n) copies are necessary for any test with one-sided error whenever r ≥ 2 + log n. In particular, this closes a roughly quadratic gap in the previous bounds for testing matrix product states in this setting. On the other hand, when r = 2 we show that Θ(√n) copies are both necessary and sufficient for the related task of testing whether a state is a product of n bipartite states having Schmidt-rank at most r, for some choice of the qudit dimensions. We also study the performance of tests using measurements performed on a small number of copies at a time. Here, we obtain new bounds for testing rank, Schmidt-rank, and TTNS when the tester is restricted to making measurements on r + 1 copies of the state.

title: Multi-domain Fusion Based Fatigued Driving Detection using Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave radar
authors: Yong Wang, Jie Lan, Mu Zhou, Liangbo Xie, Xiaolong Yang, Bao Peng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Facial movements are one of the key indicators of a driver’s state. Accurate estimation of facial movements can improve the accuracy of fatigue driving detection. The existing solutions suffer from inconvenient configuration or privacy leakage risks. Radar offers inherent privacy protection and non-contact operation and holding the potential to address the aforementioned shortcomings. Therefore, this paper proposes a method for facial movement recognition using a Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar. First, an exponential weighting cancellation algorithm is applied to suppress interference and enhance the signal-to-noise ratio of the facial signal, thereby enabling further extraction of the target’s amplitude signal. Then, the intermediate frequency (IF) signal is decomposed using Multivariate Variational Mode Decomposition (MVMD), and a correlation-based selection criterion is proposed to select potential eigenmodes for blinking and yawning signals construction. The micro-Doppler features are obtained via Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT). Finally, we propose the Multi-scale information fusion Convolutional neural network for Small sample-Driven Fatigue detection (MCSF-Net), which integrates the target’s frequency-domain micro-Doppler features with time-domain amplitude signals for fatigue detection. Experimental results show that the average classification accuracy reaches 92.71 ± 2.13, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Performance analysis of multi-user task offloading in mobile edge computing networks: A hierarchical modeling approach
authors: Lili Jiang, Yaping Cao, Ying Sun, Xuliang Wang, Yiqun Li, Dan Xu
abstract: Recently, the explosive growth of connected intelligent user devices has generated massive amounts of latency sensitive and computationally intensive applications and services, which leads to a sharp increase in network traffic. Therefore, UAV-assisted mobile edge computing networks (UMECN) are proposed to alleviate network congestion and reduce response time. This paper focuses on quantitatively evaluating UMECN service performance under different parameters, which helps to identify performance bottlenecks, and provide a basis for optimizing services, thereby improving service quality, enhancing user experience. Firstly, we propose the hierarchical model for capturing the dynamical behaviors of UMECN system, supporting interruption/resume uploading and returning process of delay sensitive tasks due to UAV movement. And then, we derive the performance metric formulas of UAV movement and UMECN system, and propose a multi-factor selection strategy base the task historical waiting time, queue length, and the number of remaining resources (WQRSS). Finally, we verify the approximate accuracy of the proposed formulas and model by comparing numerical and simulation results, demonstrate the effectiveness of the model through comparative experiments, and evaluate the impact of different parameters on UMECN service performance, which can help service providers effectively manage UMECN system.

title: Enhanced Multi-Target Detection with Optimal Sparse MIMO Radar Architecture by Polynomial Interpolation Method
authors: Xincheng Tang, Xiaokang Qi, Kuiwen Xu, Dongfang Ma, Chunyi Song, Zhiwei Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Multi-target detection is an important scientific proposition for millimeter-wave multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) radar systems. Due to the short wavelength at millimeter-wave bands, multi-channel MIMO arrays are typically realized with very dense element spacing (often on the order of λ/2), which corresponds to only millimeter-level physical separation. It increases near-field interaction and parasitic coupling through the feed or package, making mutual coupling between adjacent channels severe and deteriorating the sensing performance. However, conventional decoupling methods unavoidably increase system hardware complexity, rendering them incompatible with compact sensing platforms. As a result, sparse MIMO radar supposes to be an efficient solution. Meanwhile, the non-uniform virtual co-array generated by sparse configurations typically contains missing lags, defined as holes, leading to incomplete virtual-aperture measurements and may degrade DOA resolution and multi-target separability. Conventional used interpolation-based methods utilize the convex optimization algorithm to fill in the holes, which significantly elevates computational complexity. In this paper, we proposed an enhanced multi-target detection approach using optimal sparse millimeter-wave radar architecture and polynomial interpolation method to reduce mutual coupling among different sensing channels. In this system, a lightweight sparse MIMO radar array configuration is proposed firstly. Then, the polynomial interpolation method is introduced to achieve DOF enhancement with lower computational and hardware complexity than traditional convex-optimization-based methods. Finally, the proposed approach is experimentally validated through multi-target detection scenarios, successfully identifying 8(6) targets using a 5(4) sensing channels. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed sparse MIMO radar architecture over multi-target detection performance and computational complexity, offering a practical solution for diverse MIMO radar sensing platforms.

title: Dynamic distribution entropy analysis via ultrafast intracardiac echocardiography for monitoring of cardiac radiofrequency ablation_supp1-3669561.docx
authors: Chulhong Kim
abstract: Objective: To enable reliable, noise-robust monitoring of lesion formation during radiofrequency (RF) ablation with intracardiac echocardiography (ICE), this study introduces Dynamic Distribution Entropy (DDE), which quantifies temporal–spatial changes in backscatter statistics while suppressing ablation-induced interference. Methods: DDE integrates ultrafast plane-wave acquisition with singular value decomposition clutter filtering to stabilize entropy estimates. We evaluated DDE in (i) simulations modeling ablation-driven scatterer-size changes and (ii) ex-vivo porcine hearts imaged by ICE during RF ablation. DDE was compared with typical Shannon entropy, k-nearest neighbor entropy, cumulative residual entropy, and horizontally normalized Shannon entropy. Metrics included structural similarity (SSIM), intersection-over-union (IoU), and lesion-size agreement versus optical ground truth. Results: In simulations, DDE and CRE closely tracked dynamic scatterer evolution, yielding highest SSIM over frames (0.96). In ex-vivo experiments, DDE demonstrated the most accurate lesion-size estimation under RF-ON conditions, exhibiting the lowest bias (4.85 mm2), standard deviation (1.53 mm2), and root mean square error (5.08 mm2) among all evaluated entropy-based methods. Lesion sizes derived from DDE exhibited the best agreement with optical measurements. Conclusion: DDE provides robust and accurate intraoperative monitoring of lesion formation in ICE guided RF ablation, outperforming conventional entropy imaging under ablation-related noise. Significance: DDE offers a practical, noise-resistant quantitative ultrasound biomarker for real-time lesion assessment, supporting decision-making during ICE-guided cardiac ablation without requiring changes to clinical workflow.

title: Deployment and Beamforming Design for RIS-assisted Indoor Wireless Communication in Smart Home
authors: Shuang Liang, Guangliang Ren
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) assisted indoor wireless communication for smart home is investigated to improve the communication coverage efficiency and spectral efficiency by designing deployment and beamforming. A two-stage communication scheme is proposed. In the first stage, RIS deployment is designed with the location information of long-term stationary obstacles to maximize the normalized effective coverage efficiency (NE-CE). Three RIS deployment algorithms are proposed, which are the Heuristic Deployment (H-D) algorithm, the Simulated Annealing Angle Optimization-based Deployment (SAAO-D) algorithm, and the Simulated Annealing-based Deployment (SA-D) algorithm. In the second stage, partial channel state information (CSI)-based beamforming is designed to ensure spectral efficiency while reducing the complexity of channel estimation. In this stage, terminal association and passive beamforming are designed based on the deployed RISs and terminal location information. Additionally, closed-form active beamforming is derived based on partial CSI. Simulation results demonstrate that the NE-CE of the proposed algorithm is 1.45 times higher than that of the existing scheme when deploying 2 RISs with 64 elements each. The spectral efficiency of the partial CSI-based beamforming scheme reaches 96.5% that of the perfect-CSI-based scheme with 3 obstacles and 3 RISs.

title: Ensemble Learning for Large Language Models in Text and Code Generation: A Survey
authors: Mari Ashiga, Wei Jie, Fan Wu, Vardan Voskanyan, Fateme Dinmohammadi, Paul Brookes, Jingzhi Gong, Zheng Wang, Rafail Giavrimis, Mike Basios, Leslie Kanthan
abstract: Generative Pretrained Transformers (GPTs) are foundational Large Language Models (LLMs) for text generation. However, individual LLMs often produce inconsistent outputs and exhibit biases, limiting their representation of diverse language patterns. The closed-source nature of many powerful LLMs further restricts industry applications due to data privacy concerns. Inspired by successes in text generation, LLM ensemble techniques are now increasingly explored for code generation. This article reviews these emerging ensemble approaches to enhance understanding, encourage further research, and promote practical implementation in both text and code generation. We categorize LLM ensembles into seven main methods—weight merging, knowledge fusion, mixture-of-experts, reward ensemble, output ensemble, routing, and cascading—analyzing capabilities of those approaches. Our findings highlight key benefits such as improved diversity representation, enhanced output quality, and greater application flexibility. These insights aid model selection for real-world tasks and crucially, lay groundwork for extending ensemble strategies to multimodal LLMs and agentic workflows.

title: A 28-nm 9-kb SRAM Computing-in-Memory Macro With Segmented Charge Sharing for Multimode MAC Operations
authors: Wenjuan Lu, Lening Tan, Simin Wang, Tianchen Xue, Chunyu Peng, Zhiting Lin, Xiulong Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Computing-in-memory (CIM) is a novel approach to solve the von Neumann bottleneck and improve energy efficiency and throughput. This article presents an SRAM-CIM macro based on segmented charge sharing to support multimode multiply-and-accumulate (MAC) operations, including binary weight network (BWN) MAC, ternary weight network (TWN) MAC, and multibit MAC operations. Signed 9-bit input MAC operations can be supported in BWN and TWN networks through multiple cycles. The multibit MAC operations are realized by the cooperation of two cell structures, which eliminates the need to convert negative numbers into complements and reduces the area and power consumption of the chip. Additionally, the proposed segmented charge-sharing scheme increases the speed of accumulation and improves the overall energy efficiency of the chip. The proposed 9-kb macro is implemented in 28-nm CMOS technology with an energy efficiency of 30.74 TOPS/W and an area efficiency of 1.15 TOPS/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> in multibit MAC mode. At the system level, a ResNet-based model achieves an accuracy of 94.59% on the CIFAR-10 dataset and 74.64% on the CIFAR-100 dataset, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed CIM architecture for practical neural network inference. Compared to prior designs, it not only supports additional computing modes but also demonstrates notable improvements in both energy and area efficiency.

title: “X of Information” Continuum: A Survey on AI-Driven Multi-dimensional Metrics for Next-Generation Networked Systems
authors: Beining Wu, Jun Huang, Shui Yu
abstract: The development of next-generation networking systems has shifted from throughput-based paradigms towards intelligent, information-aware designs that emphasize information quality, relevance, and utility rather than data volume. Classical network metrics such as latency and packet loss remain significant but are insufficient for modern intelligent applications that include autonomous vehicles, digital twins, and metaverse environments. This survey presents the first comprehensive study of the “X of Information” continuum through a systematic four-dimensional taxonomic framework that structures information metrics along temporal, quality/utility, reliability/robustness, and network/communication dimensions. We uncover increasing interdependencies among these dimensions, where temporal freshness triggers quality evaluation, which enables reliability appraisal that supports effective network delivery. Our analysis reveals that artificial intelligence technologies such as deep reinforcement learning, multi-agent systems, and neural optimization models enable adaptive, context-aware optimization of competing information quality objectives. Our study of six critical application domains that span autonomous transportation, industrial IoT, healthcare digital twins, UAV communications, LLM ecosystems, and metaverse settings illustrates the promise of multi-dimensional information metrics for diverse operational needs. The survey identifies key implementation challenges that include metric integration, information-driven resource allocation, semantic communication, and federated learning optimization. We highlight critical research agendas in unified theoretical models, AI-augmented dynamic optimization, and cross-layer orchestration mechanisms that lay the foundation for intelligent, value-aware communication systems.

title: Supervised Anomaly Detection for Generalized Deepfake Detection_supp1-3669841.pdf
authors: Lin Lu
abstract: Generalization to unseen forgeries remains a significant challenge in deepfake detection. Existing methods often focus on detecting specific forgery patterns, such as blending boundaries, identity leakage, etc. However, their overfitting to known fake samples during training restricts their generalization to unseen manipulations. To address this limitation, we propose Supervised Anomaly Detection for Generalized Deepfake Detection (SAD-GDD), a novel framework that treats real data as normal and fake data as anomalies, leveraging anomaly detection to improve generalization. At its core, we propose a Supervised Anomaly Detection Strategy (SADS), which constructs a refined Multivariate Gaussian Distribution (MGD) for real data and applies supervised guidance for fake data to strengthen the discrimination between real and fake. To further enhance performance, we design a two-stream network comprising a High-Frequency Texture Branch (HFT-B) and an RGB Branch (RGB-B), both optimized with SADS. Additionally, we propose a Multi-head Cross-Attention Enhancement Block (MCAEB) to enhance the capability of RGB-B in capturing high-frequency features. Extensive experiments show that SAD-GDD achieves superior generalization compared to state-of-the-art methods across multiple widely used datasets. The code will be made publicly available upon publication.

title: Single and Multiple Open-Switch Fault Diagnosis in Electric Drives via Zero-Current Interval Analysis
authors: Luis Esteban Venghi, Facundo Aguilera, Pablo de la Barrera, Cristian De Angelo
abstract: In this work, a new and simple method for open-switch fault diagnosis in electric drives based on zero-current detection is proposed. The approach requires only two phase current sensors, with the measured currents being normalized and separated into positive and negative half-cycles. A variable-sample-time moving average is introduced for post-processing the signals. Zero-current interval detection is employed to diagnose both single and multiple open-switch faults. The proposed method was experimentally validated under various fault conditions, including single-switch, crossed-switch, full-leg, and two upper- or lower-switch open-circuit faults. Experimental results demonstrate that, despite its simplicity and ease of implementation, the method reliably detects all single and double open-switch fault scenarios in less than one electrical cycle, across different motor operating conditions.

title: 6G-Enabled Digital Twin Frameworks for Real-Time Financial Risk Prediction and Strategic Management
authors: Huixin Xu, Fengying Liu
abstract: Despite complex linkages and turbulent markets, the rapidly developing digital financial sector demands effective real-time risk management. By implementing a revolutionary multi-loop feedback architecture for continuous, adaptive risk control and decision stabilization, the Digital Twin-enabled Financial Intelligence Network over 6G (DT-FIN6G) can overcome key obstacles such as latency, data siloing, and limited prediction accuracy. The platform leverages edge artificial intelligence and 6G terahertz bandwidth to synchronize data-driven insights across remote digital twins. Additionally, it employs federated learning to improve model generalization and privacy protection while adhering to stringent regulatory constraints. This synchronized strategy assures that operations are resilient, transparent, and compliant. It also enables rapid liquidity optimization, responsive asset reallocation, and strong mitigation of systemic shocks in financial ecosystems. Simulations have shown that DT-FIN6G can achieve significant latency reductions, improved predictive performance, and strong privacy guarantees. This establishes a new standard for autonomous, secure, and adaptable financial intelligence platforms.

title: Combined AES-256 Encryption with MATLAB and Verilog HDL Biometric Authentication
authors: Pasuluri Bindu Swetha, Vasagiri Shivani, Kuruva Shivani, Shaik Mubashira, Charan Sai Raja Vennakandla, S. Fowzia Sultana
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: This research provides a Biometric-based AES-256 Encryption and Decryption Authentication System that combines cutting-edge cryptographic methods with fingerprint identification to offer dual-layered security. Verilog HDL implements hardware-level biometric matching and AES-256 encryption/decryption modules, while MATLAB preprocesses fingerprint images and converts them into 128-bit hexadecimal templates. The architecture was synthesized and simulated using Xilinx Vivado and includes a 256-bit key AES-256 core, a fingerprint matcher, and a Finite State Machine (FSM) controller that ensures cryptographic operations only take place when biometric authentication is completed. While study of the FPGA implementation demonstrates effective resource use, low power consumption, and minimal latency, simulation results show correct encryption, decryption, and access control.

title: AI-Based Phishing Attack Simulation Using Deep Learning Techniques and Cloud-Enabled Email Spoofing Detection
authors: Dinesh Babu G L, M. Indra Priya, Suresh K, M. Malini, D. Vikram, P. Santhoshkumar
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Phishing attacks, which frequently involve social engineering to trick people with phony emails, continue to be a persistent issue in cyber security. This paper introduces an AI-based framework that incorporates a cloud-enabled email spoofing detection system and uses deep learning to mimic phishing detection. The method uses LSTM models, Logistic Regression, and TF-IDF vectorization to categorize phishing content. Blowfish and RSA are two examples of encryption techniques used for safe result communication. The model was trained on a small dataset of labeled phishing and legitimate emails, and it achieved a preliminary accuracy of 50%. Future improvements will be made using real-world datasets. Confidentiality in the transmission of risk ratings and predictions is guaranteed by the encrypted output.In addition to identifying phishing content, the suggested solution attempts to mimic the actions of phishing campaigns, providing defensive and instructional tools. The foundation for intelligent, safe, and flexible phishing mitigation systems that are appropriate for cloud-based email infrastructures is laid by this work.

title: Advancement in Ransomware Detection Using Deep Learning
authors: Sonali Batra, Rajesh Kumar Shrivastava, Sanjay Kumar, Shobhit Kumar, Vardaan Shukla
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: In the digital era, malicious software, viruses, and ransomware are quite a common problem that is being faced by people, as the world is moving forward towards technology. We are exploring new ways for automation that increase the digital footprint. Hence, hackers introduce new viruses and ransomware very frequently. This paper focuses mainly on ransomware detection with the help of machine learning and deep learning methods. Here, we test a malicious dataset for ransomware detection with SVM (Support Vector Machine), ANN (Artificial Neural Network), CNN (Convolution Neural Network), Xgboost and Random Forest Classifier. The experimental results reached up to 99% accuracy.

title: A Unified AI-Powered Resume Generation and ML-Based Candidate Filtering Framework for Smart Campus Recruitment
authors: D K Shareef, A Safiya Sulthana, Jardoddi Rajeswari, Gampala Vasavi, Goneguntla Vasavi
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: The manual nature of the campus recruitment process, which includes creating resumes, screening, feature consideration, and shortlisting of candidates makes the process tedious and therefore delays and inconsistency in the hiring process. In order to overcome these drawbacks, in this paper, a Unified AI-Powered Resume Generation and Machine Learning-powered Candidate Filtering Framework is introduced that can be specifically used in smart campus recruitment systems. In the design presented, a transformer-based Natural Language Generation (NLG) engine to generate resumes automatically, an NLP-optimized semantic extraction engine to profile skills and academic qualification, and an ensemble-based ranker with Random Forest and XGBoost to predict suitability have been incorporated. Experimental analysis on a sample of 1,500 student profiles attained resume generation accuracy of 92.4% and skill extraction F1-score of 88.0% and candidate prediction accuracy of 89.7% and decreased recruitment processing time by about 43 percent. Also, the analysis of fit-score distribution shows that the framework is capable of separating high-potential candidates and average performers and allows to generate a shortlist using data-driven methods without bias. The findings affirm that the system has the potential to significantly improve the scalability, transparency and efficiency of any campus placement operation of large scale. The publication sets a standard that guides the development of fully autonomous AIs based hiring ecosystem that will include behavioural inference, psychometric analytics, and the automated interview-readiness assessment.

title: RidgeWalker: Perfectly Pipelined Graph Random Walks on FPGAs
authors: Hongshi Tan, Yao Chen, Xinyu Chen, Qizhen Zhang, Cheng Chen, Weng-Fai Wong, Bingsheng He
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: Graph Random Walks (GRWs) offer efficient approximations of key graph properties and have been widely adopted in many applications. However, GRW workloads are notoriously difficult to accelerate due to their strong data dependencies, irregular memory access patterns, and imbalanced execution behavior. While recent work explores FPGA-based accelerators for GRWs, existing solutions fall far short of hardware potential due to inefficient pipelining and static scheduling. This paper presents RidgeWalker, a high-performance GRW accelerator designed for datacenter FPGAs. The key insight behind RidgeWalker is that the Markov property of GRWs allows decomposition into stateless, fine-grained tasks that can be executed out-of-order without compromising correctness. Building on this insight, RidgeWalker introduces an asynchronous pipeline architecture with a feedback-driven scheduler grounded in queuing theory. This design enables perfect pipelining and adaptive load balancing. We prototype RidgeWalker on FPGAs and evaluate its performance across a range of GRW algorithms and real-world graph datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that RidgeWalker achieves an average speedup of 7.0× over state-of-the-art FPGA solutions and 8.1× over GPU solutions, with peak speedups of up to 71.0× and 22.9×, respectively. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Xtra-Computing/RidgeWalker.

title: MSF-Net:Multi-Sector Fuzzy Network For Interpretable Eye Disease Classification From Low-Quality Fundus Images
authors: Sudha E, Kaviya Shree K, Elakkiya G P, Akil B, Devanand R
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Distracting dementia patients While there is no cure for dementia, early identification, treatment and reverse the deterioration if the problem is caused by glaucoma or cataracts or diabetic retinopathy. But in many low-resource areas, there is a shortage of trained doctors and poor-quality images make diagnosis difficult. To overcome this, we present MSF-Net (Multi-Sector Fuzzy Network), a new and completely interpretable software-only method based on poor images of the fundus and no deep learning or transfer learning models for the classification of eye pathologies. To extract features such as vessel density, texture deterioration, and brightness levels, the MSF-Net splits each input image into several anatomical regions of interest like disc, macula, and the periphery. Classification, which is both strong and interpretable, is achieved by subjecting these features through a fuzzy inference system characterized by condition specific membership functions and rule bases. MSF-Net can facilitate doctor decision-making by returning readable confidence levels, which are not provided by black-box models. It is appropriate for mobile or embedded deployment in remote or rural health facilities, owing to its light-weight architecture and off-line ability. In this study, there is a new diagnostic tool of ophthalmology based fuzzy logic method which are easy to understand, for available and cheap. The algorithm achieved good classification performance in challenging settings, with accuracies of 85.6% for diabetic retinopathy(DR), 82.4% for cataracts, and 79.8% for glaucomatous changes when evaluating the classification performance on a curated test set of degraded fundus images.

title: A New Method for Virtual Inertia Control in Wind Turbines with Doubly-Fed Induction Generators
authors: Martin Geske, Priyabrata Shaw
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: Virtual Inertia Control (VIC) provided by Wind Turbines (WTs) can contribute to frequency support in electrical power networks. For this reason, in addition to conventional systems such as power plants and battery storage systems, new and existing wind farms are of interest to VIC. WTs with Doubly-Fed Induction Generators (DFIGs) have VIC limitations when operating at low rotor frequency, which leads to higher temperature stress on semiconductors and thus constraints on lifetime and power output. This work presents a novel control method that effectively limits the rotor current and adjusts the reactive power supplied by the rotor-side inverter during frequency support. The WTs’ speed recovery following a VIC event is also taken into consideration. Temperature simulations for power semiconductors are presented and discussed. Flexible current limiting and reactive power control for the rotor-side inverter are presented and analyzed.

title: Cell-Free Massive MIMO-Assisted SWIPT Using Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces
authors: Thien Duc Hua, Mohammadali Mohammadi, Hien Quoc Ngo, Michail Matthaiou
abstract: This study explores a next-generation multiple access (NGMA) framework for cell-free massive MIMO (CF-mMIMO) systems enhanced by stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIMs), aiming to improve simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) performance. A fundamental challenge lies in optimally selecting the operating modes of access points (APs) to jointly maximize the received energy and satisfy spectral efficiency (SE) quality-of-service constraints. Practical system impairments, including a non-linear harvested energy model, pilot contamination (PC), channel estimation errors, and reliance on long-term statistical channel state information (CSI), are considered. We derive closed-form expressions for both the achievable SE and the average sum harvested energy (sum-HE). A mixed-integer non-convex optimization problem is formulated to jointly optimize the SIM phase shifts, APs mode selection, and power allocation to maximize average sum-HE under SE and average harvested energy constraints. To solve this problem, we propose a centralized training, decentralized execution (CTDE) framework based on deep reinforcement learning (DRL), which efficiently handles high-dimensional decision spaces. A Markovian environment and a normalized joint reward function are introduced to enhance the training stability across on-policy and off-policy DRL algorithms. Additionally, we provide a two-phase convex-based solution as a theoretical robust performance. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed DRL-based CTDE framework achieves SWIPT performance comparable to convexification-based solution, while significantly outperforming baselines.

title: Fed-MA Driven Multi-Modal Data Fusion Framework with Autoencoders for Scalable and Privacy-Preserving Edge Intelligence
authors: Natrayan L
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Rapid evolution of Industry 4.0 generates massive volumes of heterogeneous data across manufacturing, logistics, and quality-control systems, creating challenges in scalability, privacy, and real-time decision-making. This study introduces a Fed-MA-driven multi-modal data fusion framework that integrates autoencoders and federated learning to enable decentralized, privacy-preserving analytics in industrial environments. At the local level, an autoencoder-based featurelevel fusion extracts latent representations from multi-sensor data-vibration, production quality, and logistics-effectively denoising and reducing dimensionality. These features are then trained using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) classifier to capture temporal dependencies, while only model weights are shared with the global server. The Federated Matched Averaging (Fed-MA) algorithm aligns neuron weights across clients, enhancing interoperability and improving the global model's learning efficiency. Experimental results across three industrial datasets demonstrate 95.62 % classification accuracy, 95.47 % F1-score, and a reconstruction loss of 0.015, with significant reductions in communication overhead and improved privacy preservation. The novelty of this work lies in the fusion of autoencoder-based latent representations with FedMA aggregation, enabling scalable, explainable, and energyefficient intelligence for next-generation Industry 4.0 ecosystems. Future extensions include integrating blockchainenabled federated governance for enhanced model security and traceability.

title: Enhancing Charging Stations Planning via Multi-Agent System and Multi-Objective Evolutionary Optimization
authors: Yutong Zhang, Chixin Xiao, Dechen Jiang, Maoxin He, Xiangyun Ge, Siyi Xiao
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Well-planned charging station deployment can fulfill user charging demand and, at the same time, minimize power system and transportation costs. This study proposes a multi-objective evolutionary optimization (MOO) framework, integrated with multi-agent system (MAS), to investigate and optimize the trade-off between the two objectives. The MAS framework incorporates the perspectives of distribution system operator (DSO), transmission system operator (TSO), and electric vehicle (EV) users. These agents collectively define the decision variables for the MOO framework. Based on the decision variables, the MOO framework formulates a bi-objective optimization problem, solved using the MOEA/D-DE algorithm, that simultaneously considers construction and maintenance costs on the grid side and charging and detour costs on the user side. The proposed approach effectively coordinates the location and operational strategies of charging stations while preserving user privacy through the MAS architecture. In case study, EV travel paths are generated using Latin hypercube sampling (LHS), which ensures a more uniform and efficient coverage of the solution space compared with the conventional Monte Carlo (MC) method. Results show that the proposed framework achieves superior solution quality compared with benchmark algorithms, providing operators and users with cost-effective and reliable charging infrastructure configuration solutions that ensure system safety and user satisfaction.

title: Progressive Incentive Scheduling Strategy Considering Virtual Power Plant Response Sensitivity
authors: Yanjia Wang, Da Xie, Xianlong Li, Chen Shi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: 抓取摘要失败: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='ieeexplore.ieee.org', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: /document/11400930/ (Caused by ProtocolError('Connection aborted.', ConnectionResetError(104, 'Connection reset by peer')))

title: A Comprehensive Analysis, Design and Implementation of Optimized Current Control for Enhanced Dynamic Performance and Stability in Single Inductor-Dual Output DC-DC Buck Converter
authors: Vijayasri Averneni, Arghya Chakravarty, Tousif Khan Nizami, Dipankar Deb
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: Multi-output DC-DC converters are widely used in compact power systems, but single-inductor dual-output (SISIDO) converters face critical challenges like cross-regulation and cross-coupling due to the shared inductor. This paper proposes a capacitor current ripple feedback (CCRF) control strategy for SISIDO buck converters to suppress self and cross-regulation and improve transient performance. A detailed small-signal model is systematically derived using state-space averaging, with compact expressions for control coefficients. Compensator parameters are optimized via Mountain Gazelle Optimization (MGO) for optimal dynamics. Sensitivity analysis shows parameter variations have negligible impact on regulation. The strategy is validated through MATLAB simulations, demonstrating superior load regulation, reduced cross-coupling, fast transient response, and robust performance under varying loads.

title: Optimal Fast-Acting Frequency Containment for Modern Power Systems with Variable Inertia
authors: Sadegh Azizi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: This paper proposes an Optimal Fast Frequency Containment (OFFC) approach during loss-of-generation (LoG) events, combining analytical derivation with an optimization framework for resource deployment. The system frequency response is modeled by a second-order power-frequency transfer function, enabling a closed-form characterization of the optimal power injection profile. This profile maximizes the frequency nadir for a given energy input. Building on this, a scalable optimization framework is established to realize the target injection using available generation resources, each represented by a trapezoidal power profile. A novel linear formulation is developed to model the trapezoidal ramping without bilinear terms. This ensures scalability and guarantees global optimality under operational constraints such as ramp limits, delays, and energy capacities. The proposed approach supports real-time deployment decisions while accounting for resource diversity, facilitating greater integration of renewable energy sources.

title: Techno-Economic Optimisation of Large-Scale Electrolyser System for Green Hydrogen Grid Integration
authors: Ahmed Rashwan, Mohamed Khalid Ratib, Kashem M. Muttaqi, Md Rabiul Islam, Danny Sutanto, Mohamed Tarek, Abdelrahman Shata, Tomonobu Senjyu
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Optimising hydrogen production systems through techno-economic analysis is vital for advancing sustainable energy solutions. Prioritising efficient production methods, analysing cost structures, integrating renewables, and leveraging economies of scale to make green hydrogen a practical option for a low-carbon future. This paper presents a methodology for optimally sizing the components of a grid-connected green hydrogen production system comprising a solar PV array, wind turbine, battery system, electrolyser, and an electrical load. A techno-economic analysis using net present value (NPV) as the objective function is used to ascertain optimal component sizes based on minimum production cost. An interior-point linear programming optimisation, considering system operational and physical constraints, with a 5 % discount rate is employed to evaluate the system's economic feasibility, having the electrolyser's power limited to 50 kW. The analyses explore how discount rates between 2 % and 8 % influence the sizing of system components and the amount of hydrogen generated. Over a 20 -year period, the results show the system produced and sold <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0, 9 6 8}$</tex> tons of hydrogen, yielding a positive NPV of $<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$228,840$</tex>, indicating the project's economic feasibility.

title: Estimation of State of Charge of Electric Vehicle Battery for Transportation Using Extended Kalman Filter Method
authors: Jayati Vaish, Khadim Moin Siddiqui, Farhad Ilahi Bakhsh, Sandhya Shrivastava, Amit Kumar, Bhupesh Kumar Pal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: Electric vehicles (EVs) depend on precise state-of-charge (SOC) estimation for optimal performance, increased battery capacity, prevention of overcharge and over discharge, and longer battery life. In this research work, the extended Kalman filter (EKF) method is proposed for SOC estimation of lithium-ion batteries in integration with thermal temperature. The proposed EKF linearizes the nonlinear battery model for the current state estimate. It uses a prediction step and a correction step to refine the SOC estimate. Thus, the proposed EKF method estimates the SOC along with battery terminal voltage and current as inputs with MAPE of 1.47% and RMSE of 0.0025. The results obtained from the EKF method is also compared from the conventional method, i.e., coulomb counting (CC) method, to determine the efficacy of the proposed system. The estimation of SOC at different ambient temperature such as $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is also determined using the EKF method. The commonly used various EV models for transportation, such as Tata Nexon EV (45 kWh), Tata Tiago EV (24 kWh), MG Comet EV (17.4 kWh), MG ZS EV (50.3 kWh), and Mahindra XUV400 (39.4 kWh), are also analyzed and compared on the basis of SOC estimation using the EKF method.

title: A DenseNet-Based Deep Learning Approach for Accurate Classification and Interpretability in the Diagnosis of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
authors: Ragini Y P, Ola Khresat, B.Mamatha, Asha V
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) is a life- threatening hematologic malignancy and requires precise and early diagnosis. In this work we introduce a deep learning model, based on DenseNets, to fully automate the classification and interpretation of all microscopic images of blood smears of ALL. The densely connected nature of DenseNet boosts gradient flow and feature reuse, providing the solid framework of images-based medical diagnosis. The data that is used in the given study is a balanced dataset due to a fair amount of normal and abnormal white blood cell (WBC) images as can be observed through the class distribution plot. Exploratory data analysis consisting of pixel intensity histograms, dimensionality reduction through PCA and t-SNE showed that the distributions of normal and the leukemic cells can be distinguished by their clustering patterns before the model training. It is seen that there exists a discriminative power in the extracted features. The suggested model had a quick convergence with the training and validation sets of 20 epochs. The training and validation accuracies were stabilized at the values of about 98.5 percent and 98, respectively. Loss values in correspondence also showed a steep decrease, which showed that over fitting had a least extent. The confusion matrix ensures the best classification results of test set with accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score of 100%, and zero false positive of negative. Also, PCA and t-SNE visualizations prove the ability of the model to draw clear boundaries between different classes. The transparency of predictions can also be seen through the distributions of feature spaces and this allows clinical transparency. Conclusively, the model constructed using DenseNet performed at an impressive level in monitoring ALL through the blood smear pictures, and serves as an encouraging method in assisting the hematologists in their early diagnosis. Future directions can include (1) integration with explanations methods of AI, and (2) testing on multi- center data to check the robustness and generalizability of the method.

title: Fine-Tuned Xception Model for Automated Classification of Indian Rock Reptiles: A Deep Learning Approach to Python and Russell's Viper Identification
authors: Bura Vijay Kumar, Jatin Sharma, Preeti Badhani, Garima Jaitly
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Particularly for Russell's Viper and Python, the increasing interactions between people and Indian rock reptiles make appropriate and early identification more crucial to assure public safety and support of conservation projects. This work uses a dataset of 10,000 images acquired from the Kaggle platform to investigate the deployment of a fine-tuned Xception model to classify these species from images. Data collecting, preprocessing, model creation, and evaluation are all part of the proposed strategy. Using depth-wise separable convolutions, the fine-tuned Xception model architecture shows remarkable performance in feature extraction and classification activities among the total of 21,394,730 parameters. Graphs exhibiting stable loss and accuracy with low overfitting expose the model to reach an 88% high accuracy. Python's 0.75 points up areas for improvement; nonetheless, review of the classification report shows balanced performance. The results reveal the model's great potential as a tool for early identification, so assisting projects in human safety, animal management, and resource allocation.

title: Levelling Up for Nature: Pathways of Engagement with Green Messages in Games
authors: Celestine Iwendi, Salome Enoshi Uwah, Farnoud Amiri, Ezekiel Gabriel Nwibo, Vandana Sharma, Paul Hollins
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Digital games have become potent platforms for environmental messaging, capable of influencing beliefs, attitudes, and behaviours. This paper explores how players engage with green content embedded within game environments, using mixed-method data from the GREAT Project with more than 181,000 anonymised gameplay sessions. This research analysed cognitive, affective and behavioural responses to in-game environmental messaging and identified key pathways that lead to sustained pro-environmental actions using PCA and K-Means clustering analysis, by which varying types of user were identified from casual to deeply engaged players. The engagement questions were grouped into three categories: narrative frame, behavioural pledges, and social reflection. Our findings showed that 68% of the players participated in at least one green-themed pledge, which indicates a strong sensitivity to environmental content, informing a multilayered engagement model that supports both player experience and environmental literacy known as a 5-pathway green engagement model (5-PGEM), supporting the hypothesis that green messages, when embedded in games, can significantly shape eco-friendly actions.

title: The Power of Trust in Recommender Systems: Improving Outcomes and Reducing Noise
authors: Dheeraj Kumar Sahni, Dhiraj Khurana, Nakul, Suman Rani
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Recommendation is our daily life activity. Trust is the inherent part of the recommender system which plays a chief role in trade of things. Recommender systems are growing with a high speed in the different domains of the digital world. Trusts associated recommenders is a new direction in the recommender system which improves the overall outcome and reduces the noises in the recommendation generation. There are several key dimensions to measure trust through which the generated recommendation can be ranked for the better outcome. The trust ranking explains the reason for the generated recommendation and spreads the correction in the chain of recommendation system which augments the outcome with exponential speed. The multi-axis facets such as accuracy, fairness, privacy, long term user well-being together underscores the growing need of trust propagation mechanism within the recommender system. This article presents the key dimensions of measuring trust propagation and its associated impact on the recommendation system. The result achieve mean average precision score of 0.69 suggests that the system maintains a balanced level of accuracy, successfully distinguishing relevant recommendations from non-relevant ones in the majority of cases.

title: An Adaptive Reinforcement Learning Based Control Strategy for Inverter-Based Resources-Dominated Power Grids
authors: Neelofar Shaukat, Md. Rabiul Islam, Syed Ayaz Ali Shah, Jamil Ahmad Khan, Md Sanwar Hossain, Kashem M. Muttaqi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: The dominance of Inverter-Based Resources (IBRs) is transforming the dynamics of power systems. These IBRs support decarbonization but introduce new challenges for voltage and frequency stability by reducing the system inertia. This research paper explores the Reinforcement Learning (RL) based control strategy for Grid Forming Inverters (GFMIs) to improve the resilience of power systems during disturbances. This study simulates a simplified two-area test system presenting the Spanish grid topology with a fault, comparing the RL control approach against a conventional control strategy. Simulation results illustrated that RL-based control significantly reduces worst-case frequency deviations, reflecting the improvement of controller adaptability and enhanced post-fault recovery. The test system (not representing Iberian grids in full fidelity) demonstrates how adaptive control methodology can mitigate the risk of events like the Iberian Peninsula blackout event in April 2025.

title: Underwater Data Sensing Methods: A Review of Sensors, Compressed Sensing and Imaging, Sparse Recovery Algorithms, and Future Directions
authors: Manohar K, M. Sabarimalai Manikandan
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Modern underwater sensing and monitoring systems use a combination of heterogeneous sensors and cameras, communication networks, artificial intelligence, on-device and edge processing technologies for recording and processing signals/images to detect, locate, track, and explore the underwater things, and the effectiveness of undersea autonomous operations in neutralizing or destroying naval mines. In this paper, we present a systematic review of underwater sensors and cameras with their specifications and sensor-specific data processing techniques, Nyquist-rate sampling (NRS) and compressed sensing (CS)-based acoustic data acquisition (ADAQ) methods, compressed imaging methods, and sparse recovery algorithms to reconstruct the signal and image from the compressive measurements. Based on our methodological reviews, we present the significance of exploring a suitable CS matrix such that the CS-based ADAQ technique can produce either the compressed data or encrypted compressed data, which can aim to reduce overall energy consumption and latency at the on-device and edge computing nodes embedded with underwater monitoring systems and to enable real-time analysis in harsh marine environments. We further present a comparison of different sparse recovery algorithms in terms of distortion metrics and convergence speed so that the performance of the new sparse recovery algorithms can be compared with existing algorithms in the domains of CS-based internet of underwater things (IoUT). Finally, we present future research directions to be considered in improving the energy efficiency of battery-operated underwater monitoring systems by highlighting the limitations of traditional cloud-based systems and challenges of underwater edge computing nodes.

title: FPGA-Based Real-Time Quality Analysis Framework for Photoplethysmogram Signals
authors: Praveen Kumar Yadav
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: This study presents the hardware implementation and performance assessment of a unified System-on-Chip (SoC) framework for modular digital signal processing (DSP) on an FPGA. Ten high-level synthesis (HLS)-based IP cores are included in the framework, which is aimed at biomedical applications, specifically photoplethysmogram (PPG) signal analysis, on a Xilinx Zynq-7000 SoC. The developed IP library comprises modules for mean removal, first-order derivative filtering, zerophase IIR filtering, amplitude normalization, and feature extraction. Evaluation was performed on 1,340 PPG segments (670 clean and 670 noisy) from the BIDMC, SLP, PulseDB and Wrist PPG databases using four processing pipelines: time-domain, derivative-based, FFT-based, and STFT-based. A menu-driven UART-based SDK interface enables performance benchmarking, cycle-accurate monitoring, and seamless hardware-software interaction. To guaranty improved performance, each IP core is developed analytically, implemented with Vivado HLS, and verified against MATLAB reference output. Using derivativebased skewness characteristics, a thorough investigation of signal quality analysis provides up to 99% accuracy in classifying clean and noisy signals. The synthesis result at 100 MHz shows that the compute-intensive spectral modules offer detailed time-frequency characterization, with a total SoC power of less than 2 W and each IP less than 160 mW. The light modules (mean removal and derivative filtering) have latencies as low as 100–125 cycles. Design optimizations such as memory partitioning, function inlining, and loop pipelining further improve throughput and resource efficiency. The suggested SoC framework provides a hardware base that is real-time, reusable, and power-efficient for biomedical signal analytics. Bridges the gap between algorithm creation and embedded implementation. The framework enables physiological monitoring, edge-level health analytics, and robust PPG quality assessment on the device.

title: Scenario-Based Financial Evaluation of Battery Sizing Strategies for DC Fast Charging Sites
authors: Muhammad Ashar Ayaz, M A Farhan, Abhishek Mandaliya, Anshi Rai, Vidya Sagar, Balu Jegganathan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: TThe growing demand for Electric Vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure, driven by the electrification of transportation, has prompted increased interest in battery energy storage systems (BESS) to manage high-power loads and mitigate grid stress during peak hours. While BESS integrated with DC Fast Charging (DCFC) offers technical benefits, its financial feasibility remains highly dependent on contextual factors. This paper presents a scenario-based financial assessment of BESS across 48 configurations under three distinct electric utility providers, each characterized by unique peak hour durations and pricing structures. Using key financial indicators including Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), and Payback Period (PBP), the study evaluates economic performance across varying levels of battery support. The paper concludes by identifying the critical factors necessary to achieve a positive NPV, including extended peak durations, favorable electricity pricing, and optimal battery sizing aligned with site-specific load demand.

title: Revolutionizing Image Processing with Quantum Algorithms: Encoding, Enhancement and Recognition
authors: Shivani Sood, Ashima Kalra, Gurjeet Singh
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Quantum image processing uses quantum computing, which implements the concepts of entanglement and superposition to increase efficiency, scalability, and revolutionary promise in demanding image processing ap plications. In this research paper, a comprehensive frame work for quantum image processing (QIP) is presented, which includes innovative quantum algorithms for pattern recognition, image improvement, and representation. The system presents advanced quantum encoding models for effective image data manipulation and storage. In addition, it discusses the quantum image-based algorithms that give better results than classical techniques in terms of speed and accuracy for image enhancement tasks such as edge recognition, contrast adjustment, and noise reduction. Researchers have developed quantum-based feature ex traction and classification methods for pattern recognition, which demonstrate enhanced scalability and accuracy for complex visual data. It has various applications in satellite data processing, secure visual data transmission, and medical imaging highlighted by simulations and theoretical analysis that confirm the proposed approaches.

title: PEB-Net: Deep Local Patch Enhancement for Low-Light Image Restoration
authors: Arti Jain, Pradeep Singh
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Low-light conditions significantly degrade the visual quality of images, leading to loss of details, poor contrast, and amplified noise, thereby affecting both human perception and the performance of automated vision systems. In this paper, we proposed a novel deep learning-based approach for low-light image enhancement using a Patch-wise Enhancement Network (PENet). The method first cuts the input low-light image into fixed-size (64×64×3) patches, and then processes these patches through a deep hierarchical architecture, which consists of multiple convolutional layers and Patch Enhancement Blocks (PEBs). Each PEB consists of two pointwise convolutions, depthwise convolutions, residual connections, layer normalization, and an efficient multilayer perceptron (MLP) with feature recalibration and non-linear transformation. Such convolution-PEB stages are repeatedly applied 19 times over the network, enabling the network to incrementally refine local features while remaining consistent globally. Finally, the reconstructed enhanced patches are seamlessly spliced to get the final enhanced image. Comprehensive experiments have been performed and shown the proposed method to make the illumination enhanced, hidden detail recovered, and noise reduced, which largely outperformed other current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in terms of both visual quality and quantitative metrics.
