title: Real-Time IoT and Machine Learning Framework for Fault Diagnosis and Monitoring in Solar Power Systems
authors: K. Ramesh, P. Raju, M.V.S.S. Sasank, G. Venu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: : The increasing adoption of solar power highlights the need for reliable fault diagnosis and monitoring systems to ensure optimal performance and safety. This study presents an IoT enabled machine learning framework for real-time fault detection and monitoring of solar plants. The framework is implemented on a real-time solar plant with the following specifications: 300 W solar panels $\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{oc}}: 40 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{sc}}: 8 \mathrm{~A}\right), 5 \mathrm{~kW}$ inverters, 48 V 200 Ah battery storage, 60 A charge controllers, and 4 kW mixed AC/DC loads. Fault scenarios, including ground faults, short circuits, and panel degradation, are simulated, with sensor data collected in real time using strategically deployed voltage, current, temperature, irradiance, and vibration sensors. The data is processed into a comprehensive dataset for machine learning analysis. Algorithms such as Random Forest, Support Vector Machines, and Neural Networks are evaluated, with model performance validated using metrics like accuracy, precision, and F1score. IoT integration facilitates real-time alerts to mobile devices, ensuring timely fault responses. The proposed framework achieves high fault classification accuracy and enhances operational safety by minimizing downtime. Its scalability makes it adaptable to various solar plant configurations, offering a robust solution for renewable energy management. This study underscores the potential of combining IoT and machine learning to optimize solar plant reliability and efficiency while enabling advancements in predictive maintenance.

title: Implementation of Custom VLSI Chips to Accelerate Edge AI for Real-Time Audio and Video Processing Applications
authors: A Hency Juliet, Karthiga. R, Maram Y. Al-Safarini, Bhukya Balakrishna, M. Dinesh, Peer Mohamed A
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: The increasing requirements of real-time audio and video processing at the edge of the network, which include but are not limited to, surveillance, autonomous, wearable electronics and smart cities, have demanded low-latency and high-fidelity computing platforms. "Existing AI systems based on the cloud are simply not enough by these standard sat, they have latency, pose privacy issues, and do not serve real-time needs. In this work, we consider custom Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) chips for fast Edge AI processing, where we target real-time audio and video workloads. Specialized VLSI accelerators are developed with application-specific organization tailored for deep neural networks (DNNs), convolutional neural networks (CNNs), recurrent models and transformer-based processing. The VLSI system is optimized in terms of high-throughput in parallel data pipelines, energy efficiency, as well as domain-specific accelerators for speech recognition, face detection, object tracking, and noise removal. Optimization at the hardware level, such as the application of fixed-point arithmetic, redesign of memory hierarchy and custom instruction set development, are included to allow a larger performance advantage over a generic processor or a GPU. The system is designed and implemented, in a design flow, which consists of HW/SW codesigning, synthesis, simulation and silicon layout optimization using commercial EDA tools. Benchmarks with live time-critical tasks such as live video processing for surveillance, on-line speech command interpretation and online multi-object tracking in changing environments are considered to allow assessment of the chip architecture. Results show up to 10× improvement in inference latency and a reduction of up to 65% power consumption with respect to traditional CPU/GPU-based solutions on the same workloads. Additionally, the study presents a modular chiplet the architecture and scalability and application specific partitioning for edge AI systems that have a mix of inputs. The proposed architecture also adds adaptive workload scheduling and dynamic voltage-frequency scaling (DVFS) scheme to boost performance in different working modes. Hardware-integrated security primitives safeguard data authenticity and defend AI models against untrusted parties looking to alter the model. This work lays the groundwork toward the development of hardware-optimized edge AI systems for the latency-, power-, and scalability-restricted realm of real-time audio-video processing. As a result, custom VLSI implementation enables performance improvement and provides a way toward embedding AI into lightweight, and energy-constrained-edge devices. The findings are a significant step toward making pervasive, general-purpose computing more of a reality in settings where speed, precision and dependability are essential, Kitchin says.

title: Design and Analysis of Novel Hybrid Coil for Large Horizontal and Vertical Misallignment-Tolerant
authors: Bibek Kumar, Avik Bhattacharya
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: Inductive Power Transfer (IPT) is gaining popularity as an EV charging application. The main constituent of IPT is the Coupling Coil (CC) for determining the power transfer efficiency and handling tolerance to misalignment. The lateral and vertical misalignment is inevitable in IPT charging, as different types of vehicles have different ground clearances. The conventional circular and square coil is not able to handle both types of misalignments, i.e. Lateral and vertical misalignment. This paper proposes a novel Hybrid coil that combines circular and square coils’ characteristics. A comparative study has been carried out among three coils: Hybrid, circular and square coils. The result shows that the proposed Hybrid coil has a better coupling coefficient than the conventional circular and square coils under both lateral and vertical misalignment conditions. The electromagnetic performance of the coils is evaluated using the finite element method (FEM) based simulations in ANSYS, focusing on self-inductance and mutual inductance characteristics. The results confirm that the hybrid coil design improves misalignment tolerance and provides a practical solution for efficient and reliable wireless charging of EVs.

title: Hybrid PSO-ACO Optimized CNN: A Novel Approach for Pneumonia Detection
authors: Harshit Sharma, Simran Kaur, Harish Sharma, Harshada Chandel
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Pneumonia detection is a critical task in medical imaging that involves accurately classifying chest X-ray images to diagnose the presence of pneumonia. Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have demonstrated remarkable success in this domain by automatically extracting and learning relevant features from X-ray images, making them highly effective for medical diagnosis. However, optimizing CNN architectures and identifying ideal hyperparameters remains a complex and challenging process that requires extensive tuning and evaluation. This study proposes a novel approach that integrates Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) with CNNs to enhance image classification performance. By leveraging PSO’s global exploration capabilities and ACO’s local exploitation strengths, the proposed PSO-ACO framework effectively balances the search process within the hyperparameter space. The seamless integration of these metaheuristic algorithms into CNN architecture optimization results in significant improvements in classification accuracy and a reduction in training time. Beyond enhancing CNN efficiency, this hybrid strategy contributes to improved diagnostic accuracy while mitigating time constraints in clinical settings.

title: Federated Learning: A Local Model Weight Averaging Approach for Smart Home Load Forecasting
authors: Vikash Kumar Saini, Ameena S. Al-Sumaiti, Rajesh Kumar, Surender Hans
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: Increasing adoption of smart home technologies means large amounts of energy consumption data are stored, highlighting the need for effective load forecasting methods for home energy management. Conventional forecasting approaches typically treat the forecasting task separately from the decisionmaking process, leading to disproportionate economic penalties due to load deviations. To overcome these limitations, this study employs a local model averaging approach through federated learning, utilizing an LSTM model to develop the federated learning framework. The numerical results demonstrate that the federated learning model outperforms local-level learning models in terms of forecasting accuracy for the given load dataset. The MSE for individual houses from H-1 to H-8 using the federated learning process is consistently better than those achieved with local-level learning, with improvements of 39.106%, 11.940%, 28.723%, 30.523%, 31.088%, 49.446%, 29.315%, and 29.226%, respectively.

title: A Linear Mixed Integer Optimization Problem Formulation for Siting, Sizing and Charging Schedule of Battery Energy Storage System in Active Distribution Networks
authors: Deepjyoti Saha, Sanjib Ganguly
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: This work presents an approach of finding the optimal number, location and size of battery energy storage system (BESS) in an active distribution network in order to maximize distribution system overall profit and minimize payback period of BESS installation and operation cost. The optimal number, site and size of the BESS are obtained by maximizing profit function which includes battery investment expenditure. A 33-bus power distribution network is used to study the adopted methodology. The optimization problem is solved using Mixed integer linear programming. After allocation and scheduling of BESS in the power distribution network the profit of distribution company is found to increase as compared to the case of distribution network without BESS. Payback period is also found to be considerably minimized which justifies viability of BESS installation.

title: Object Detection Using Deep Learning Algorithm in Aircrafts
authors: Munugapati Bhavana, N. Swapna, Sujatha Vadlamudi, Gagan Kumar Koduru, Algubelly Yashwanth Reddy, Koppula Srinivas Rao
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Object detection, a fundamental assignment in imaginative and prescient computer, pursuits to pick out and locate objects inside a photo or video. This project explores the latest application gadget modern day, especially deep today's techniques, to gain correct and efficient Object detection. By way of leveraging powerful convolutional neural networks (CNNs), we educate fashions to recognize diverse object classes and generate bounding boxes around them. The challenge investigates various state-of-the-art object detection architectures, together with quicker R-CNN, YOLO, and SSD, and evaluates their performance on widespread benchmark datasets. Furthermore, we discover techniques for enhancing detection accuracy, including statistics augmentation, switch cutting-edge, and model optimization. The developed system demonstrates promising outcomes in actual-international applications, inclusive of independent vehicles, surveillance systems, and picture analysis equipment.

title: Coverage and Reach Evaluation of Multi-Topology Mesh Networks for Emergency Communication
authors: Daniel Weber, Bertram Schütz
publish: 2025 IEEE World Forum on Public Safety Technology (WF-PST)
abstract: This paper investigates the feasibility and scalability of a multi-topology mesh network as a high potential public safety technology for emergency communication in disaster response and civil defense. Coverage and reach of such systems are evaluated using real-world location datasets of German households, schools, and public warning sirens. The impact of key parameters, such as walking distance and communication range are investigated. The results demonstrate the potential of this approach in providing off-grid capable access points in these public locations.

title: Towards Compute-Aware In-Switch Computing for LLMs Tensor-Parallelism on Multi-GPU Systems
authors: Chen Zhang, Qijun Zhang, Zhuoshan Zhou, Yijia Diao, Haibo Wang, Zhe Zhou, Zhipeng Tu, Zhiyao Li, Guangyu Sun, Zhuoran Song, Zhigang Ji, Jingwen Leng, Minyi Guo
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: Tensor parallelism (TP) in large-scale LLM inference and training introduces frequent collective operations that dominate inter-GPU communication. While in-switch computing, exemplified by NVLink SHARP (NVLS), accelerates collective operations by reducing redundant data transfer, its communication-centric design philosophy introduces the mismatch between its communication mode and the memory semantic requirement of LLM's computation kernel. Such a mismatch isolates the compute and communication phases, resulting in underutilized resources and limited overlap in multi-GPU systems. To address the limitation, we propose CAIS, the first ComputeAware In-Switch computing framework that aligns communication modes with computation's memory semantics requirement. CAIS consists of three integral techniques: (1) compute-aware ISA and microarchitecture extension to enable compute-aware in-switch computing. (2) merging-aware TB (Thread Block) coordination to improve the temporal alignment for efficient request merging. (3) graph-level dataflow optimizer to achieve a tight cross-kernel overlap. Evaluations on LLM workloads show that CAIS achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.38 \times$</tex> average end-to-end training speedup over the SOTA NVLS-enabled solution, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.61 \times$</tex> over T3, the SOTA compute-communicate overlap solutions but do not leverage NVLS, demonstrating its effectiveness in accelerating TP on multi-GPU systems.

title: Stock Market Sentiment Analysis Leveraging Transformer-Based Deep Learning Models for Financial News Interpretation
authors: Vandranki Jatin, Harsh Bardhan Singh, Kasam Vivek Reddy, U. Hariharan
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: In this paper we investigate the performance of transformer-based models, in particular FinBERT, for sentiment analysis task of financial headlines. Even though transformers have been doing very well on general-purpose NLP tasks, getting them to empathize with finance - often subtle, context-rich, and conservative - remains tricky. To see how well FinBERT is optimized for the field of finance, I compare it to standard machine learning classifiers such as Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine, and Random Forest and some CNN-based deep learning models.we had some experiments using the StockEmo dataset, with the addition of more finance-headline corpora. We tested the models using the standard metrics of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1. The results indicate that traditional classifiers and CNNs are able to surpass FinBERT in some instances, which indicates the possibility of over-fitting and lack of generalization in the domain of fine-tuning. We also discuss the challenge of such model interpretation and how the sentiment of finance is subject to change over time, suggesting a two angle approach combining model transparency with sensitivity to time in the markets.These results help us to select the right models and actually implement the sentiment analysis engine in finance projects.

title: Secure Log Ingestion and Visualization Using Dockerized Graylog with Multiple Log Shippers
authors: Sushma V, Sai Amarnath G, Chinmay J Patil, Swetha P
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: Graylog is an open-source log management platform that enables real-time collection, indexing, and visualization of machine data across distributed systems. A secure, scalable, and containerized logging system is built using Graylog, deployed in a multi-agent architecture. Logs are collected from three Windows host machines using Fluent Bit, NXLog, Winlogbeat, and Metricbeat to ensure comprehensive coverage of system, security, and application-level events. Docker is used to containerize the full stack–including Graylog, Elasticsearch, and MongoDB–for portability and simplified deployment. Transport Layer Security (TLS) is implemented with OpenSSL to establish encrypted communication between log shippers and the Graylog server, ensuring data confidentiality and integrity. Elasticsearch serves as the backend datastore for fast indexing and full-text search, while MongoDB handles configuration and metadata storage. Log collection techniques include event-based forwarding, agent-based harvesting, and stream-based routing of critical Windows event logs. Filtering is applied using Graylog streams and pipeline rules based on event IDs and log types, such as failed logins, system alerts, and user activity logs. Real-time alerting is enabled through Slack integration to notify high-priority events like repeated login failures. Experimental evaluation demonstrates secure and reliable ingestion, parsing, filtering, and visualization of logs from heterogeneous sources with low latency. The final setup offers a robust, production-ready solution for centralized log monitoring, supporting security compliance, operational visibility, and scalable deployment in development and pre-production environments.

title: Solar Integration for Stability of Railway Traction Power: A Vehicle Dynamics Approach
authors: Mustafa Kabak, Ali Jamali, Bahram Bolandparvaz, Nasrin Aslshahrivar, Reyhaneh Hesami, Reza N Jazar, Hamid Khayyam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Railway systems are among the largest electricity consumers, with peak demand creating significant challenges for grid stability and long-term sustainability. This study presents a vehicle dynamic-driven framework to forecast and optimise traction power demand on a metropolitan railway line in Australia incorporating solar integration as a stabilisation strategy. A detailed mathematical model is developed to capture the interaction between traction power requirements and roofmounted photovoltaic (PV) generation under real operating conditions. Comparative evaluations of grid-only and grid-solar scenarios demonstrate that PV integration effectively reduces peak demand, mitigates voltage fluctuations, lowers operational costs, and advances carbon reduction objectives. The findings underscore the transformative role of renewable energy integration in enhancing both the operational efficiency and grid reliability of next-generation railway systems.

title: Bridging Visual and Auditory Worlds: Comparing ViT and TTS in Assistive Tech
authors: Shri Varshini D R, Rajakumari R
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Assistive technologies increasingly rely on artificial intelligence to help individuals with visual impairments access both their surroundings and digital information. This work compares two key AI components-Vision Transformers (ViTs) and Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems-to highlight how they address different aspects of accessibility. ViTs interpret visual scenes, enabling functions such as object recognition, environmental awareness, and image-to-text description. TTS systems, in contrast, convert written content into natural speech, offering an auditory pathway to digital information. By examining their capabilities, limitations, and complementary roles, this paper outlines how integrating ViT-based perception with TTS-based communication can create more responsive and intuitive assistive solutions. The comparison underscores the potential of combining visual understanding with spoken output to enhance independence and user experience for people with vision impairments.

title: A Robust Control Approach for Mitigating Disturbances in Grid-Interfaced Power Inverters
authors: Shyamal S. Chand, Branislav Hredzak, Majid Farhangi, Arman Ali, Maurizio Cirrincione
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: The stability and dynamic behavior of inverterbased resources are significantly influenced by uncertain grid operating conditions. In weak grid environments, faults can induce extended oscillations in the currents injected at the point of common coupling, thereby compromising the stability of the DC link voltage. This paper evaluates a robust adaptive control scheme that operates in conjunction with current loop proportional-integral controllers to generate a compensating voltage, ensuring dependable performance of voltage-oriented controlled inverters under various grid dynamic scenarios. The adaptive compensator is formulated on the basis of the deadbeat control principle, utilizing grid impedance data to compute the feedforward voltage in real time. Comparative analyses demonstrate the approach's effectiveness in damping oscillations and minimizing post-fault recovery time for DC link voltage, as well as active and reactive power, in low short-circuit ratio grids subjected to voltage imbalance, asymmetric grid faults, and loss of synchronization by employing comprehensive simulation models and real-time experimentation.

title: A Data-Driven Framework for Phased User Migration in Enterprise System Modernization
authors: Aparna Krishna Penumatcha, Isaac Duraisingh
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: The process to modernize live enterprise systems requires vital operation to move active users between systems while ensuring operational continuity and ensuring all data is protected. The study shows that phased migration strategies are more successful than big bang strategies but existing research does not provide enough direction on effective user segmentation for various migration phases. The proposed study develops a databased user segmentation system which integrates four essential elements which include feature usage patterns and system complexity levels and data quantity and organizational risk assessment. The system incorporates a system for scoring users based on multiple criteria to advance users between migration stages using threshold values. The simulation with 500 synthetic enterprise users indicates that the complexity of integration has an impact on migration risk but weighting by revenue decreases business disruption. The research shows that thirteen percent of users require early phase migration but seventy three percent require standard migration phases and fourteen percent require special treatment due to their high risk status. The proposed solution links the technical migration models to organizational decision making using a solution that generates measurable and traceable and automated user segmentation for enterprise system modernization.

title: Enhancing the Hydrogen Energy's Decarbonization Value: A Hydrogen Credit Market Mechanism Design
authors: Jia Wang, Yingbiao Xie, Shunan Yao, Siyu Zhang, Yulin Yan, Ji Zhang, Hui Hou
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: As a pivotal pillar for low-carbon transition, hydrogen energy requires not only technological breakthroughs but also robust support from market mechanisms for its development. A hydrogen credit (HC) market mechanism is proposed to enhance hydrogen energy's decarbonization value. First, an HC certification system is established based on equivalent carbon reduction accounting, integrated within an offset-settlement market framework. The offset mechanism enables HC conversion into carbon allowance, expanding compliance pathways in carbon markets. Concurrently, the settlement mechanism allows regulatory authorities to subsidize surplus HC, monetizing their environmental benefits. Second, leveraging mobile hydrogen energy storage as an HC value conversion hub, a port integrated energy system (PIES) optimization model is developed by coupling energy and transportation flows. Finally, case study simulations conducted at a port in Foshan, China, demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed model in reducing total system costs.

title: AtmosFlux-NetX: A Multimodal Spatio-Temporal Fusion Model for Ultra-Fine Air Pollution Reconstruction
authors: Kalaivani C T, A. Hemamalini, Archana T, Radhakrishnan. T, Krishna. M
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Proper reconstruction of air quality is still a difficult task because of the high sparseness of sensors and the non-linear pollutant movements as well as the dynamism of environmental factors. This work presents a proposal of GreenSphere-GAN, a two-domain adversarial model that is built to produce ultra-fine spatial-temporal air quality maps and at the same time detects sources of pollution. The approach incorporates four new algorithms, including a hyper-spatial encoder, which captures micro-scale interactions of pollutants, a temporal-flux predictor, which captures dynamic propagation patterns, a cross-domain fusion attention module which integrates meteorological data with emission data and a residual adversarial refinement network which makes the reconstruction more real-world. All these elements enable GreenSphere-GAN to determine with high accuracy the concentrations of pollutants and pinpoint the likely emission hotspots. Experimental assessment proves that the framework has a considerable enhancement of reconstruction precision and the reliability of localization of the source. The results demonstrate that GreenSphere-GAN is a robust instrument in environmental control because it will allow implementing more efficient policymaking and actively controlling urban pollution.

title: Operator Splitting Scheme Based Distributed Energy Management for Networked Multi-Microgrids
authors: Zuqing Zheng, Zhao Yang Dong, Guo Chen, Yuechuan Tao, Enbing Su, Shuying Lai, Zuliang Huang, Tong Li, Xuegang Jiang
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Efficient and coordinated management of networked multi-microgrid systems is crucial, especially in background of energy structure transition and wide deployment of distributed renewable energy sources. To address the energy management challenges of these systems, this paper proposes a novel distributed framework. The framework requires only communication between neighboring microgrids to realize local computation, which effectively maintains the autonomous decision-making capability of each microgrid. By abandoning centralized data aggregation, the approach guarantees data privacy and local operational decision independence. A rigorous derivation constructed based on operator splitting theory proves that the distributed framework can converge to the optimal solution of the problem when the local step-size condition is satisfied. Simulation results further verify the convergence and effectiveness of the framework.

title: A Case Study for Vulnerable Road Users Protection: Systematic Approach for Managing and Scheduling C-ITS Workloads in Heterogeneous Clusters
authors: Berkay Yaman, Yavuz Selim Bostanci, Ozay Tokgozlu, Mujdat Soyturk
publish: 2025 IEEE World Forum on Public Safety Technology (WF-PST)
abstract: Safety-critical C-ITS applications—such as Vulnerable Road User (VRU) protection—require low latency, as mandated by international standards. Current communication infrastructures often fail to meet these stringent requirements due to end-to-end cumulative delays in data collection, cloud-based decision-making, and response delivery. While edge computing reduces latency, achieving predictable performance across the edge–cloud continuum requires intelligent workload orchestration that dynamically adapts to system conditions. This paper introduces a network-aware orchestration system that replaces the native scheduler of a container orchestration platform to enable latency- and energy-sensitive workload placement in heterogeneous clusters, considering processing and communication metrics. A Decision Assistance Service (DA Service) is integrated, which leverages empirical delay and energy profiles along with real-time network metrics exported from cluster nodes. The scheduling problem is modeled as a binary integer linear program that dynamically balances latency-sensitive and energy-efficient objectives through tunable trade-offs. The proposed approaches are validated through real-world deployments at multiple intersections equipped with IP cameras and DSRC-based roadside units. Real-world experimental results across three scenarios show that our system consistently meets strict latency constraints while optimizing energy consumption. These findings highlight the critical importance of integrating real-time network and device metrics into orchestration logic to ensure responsiveness and predictability in distributed C-ITS environments.

authors: Feifei Qiu, Bing Chen, Zhiwen Gao, Dongchuan Peng, Junjun Jin, Chunlang Luo, Jiakai Chen, Kejun Jiang, Guoqing Gou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement

title:
Oscillation Experiments at the VENUS-F Zero Power Fast Reactor: Measurement of a B
                    <sub>4</sub>
                    C Reference Sample
authors: Federico Di Croce, Federico Grimaldi, Antonin Krása, Pierre-Etienne Labeau, Chris Van Grieken, Niels Deboel, Marnix Moons, Guido Vittiglio, Jan Wagemans
abstract: VENUS-F is the zero-power fast spectrum reactor operated at SCK CEN. The facility offers a rare opportunity for nuclear data validation in a fast neutron spectrum. Neutronic experiments to support the design of heavy-metal-cooled fast reactors are conducted. In particular, the oscillation technique for sample reactivity worth measurements has recently been implemented. This work shows the results of the first oscillation experiments at VENUS-F, conducted with a B<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub>C reference sample. We demonstrate the feasibility of oscillation experiments in VENUS-F, describe their implementation, and assess robustness of this measurement technique, showing the repeatability of results across nine experiments. The results consistency across the oscillation cycles and detectors shows the reliability of the experimental and processing methods, achieving an uncertainty below 1% for a reactivity effect of approximately 6 pcm. Repeated experiments allow us to assess the impact of the oscillation pattern and related parameters on the precision of the results and the change in performance of two processing techniques: one based on averaging the Inverse Point Kinetics reconstructed reactivity and the other relying on Fourier Transform and the Zero Power Transfer Function. The agreement between repeated experiments and the comparable performance of the processing methods demonstrate the potential of the experimental method and build confidence in the results, supporting future oscillation experimental campaigns at VENUS-F.

title: EMND-SS: Equivalent Magnetic Node-Driven Sparse Surveying for Reliable Magnetic Mapping in Unknown Environments
authors: Gongxu Liu, Maosheng Wang, Long Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The construction of traditional magnetic map is time-consuming and labor-intensive, and lacks guidance for lightweight magnetic surveying theory, resulting in unreliable magnetic maps reconstruction based on sparse surveying in unknown areas. To address this issue, this paper introduces an equivalent magnetic node-driven sparse surveying (EMND-SS) technique for reliable magnetic mapping. An integrated model is established to characterize the fluctuation, topology, quality, and quantity of equivalent magnetic nodes. Based on local magnetic field variations, the effective horizontal and vertical boundaries of the nodes are determined, enabling adaptive adjustment of the aircraft’s speed and cross-track surveying spacing. Under the requirement of low-complexity trajectory design, a quasi-spiral trajectory is adopted to perform non-uniform sparse surveying from outer to inner regions. This approach ensures the acquisition of high-quality, well-topologized magnetic nodes under constrained and sparse surveying conditions. Extensive results from multiple datasets and multimodal experiments show that the proposed EMND-SS technique is insensitive to both the sparsity of magnetic nodes and the choice of reconstruction methods. It achieves reliable reconstruction results, with both the magnetic feature accuracy and the position stamp accuracy reaching over 93 % of their respective ideal upper bounds, while the precision of reconstructed magnetic features exceeds 0.15 nT.

title: A Hybrid-Policy MADQN-Based Resource Allocation Solution for Power-Domain NOMA-Enhanced RAN Slicing
authors: Yuanyuan Sun, Zhenjiang Shi, Jiadai Wang, Jiajia Liu
abstract: Although both service capacity (i.e., the number of users whose quality of service requirements are satisfied) and security (as impacted by resource isolation) are critical for radio access network (RAN) slicing, there often exists a trade-off between them. Enhancing security by reducing spectrum sharing among slices across different types inevitably compromises the service capacity. Moreover, traditional orthogonal resource utilization significantly limits service capacity, making it difficult to accommodate the rapid growth in the number of diverse users. To address this, we propose a non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA)-enhanced RAN slicing scheme by introducing power-domain NOMA. We then focus on the subchannel set optimization problem in a multi-base-station uplink RAN slicing system. To cope with the network dynamics caused by user mobility and stochastic packet arrivals, we design a hybrid-policy multi-agent deep Q-network-based solution. Numerical results show that the proposed scheme achieves good performance, significantly enhancing service capacity while ensuring security of slices.

title: SPECTRA: Secure Framework for Adaptive Frequency Hopping and Decentralized Storage
authors: Qiang Zhi, Yixin Liu, Xiaoting Hu, Yi Zhu
abstract: In highly dynamic and interference-prone environments, secure access to wireless communication spectrum and protection of user privacy present significant challenges. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel framework called the Secure and Privacy-Enhanced Channel Transmission Architecture (SPECTRA). SPECTRA enables fine-grained access control policies defined by a trusted authority and enforces them through distributed Semi-Trusted Distributed Servers (STDSs) using Attribute-Based Encryption and Zero-Knowledge Proofs. This design allows for dynamic spectrum authorization without revealing the requester's sensitive attributes. Furthermore, the frequency-hopping process is modeled as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), and optimized through Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to adaptively enhance communication reliability and resistance to interference. SPECTRA also supports both secure replacement and secure incremental update mechanisms, incorporating hash aggregation and elliptic curve signature-based batch verification to improve the efficiency and security of multi-request authentication. Extensive simulations demonstrate that SPECTRA achieves high-accuracy access control, effective communication privacy preservation, and stable frequency-hopping performance under adversarial and uncertain conditions.

title: Mind Your Prompt: How Tone Affects LLM Consistency and Safety
authors: Mohamad Kassab
abstract: Politeness affects performance–even in AI. When interacting with language models, the tone of a prompt can alter how much they explain, how positively they respond, and how helpful they seem. For reliability-focused teams, prompt phrasing is not cosmetic; it is a practical lever for consistency and clarity.

title: Asymmetric KV Cache Compression using State-Aware Sparsity and Quantization
authors: Myeongji Yun, Dongyun Kam, Priyadarshini Panda, Youngjoo Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Artificial Intelligence
abstract:
To enable the efficient deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) on resource-constrained devices, recent studies have explored Key-Value (KV) Cache compression, such as quantization and sparsity-based methods. However, prior works suffer from limitations in both accuracy and memory efficiency, as they overlook the structural differences between Key and Value states and the interaction between sparsity-based compression and quantization. Moreover, previous works often underexplored the variability of generated token lengths across requests, leading to inconsistent performance.

To address these limitations, we present a new asymmetric, state-aware KV Cache compression pipeline. The proposed pipeline tailors quantization granularity, sparsity-based compression type, and sparsity level to each state guided by our analysis of methodological characteristics and state-specific structural properties. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the accuracy degradation and KV Cache footprint under compression are sensitive to the ordering of different operations, highlighting the need for an order-aware design. Building on our state- and order-specific insights, we assess the influence of individual channels before applying Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE), and define a new criterion for structured channel pruning, enabling pre-RoPE pruning to mitigate accuracy loss. We also incorporate parameter-reusing techniques and avoid extra decoding-time computation and memory overhead. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves superior accuracy-memory trade-offs and consistent memory savings across sequence lengths in both quantized and non-quantized settings.

title: Mani-NENet: Manifold Neuron Energy Network-enhancing Dysplastic Cells Perception for Lung Adenocarcinoma Subtypes Recognition
authors: Yusong Mao, Xiaoliang Xu, Jiayang Luo, Hao Cui, Furong Luo, Pan Huang, Peng Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Dysplastic cells are vital for diagnosing Subtypes of Lung Adenocarcinoma (SLA) but are difficult to classify due to high morphological similarity. Existing models often suffer from a feature coherency problem, failing to effectively align local dysplastic details with global subtype representations. To address this challenge, we propose Mani-NENet, a manifold neuron energy network-enhancing dysplastic cells perception built on three core innovations. First, we design a Dysplastic Perception Block (DPB) with a novel neuron energy function that prioritizes highly discriminative cell populations, inspired by the similarity between neuronal activation mechanisms and the diagnostic process. Second, we introduce a Differentiated Representation Learning Block (DRLB) in manifold space to amplify subtype-specific features and mitigate the challenge of high intersubtype similarity. Third, we propose a Key Patch Block (KPB) to enhance the extraction of low-level image features, capturing fine-grained morphological details of dysplastic cells. Extensive experiments on the AMU-SLA and AMU-BC datasets demonstrate that Mani-NENet outperforms other 19 state-of-the-art methods, achieving top-ranking average accuracies of 89.21% and 94.71%, respectively. The ablation results demonstrate that our classification performance improves by 1.91%, 2.42%, and 5.54% after applying DPB, DRLB, and KPB, respectively. In addition, we analyze the classification performance of each subclass and achieve accuracies of 94.64%, 96.82%, 97.49%, and 98.66% for LIA, AAH, MPIA, and PIA, respectively. Qualitative analysis shows accurate localization of dysplastic regions, demonstrating strong clinical interpretability and potential for human–machine collaboration.

title: Message from the Program Chairs: NISS 2025

title: Adaptive Segmentation of EEG for Machine Learning Applications
authors: Johnson Zhou, Joseph West, Krista A. Ehinger, Zhenming Ren, Sam E. John, David B. Grayden
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Objective. Electroencephalography (EEG) data is derived by sampling continuous neurological time series signals. In order to prepare EEG signals for machine learning, the signal must be divided into manageable segments. The current naive approach uses arbitrary fixed time slices, which may have limited biological relevance because brain states are not confined to fixed intervals. We investigate whether adaptive segmentation methods are beneficial for machine learning EEG analysis. Approach. We introduce a novel adaptive segmentation method, CTXSEG, that creates variable-length segments based on statistical differences in the EEG data and propose ways to use them with modern machine learning approaches that typically require fixed-length input. We assess CTXSEG using controllable synthetic data generated by our novel signal generator CTXGEN. We validate on a real-world use case by replacing fixed-length segmentation in the preprocessing step of a typical EEG machine learning pipeline for seizure detection, and offer guidance on how application may be extended to other problem domains. Main results. We found that using CTXSEG to prepare EEG data improves seizure detection performance compared to fixed-length approaches when evaluated using a standardized framework, without modifying the machine learning method, and requires fewer segments. Significance. This work demonstrates that adaptive segmentation with CTXSEG can be readily applied to modern machine learning approaches, with potential to improve performance. It is a promising alternative to fixed-length segmentation for signal preprocessing and should be considered as part of the standard preprocessing repertoire in EEG machine learning applications.

title: Pose-Free Radiance Reconstruction with Depth and Exposure Priors on a Cloud-Native Hybrid KAN Framework
authors: Zhuohao Gong, Xinyang Guo, Wenxin Hu, Yanjie Li, Weichu Liu, Xiping Hu
abstract: Training neural radiance fields without pre-estimated camera poses remains challenging, particularly in the presence of complex illumination and large inter-frame motion. This work presents a self-calibrating radiance-field framework designed to address these difficulties through a unified optimization strategy. Reliable monocular depth priors from the Depth Anything V2 model are incorporated to provide stable geometric constraints, and multiview point-cloud alignment is enforced to prevent pose drift under wide baselines. Illumination-induced photometric inconsistencies are handled by a jointly optimized exposure-compensation module that separates transient brightness variations from the underlying scene radiance. To enhance the representational capacity beyond that of standard multilayer perceptrons, a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network is adopted as the radiance-field backbone, enabling improved recovery of fine geometric and appearance details. The complete pipeline is deployed on a cloud-native infrastructure that supports scalable optimization and interactive access through an LLM-assisted interface. Experiments on indoor and outdoor datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves pose accuracy and novel-view synthesis quality over existing self-calibrating approaches.

title: Crowd Entropy-Based Prediction Model: Unidirectional Flow
authors: Alala Bahamid, Mahmoud Masoud, Azhar Mohd Ibrahim, Tanveer Saleh
abstract: The prediction of critical conditions during emergency evacuation is a key safety factor in crowd movement and urban transport management. Previous entropy-based models developed for evaluating crowd risk primarily relied on either density or velocity attributes, which alone are insufficient for danger prediction. Therefore, this paper proposes a crowd Boltzmann entropy-based prediction model that integrates the local density with average local speed to identify the critical situations that may result in crowd disasters, empowering sufficient prediction of crowd critical conditions and accurate description of the nature of a crowd motion, therefore enabling early preventative intervention. This proposed method is embedded into a neuro-symbolic evacuation model that has human-level capabilities of reasoning and performance to ensure realistic prediction on the nature of crowd motion. The results reveal that the longest critical conditions last in the nearest area to exit with the highest average entropy of 0.44, where the shortest is recorded in the farthest area to exit with the lowest average entropy of 0.29. This is consistent with previous literature on the crowd dynamics. Finally, the work demonstrates more universal and consistent descriptions of crowd psychology and motions and outperforms well-established prediction approaches such as crowd pressure and flow.

title: Learning Execution Plan Embeddings for Multi-Dimensional Query Resource Prediction
authors: Mahendran Vasagam, Ashok Kumar, Anshul Garg
abstract: Multi-cluster query engines are common in production, but routing queries to appropriately-sized clusters remains an unsolved problem. Current approaches like round-robin, hash-based routing, or manual rules waste resources when simple queries land on oversized clusters and cause failures when complex queries hit undersized ones. Production systems experience resource mismatch failures, requiring manual workarounds such as migrating problematic queries to separate engines. We address this with a system for intelligent cluster sizing using learned execution plan embeddings to predict query resource requirements. Instead of analyzing query text, we vectorize execution plan structure and search for similar historical queries, predicting memory, Central Processing Unit (CPU) and runtime based on actual past performance rather than unreliable optimizer estimates. We employ a tiered inference strategy that supports fast, feature-based vectors (10 basic dimensions; 13–23 ms overhead), gradient boosting with enhanced features (24 dimensions; 76.5% accuracy), and graph neural network embeddings for complex queries (78.1% accuracy). An adaptive classifier maps predictions to cluster sizes using thresholds that adjust based on observed utilization patterns. Unfamiliar queries are routed conservatively, and the system automatically improves from observed outcomes. Empirical validation on 2,954 queries from a Trino deployment shows 98.8–99.8% within-one-size accuracy and 74.8–76.5% exact-match routing. In benchmark workloads, our approach reduces out-of-memory failures by 82% and infrastructure costs by 33%, with less than 51 ms of routing overhead. Gradient boosting achieves 76.5% accuracy, while graph neural networks reach 78.1% for complex join structures.

title: Multi-Data Models Control of Grid-Connected Inverters for Enhanced Adaptive Compensation Capability
authors: Qi Zhang, Cong Li, Rongwu Zhu, Fujin Deng, Jiahao Zhang, Hui Yang, Xiangdong Sun
abstract: Data-driven regression compensation control effectively mitigates the inherent nonlinearities in grid-connected inverters. However, the structural limitations inherent to the single-data regression model challenge the balance between minimal data requirements and adaptive optimization, thereby constraining compensation precision and generalization capability. To overcome the generalization limitations of the single-data model, this paper proposes a collaborative compensation control strategy based on multi-data regression models, which employs adaptive weighting for dynamic model fusion. This method integrates techniques for operating interval and boundary feature extraction to construct multi-data regression models using lightweight offline regression. Real-time matching of operating features enables dynamic model selection and weight assignment, thereby accurately reconstructing the compensation signal in extrapolation regions beyond the training data boundaries. Furthermore, by integrating with a low-order controller, a hybrid control architecture merging the mechanism-based and data-driven paradigms is established, significantly enhancing the adaptive compensation performance. Experimental results validate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed strategy.

title: High Accuracy CaAlg-coated D-shaped Optical Fiber Humidity Sensor using Multi-peak Wavelength Shift Averaging Method
authors: Yuzhe Sun, Yunke Du, Zongrui Ma, Ruixin Liang, Rui Jin, Yang Yan, Yongfeng Mei, Dong Zhao, Chao Wang, Bo Jia
abstract: A calcium alginate (CaAlg)-coated D-shaped single mode fiber humidity sensor is proposed in this paper. The sensor is fabricated by grinding single mode fiber and wrapping CaAlg with rolling stress. The D-shaped optical fiber sensor utilizes outward cladding mode to enhance sensing sensitivity to external refractive index changes. To visualize the spectrum response of D-shaped optical fiber humidity sensor, the periodic resonance spectrum is generated by tapered fiber connected to a broadband light source. External humidity changes are detected by observing transmission spectrum wavelength shift. The multi-peak wavelength shift averaging method is used to improve the humidity measurement linear accuracy. Results show that the relative humidity (RH) sensitivity is 0.0269 nm/%RH in 10-99%RH range, with temperature insensitive. This study provides an economical method for achieving accurate humidity detection using relatively low resolution commercial optical spectrometers. With high humidity sensing sensitivity, measurement precision, and temperature insensitivity, the proposed sensor has potential applications in practical humidity measurement.
