title: Uncertainty-Aware Market Participation Models for Distributed Energy Resource Aggregators
authors: Chatum Sankalpa, Ghulam Mohy-ud-din, Erik Weyer, Maria Vrakopoulou
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: In the context of net-zero emission targets, distributed energy resource (DER) aggregators are positioned to play a growing role in electricity markets by monetizing their flexible assets through strategic market participation. However, their revenue is significantly affected by DER uncertainties. This paper presents three two-stage stochastic optimization methods: risk-neutral, robust, and chance-constrained, to model and mitigate the impact of DER uncertainties introduced by solar photovoltaic (PV) and loads for an aggregator participating in joint energy and regulation frequency control ancillary services (FCAS) markets in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). The solution approach includes linear recourse policies and scenario optimization to enhance tractability. The PV and load uncertainties are modeled using real historical data from Australian customers via a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method. The proposed methods are evaluated using observed data, demonstrating the potential of stochastic optimization in mitigating uncertainty and improving profitability for aggregators in the modern electricity markets.

title: Parameter Inversion Algorithm for Complex Systems Integrating Deep Physical Networks and Optoelectronic Information Sensing
authors: Xiatian Lin
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: The research background of this study is the need for parameter inversion in complex systems deeply coupled with deep physical networks and optoelectronic information sensing technologies. As a theoretical innovation, this study constructs a unified mathematical representation framework and proposes a Deep Physical-Optoelectronic Reinforcement Parameter Inversion (DPORPI) algorithm. First, this study abstracts the system as a multi-layered heterogeneous graph composed of physical nodes, optoelectronic nodes, heterogeneous links, security levels, and computing resources. This innovation can uniformly characterize the system's interconnected structure across scales, modalities, and topologies. Based on this, a Joint-PGCN (Physical-Optoelectronic Joint Graph Convolutional Network) is designed. Simultaneously, a dual-domain convolution operator and a physical coupling attention term are introduced to simultaneously extract multi-domain interconnected features. A cross-domain mapping path is constructed through a Service Function Chain (SFC) constraint mechanism, implementing structured constraints for parameter transfer under five conditions: node consistency, computing resources, optoelectronic bandwidth, security level, and physical-optoelectronic dual-domain consistency. Finally, this study constructs a reinforcement learning optimization module. This module can jointly model the inverted state space, policy actions, and reward function. This optimization enables the inversion path to maintain stable convergence in dynamic and complex environments. This work used a complicated system with multi-source sensor fusion to build a test set in the experimental portion. According to the findings, DPORPI has an inversion root mean square error of 0.029 at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SNR}=20 ~\text{dB}$</tex>, which is 61.3%, 50.0%, and 29.3% less than GCN (0.075), PINN (0.058), and Joint-PGCN (0.041), in that order. DPORPI maintains an RMSE of 0.062 under the low-quality observation condition of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SNR}=5$</tex> dB, which is 61.7% less than GCN (0.162). Furthermore, DPORPI outperforms Joint-PGCN (84.7%) and PINN (79.2%) in cross-scenario testing. This maintains an average inversion stability of almost 92.1% under the three types of the system structure modifications.

title: Strategic Planning of Battery and Hydrogen Storage Systems for Optimal Dispatch During Renewable Drought Conditions
authors: Md Akramul Alim, Ashish P. Agalgaonkar, Kashem M. Muttaqi, Ghulam Mohy-ud-din
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: As wind and solar generation become increasingly central to Australia's electricity grids, strategic planning of energy storage systems will be essential to ensure optimal dispatch and maintain reliability and economic efficiency particularly under conditions of renewable drought and dunkelflaute. This paper presents a novel heuristic data-driven-feedback-enhanced approach to determine the optimal sizing and siting of battery and hydrogen energy storage systems. The hydrogen units are designed to supplement curtailed variable renewable energy (VRE) generation, export energy under normal conditions, and serve as a strategic reserve during renewable droughts. Moreover, the proposed approach uses power flow equations to accurately model the operation of the power system across a broad set of deterministic scenarios. The proposed approach is implemented and validated on the IEEE RTS 24-bus system using the Australian National Electricity Market demand profiles. Simulation results confirm that the proposed approach reduces operational costs and minimizes the VRE curtailment using optimal sizing and siting of battery and hydrogen systems while analyzing the variations in renewable generation and loads. In addition, in VRE draught or dunkelflaute conditions, the hydrogen storage units support the grid, demonstrating their critical role as backup energy resources.

title: Harnessing Artificial Intelligence for Data Preprocessing to Improve Malware Detection Accuracy
authors: Bhagwant Singh, Sikander Singh Cheema
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: The swift expansion of dangerous software creates a substantial obstacle for cybersecurity infrastructures, demanding cutting-edge detection solutions. Classical approaches, which hinge on recognizing fixed patterns, are growing less capable of countering flexible and stealthy malware. Artificial intelligence, particularly leveraging machine learning and advanced neural networks, has proven to be an effective instrument for spotting emerging cyber dangers. The success of these intelligent tools relies heavily on thorough data preparation techniques implemented before model development. This investigation unveils a groundbreaking preprocessing method that taps into AI to boost the precision of malware identification. The strategy merges two essential features: automated extraction of characteristics from unprocessed malware code using autoencoder networks, and the production of artificial data through Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to correct uneven datasets. By reshaping raw malware into valuable feature collections and supplementing training datasets with computer-generated samples, this technique markedly improves detection capabilities, delivering F1-score increases of up to 8.5% when compared to standard practices. This research bolsters cybersecurity by delivering an all-encompassing preprocessing plan that reinforces AI-powered malware detection, establishing more robust safeguards against the ever-changing landscape of digital hazards.

title: Advanced Obstacle Detection for Improved Collision Avoidance in Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Sharmistha Dey, Kuldeep Chouhan
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: The increasing integration of Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) in modern transportation systems underscores the critical need for reliable obstacle detection to enhance collision avoidance. According to a global report, approximately 1.19 million fatalities occur annually due to road accidents, emphasizing the urgency of advanced safety mechanisms. This study proposes a novel classification-based obstacle detection approach for AVS, leveraging a hybrid model integrating fuzzy logic with deep learning to improve accuracy, robustness, and real-time performance. This work proposes a fuzzy inference system and a YOLO-based hybrid approach that utilizes fuzzy classification to detect objects. Experimental validation, conducted using the KITTI dataset and real-world image captures, demonstrates a Mean Average prediction accuracy of 90%, significantly improving obstacle classification and collision avoidance capabilities. A comprehensive comparative analysis with conventional object detection methods evaluates the proposed model’s efficiency, accuracy, and computational cost. The proposed approach outperforms traditional methods, offering a reliable and efficient solution for autonomous navigation.

title: Advancing Breast Cancer Detection Through ConvNeXt-Tiny and Attention-Based Deep Learning
authors: Gunjan Mehra, Mahesh Manchanda, Mohd Shuaib, Aditya Joshi, Amit Juyal, Manoj Diwakar
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Breast cancer remains one of the most important health issues that concern the world, where timely and accurate diagnosis is essential for enhancing survival. With recent advancement in deep learning, automated image analysis in medicine has become a potential strong ally in aiding clinical decision-making. In this article, we propose a breast cancer classification model that integrates the ConvNeXt-Tiny architecture with the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM). ConvNeXt serves as a powerful feature extractor, while CBAM enhances the emphasis of the model on most informative spatial and channel-wise features. In order to increase the generalization, the model was trained using data augmentation, class rebalancing, and learning strategies. The proposed ConvNeXt-Tiny + CBAM model achieved a classification accuracy of 98.8 surpassing several traditional CNN-based methods. The training and validation results also showed consistent convergence without overfitting. These findings highlight the potential of combining ConvNeXt and CBAM to develop reliable, accurate and clinically applicable breast cancer detection systems.

title: Optimal Operation Strategy for a Virtual Power Plant with Heterogeneous Distributed Energy Resources
authors: Nabin Babu Ojha, Ehsan Pashajavid, Arindam Ghosh, Ashish P. Agalgaonkar
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: This paper presents an optimisation model for the coordinated operation of a virtual power plant (VPP) that integrates a range of VPP-owned distributed energy resources (DERs), including wind turbines, photovoltaic systems, diesel generators, battery energy storage systems (BESSs), flexible loads, and behind-the-meter prosumers. A mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) framework is developed to determine optimal dispatch schedules and grid interactions under time-of-use (ToU) pricing and feed-in tariffs (FiT), with the objective of maximising VPP profit. The model is evaluated across three operational scenarios: (1) No VPP coordination, (2) VPP without BESS optimisation, and (3) VPP with BESS optimisation. The optimisation is implemented in Python using the Gurobi Optimiser's branch-and-cut algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate a substantial <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{5 6 \%}$</tex> increase in daily profit under the optimised VPP with BESS scenario, highlighting the economic advantages of coordinated DER utilisation and strategic grid exports. The proposed approach provides a practical decision-support tool for VPP operators, with potential for future enhancement to incorporate uncertainty in renewable energy generation and associated market conditions.

title: AI/IoT–Enabled Precision Hydroponics System for Optimal crop yield
authors: Arvind Kumar G, Parth Navale, Puneeth S L, Punith H M, Bharath Reddy K
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Hydroponics represents an innovative approach to farming, utilising water as a medium to deliver essential nutrients to plants. This method offers several advantages, particularly in urban settings where space is limited. Urban residents, often occupied with work, school, and other activities, can benefit from hydroponic systems implemented within their homes, efficiently using available space.This research work aims to enhance hydroponic systems by integrating Internet of Things (IoT) technology, leveraging the capabilities of the ESP32 microcontroller and the Blynk platform. Remote monitoring of key parameters such as water availability, temperature, and pH levels becomes essential for effectively managing hydroponic setups, enabling remote observation and reporting of these crucial factors for further decision-making, leveraging the need for minimal human interference. The utilisation of the ESP32 microcontroller, coupled with the Blynk platform, facilitates seamless data transmission and real-time monitoring, empowering users to make informed decisions and take appropriate actions irrespective of their location.

title: Speech Analysis for Parkinson’s Disease Detection: Exploring Acoustic Features and Machine Learning Approaches
authors: Rakshita Makkar, N. Deepa, Sridhar P Arjunan, Maneesha Singh, Prashant Jha
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Parkinson’s disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by motor impairments and non-motor symptoms, including cognitive and speech impairments. Among these, hypokinetic dysarthria—a speech disorder—remains underexplored. Speech disturbances in PD arise due to impaired vocal production caused by motor deficits, leading to altered acoustic features such as Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs), spectral, prosodic, and energy-related variations. These features reflect reduced vocal fold vibration, monotonicity, weakened phonation, and disrupted harmonic balance. Recent advances in machine learning enable an effective identification of PD through these speech features. Models such as Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest, and Logistic Regression classify PD speech, while Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) leverage raw spectrograms for automated detection. This approach holds promise for noninvasive, efficient screening and monitoring of PD, advancing our understanding of its impact on speech dynamics and aiding early diagnosis. Incorporating MFCCs helps capture the articulatory and spectral distortions caused by PD, making them valuable markers for distinguishing affected speech. These features, along with measures such as pitch variability and Harmonic-to-Noise Ratio (HNR), allow deeper insights into the vocal degradation associated with the disease. From the results, we see that Support vector machines (SVM), Random Forests,and Logistic regression have also shown promise when trained on the extracted features in acoustic forms.

title: Online Optimization of Resource Allocation in Computer-Aided Public Safety Dispatch Systems
authors: Andrew Vakhutinsky, Christophe Gerard
publish: 2025 IEEE World Forum on Public Safety Technology (WF-PST)
abstract: Efficient emergency response systems are essential for reducing response times and improving public safety outcomes. In this study, we present an approach to optimize the dispatch of emergency units based on the real-time incident data and agency-specific response plans with heterogeneous unit types. We propose a scalable online optimization approach that enables strategic, real-time assignment of units to current incidents while remaining adaptive to future, unpredictable demands. The effectiveness of the methodology is demonstrated using real-world data from emergency response agencies and validated through simulation.

title: Generalizable Deep Learning for Urban Air Pollution Forecasting: AirNet-Adapt with Cross-Domain Alignment on Beijing and UCI Air Quality Data
authors: Belin J Febina, Ancy Snowshima J, Abdur Rahman
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: The problem of urban air pollution prediction is still an urgent issue since cities become more exposed to environmental threats and human health problems. This paper presents a domain- adaptive deep learning framework, called AirNet-Adapt, that can improve generalization to a wide range of urban settings. AirNet-Adapt uses the concept of crossdomain alignment to combine spatial-temporal patterns of pollution with the use of the dataset on Beijing and sensor- based atmospheric measurements on the UCI Air Quality dataset. The model learns variations in the behaviour of pollutants in different cities using spatial feature extraction and adaptive representation learning. Through experimental analysis, it is proven that composite feature matching across multiple domains can enhance accuracy of prediction much higher than traditional single-city based forecasting models. AirNet-Adapt offers a scalable and robust system that can be used to forecast urban air pollution in a robust and generalizable way.

title: Machine Learning Based Seamless Solar-Grid Transition and Voltage Regulation Using Power Electronics in Industrial DC Grids
authors: Jency Joseph. J, Gugaan. SA, Bhoopathy Raj. M, Dhivya Bharath. K
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)

title: Real-Time Proctoring System for Online Examinations Using MediaPipe Framework
authors: Suresh Babu Dasari, Hrutik Timmala, Sai Vadaparthi, Laxminarayana Thungula
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: In the era of online education and remote recruitment, the integrity and security of exams and interviews conducted over the internet pose significant challenges. To address this, a Real-Time Proctoring System (RTPS) is essential to monitor and supervise online exams and interviews in real-time. This system leverages advanced technologies such as live video monitoring, screen recording, and facial recognition to ensure the authenticity of participants and detect any form of cheating or misconduct by using Mediapipe framework for facial recognition, PnP module for head pose estimation, SoundDevice library for detecting any background noises by observing sound amplitude thresholds. The RTPS provides a comprehensive solution for educational institutions, companies, and recruitment agencies to conduct online assessments with confidence and integrity. the key features and functionalities of the RTPS, highlights the importance in maintaining the credibility and validity of online exams and interviews.

title: Enhancing intrusion detection system security by metaheuristic-based feature selection and machine learning
authors: Kaushal Kumar Gupta, Mohd. Rehan Ghazi, D. R. Gangodkar
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: A Network Intrusion Detection System is very important for security of modern networks against developing cyber threats. The study explores the use of metaheuristic applications for the feature selection on UNSW-NB15 data set for improvement of IDS performance. Metaheuristic algorithms namely Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Genetic Algorithm (GA), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), Cuckoo Search (CS) and Fire Fly Algorithms (FA) are used to find out the most relevant features for reducing computational overheads, the selected features are then used to train Machine Learning Classifiers namely Random Forest (RF), Decision Tree (DT), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) and K Nearest Neighbor (KNN) for evaluation of accuracy, F1 Score, Specificity, False Positive and sensitivity. Experimental results demonstrate that PSO Performed best with RF classifier and achieved an accuracy of 99.02%. Cuckoo Search Algorithm performed best with DT achieving an accuracy of 97.60%. ACO performed best with XGBoost achieving an accuracy of 98.90% and again PSO performed best with KNN achieving an accuracy of 97.54%.

title: Arduino Uno Based LPG Gas Detector and Regulator Control System
authors: Shelej Khera, Aarti Chaudhary, Anshul Mahajan, Praveen Kumar Malik, Sakshi Chamoli, Gotte Ranjith Kumar
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: This paper describes an LPG gas alarm and auto shut-off prototype built around an Arduino Uno to make homes and factories safer. At its core sits an MQ-2 sensor that tracks gas levels nonstop; if leakage is detected, the system lights up warning LEDs, sounds a buzzer, and pivots a small servo that cuts off the regulator. By pairing constant monitoring with automatic action, the setup fills a key shortcoming in off-the-shelf detectors and sharply lowers the chance of explosion or poisoning. The Arduino acts as the brain, reading the sensor values in real time. While everything is normal, a green LED stays on and the servo keeps the valve open. If gas rises above a preset level, the red LED flashes, the buzzer blares, and the servo turns, shutting the line. A 16x2 LCD shows current gas concentration and lets users see at a glance whether the system is working correctly. The biggest leap in this design is its mix of a simple mechanical shut-off and tried-and-true detectors, giving users a one-stop safety net. Extensive trials with different gas levels showed it spots leaks in under five seconds and keeps the servo motor humming steadily. Head-to-head tests prove it outshines rival products thanks to faster, fully automatic response and a more integrated setup. On the ground, the system is friendly to budgets (parts add up to less than $25), sips power at 5 volts DC, and snaps into place with minimal tools. We see clear paths to kitchens, factories, and labs that handle LPG, far beyond the home setting. Down the road, adding Wi-Fi for remote checks and quick-learning software to cut false alarms are easy upgrades. Overall, the work shows that a modest microcontroller can yet close the dangerous gap between gas detection and instant shut-off.

title: Artificial Neural Network-Based Prediction of Soil Settlement
authors: Archit Priyadarshi, Muyassar Allaberganova, Anant Deogaonkar, Chandan Vichoray, Mohit Bisht, Rishabh Mandal
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: The estimation of sediment settlement on river basins is a challenging and poorly understood subject. Numerous formulae based on various theoretical or practical techniques have been included in the geotechnical literature to produce an exact or nearly accurate prediction of such settling. However, these approaches consistently fall short when it comes to precise settlement prediction. Current techniques for predicting settlements mostly concentrate on estimating the greatest amount of ground settlements by employing static influencing elements, such as soil properties. However, these methods cannot be used directly to forecast daily ground settlements since various time-dependent influencing factors can have an impact on daily ground settlements, and such factors should be taken into account for an accurate daily ground settlement prediction. This paper presents a daily ground settlement forecast approach based on artificial neural networks to address this issue. The optimal artificial neural network takes into account both time-dependent and static effect elements, as well as historical settlement monitoring data. Statistical metrics were employed to assess the various artificial neural networks that were built using the Gradient Descent and Levenberg-Marquardt training methods. The Levenberg-Marquardt training algorithm has produced more accurate results than the Gradient Descent training algorithm, according to statistical research.

title: MACV: A Specialized Multi-Agent and Consensus Framework for Reliable LLM Outputs
authors: Rakesh More, Sudarshan Varma, Nilay Varma
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) write smoothly but still sometimes make believable yet incorrect or unsupported claims so called hallucinations. To address this, we propose MACV (Multi-Agent Cross-Verification), a modular system that has several cooperating components; a Primary Response Generator (PRG) that drafts answers; a Fact Checking Agent (FCA) that checks claims against external sources like Wikipedia or PubMed; a Domain-Specific Validator (DSV) that inspects specialist content using structured datasets and ontologies; an Adversarial Tester (AT) that looks for contradictions and weak reasoning; and finally a Consensus Mechanism that combines agent outputs into a confidence-tagged final response. We explain the architecture, agent interfaces, consensus strategies, and how we evaluate the system. Tests on benchmarks—SQuAD, SciQ, and FinQA datasets show MACV meaningfully lowers hallucination rates compared with single-LLM and RAG baselines while adding modest compute overhead. We also examine failure modes, scalability trade-offs, and practical deployment issues, and conclude that heterogeneous, cross-checking agents are a promising path toward more reliable LLM behavior in realworld settings.

title: Smart Healthcare Assistant: Doctor Recommendation and Appointment Scheduling Using ML & NLP
authors: D. Balakrishnan, Bhogathi Pravallika, Bommana Devendra Reddy, Byreddy Sowmya, Cherukuri Girija Sai Siri, Umasree Mariappan
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: The growing demand for healthcare services to be delivered as fast and effortlessly as possible, the deployment of intelligent systems for patients to consult doctors at the right time is under consideration. This paper proposes an ML-NLP-based Smart Healthcare Assistant that provides recommendations of doctors and appointments based on individual needs. It provides for log-in, entry of symptoms, advice for simple diseases, and a sorted list of physicians based on their specialization, rating, fees, and timings. Later, it helps in booking appointments, online payment, and generating receipts easily. By integrating ML and NLP, the system will ensure an enhanced user experience with minimum waiting time and increased access to health care. The proposed strategy shows how AI-technologies can enable automation of patient-doctor interactions in such a way that personalized and efficient health care services become accessible.

title: Incorporating Intra-Interval Power Balance Behavior in Ex-Ante Market Dispatch and Pricing
authors: Yuan Zhang, Mingxu Xiang, Qingyue Ran, Zhifang Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: The increasing penetration of renewable energy challenges traditional market dispatch and pricing mechanisms, which often overlook real-time intra-interval power imbalances, leading to extra cost or unreasonable price signals. This study addresses this critical gap by developing a market dispatch and pricing model that explicitly incorporates intra-interval power balance behavior. The proposed dispatch model integrates intra-interval power balance constraints into the ex-ante market clearing process, ensuring that generation dispatch proactively accommodate real-time balancing needs. Furthermore, the pricing mechanism extends the traditional Locational Marginal Price (LMP) to include components associated with intra-interval balancing costs, thereby providing a more comprehensive reflection of real power balance. Case studies based on the IEEE 30-bus system with real-world data demonstrate that the proposed model reduces overall costs at least 0.91 % and the price signals exhibit distinct characteristics.

title: Advancing Earthquake Prediction Accuracy Through Machine Learning: A GBM-Based Study on Diagnostic Performance and Reliability
authors: Ragini Y P, Ola Khresat, B. Mamatha, Rakesh Chandrashekar
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: In the study of seismic data, earthquake prediction has proved to be an important issue because of the sophisticated, non-linear, character of the information. In this paper, we discuss the effectiveness of Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM), a strong ensemble learning algorithm, in classifying earthquake events over a well-organized seismic data set. We used stratified data partitions to train and validate our model, which would result in the balancing and confirm appropriate representation of classes. This analysis was done by using various performance measurements and illustrations in order to carry out an effective evaluation. The Confusion matrix indicates a high level of classification of the model, with true positive number of 358 and true negative number of 288, and false negative 34, and false positive 85. The combined accuracy is at 84 percent implying a balanced sensitivity on the model with regard to earthquake and non-earthquake cases. Also, the precision of non-earthquake and earthquake are 0.89 and 0.81 respectively in the classification report whereas recall is high in earthquake cases (0.91) which means high sensitivity and less chance of misses. The high values of the F1-score (0.83 and 0.86, respectively) indicate overall high balance between the precision and recall in classes 0 and 1. The Discriminative strength of the model is further supported by Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve which indicates a good ability to discriminate between the 2 classes with Area under the curve (AUC) score of 0.90. In the learning curve results, training and validation scores improve steadily and converge at the end of the learning curve, and threshold analysis proves that the model is optimized by avoiding either false positive or false negative. This method which is based on GBM shows quite high level of accuracy and strength in earthquake prediction. Due to its capacity to run complicated seismic characteristics, it is being utilized as an invaluable instrument in disaster preparedness, in combination with early warning systems, which portrays a data-based approach to the kind of reliability that results in seismic event predictions.

title: Detection of Cyber Attack in a Digital Twin-Enabled Grid-Connected Microgrid
authors: Md Mhamud Hussen Sifat, Md Enamul Haque, Shama Naz Islam, B. Kalyan Kumar, Deepak Ronanki
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Modern microgrids (MGs) face growing cyber threats, notably false data injection attacks (FDIAs) on inverter set points that disrupt grid stability. Conventional voltage- and current-based detection methods often yield high false positives due to the similarities of impacts of FDIA with overload or PV fault signatures. This paper proposes a digital twin (DT)enhanced detection framework to address this limitation. The DT serves as a high-fidelity replica of the MG, capable of forecasting load demand and generation, thereby providing the classifiers with additional features that encapsulate fault conditions and load uncertainty. Furthermore, comparative analysis demonstrates that incorporating DT-generated insights significantly improves the performance of various machine learning and deep learning based classifiers. The results highlight the DT's potential to enhance resilience against cyber-attacks in MGs.

title: Modeling, Control and Implementation of Grid Forming Electric Vehicle Charging System
authors: Kisal K. Gunawardana, Md Enamul Haque, M. T. Arif, Ameen Gargoom
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: The large-scale integration of electric vehicle into the power grid impacts the stability of distribution grid with renewable and distributed energy sources. Therefore, there is a growing need for smart electric vehicle (EV) charging systems equipped with grid forming capability to provide ancillary services and ensure stability of distributed grid. This paper presents a grid-forming inverter (GFM) interfaced electric vehicle charging system employing droop control to enhance grid stability. The design and modeling of grid forming EV charging system is presented and implemented in Matlab/Simscape based simulation and experimentally validated using dSPACE MicroLabBox. Simulation and experimental results confirm that grid forming EV charging system has the ability to provide grid support under grid disturbances and regulate voltage and frequency under steady state and dynamic conditions. The outcomes of this research highlight the potential of GFM-enabled EV chargers as active grid participants, paving the way for resilient and flexible future distribution networks with high renewable and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E V$</tex> penetration.

title: Locating Mis-Wiring Associated Earth Fault users in Low Voltage Distribution Network with Poor Metering Data
authors: Xiaofei Feng, Chaoqiang Chen, Feng Chen, Bin Li, Sheng Su
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Mis-Wiring associated earth faults exist widely in the low voltage distribution network (LVDN). Based on smart meter measurement data, this earth fault detection and location can be achieved by attribution analysis methods. However, the effectiveness of attribution analysis can be affected by interference from normal users with low electricity usage and poor quality of metering data, which significantly reduces the accuracy to identify earth fault users of mis-wiring. This paper first introduces the characteristics of normal users with low electricity usage and the classification of data quality. Their impact on the identification of mis-wiring associated earth fault users is analyzed. Based on random sample consensus (RANSAC) and loss function weighting, a robust regression identification method for mis-wiring associated earth fault users is proposed. Simulations are performed to test the identification of multiple mis-wiring fault users with poor quality of metering data including noise, errors, asynchrony, and data missing, to verify the effectiveness of the proposed method. Ablation experiments are used to verify the superiority of the proposed approach over multivariate linear regression.

title: Enhanced Question Answering via Multi-Stage Search, AI Re-Ranking and Adaptive Queries
authors: P. Feridoz Khan, Kudumala Sneha Latha Reddy, Kapelli Sai Deepak, Madathala Vardhini, Kuppuri Lohithaksh, Addala Hemanth Kumar
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence and information retrieval has intensified the need for systems that can deliver accurate and contextually relevant answers to complex user queries. Conventional question-answering models often depend solely on pre-trained knowledge, which can lead to outdated or factually inconsistent responses. To overcome these shortcomings, this project introduces an Enhanced Question Answering Framework that integrates Multi-Stage Search, AI-Based Re-Ranking, and Adaptive Query Mechanisms. The proposed system employs a hybrid retrieval process combining dense and sparse search methods to maximize information coverage from large-scale and heterogeneous data sources. Retrieved results are then refined through an intelligent neural re-ranking model that prioritizes semantically relevant content, followed by adaptive prompt construction to minimize noise and improve response accuracy. The integration of these components ensures precise evidence selection, reduces hallucination, and enhances factual reliability and explainability. Experimental analysis on open- domain and multi-hop reasoning datasets demonstrate improved exact match and F1 scores compared to baseline retrieval- augmented models. The proposed architecture also shows robustness across varied query complexities and dataset domains, maintaining consistent performance even under ambiguous input conditions. This framework establishes a more transparent, efficient, and trustworthy approach for building advanced, data- driven, and domain-adaptive question-answering systems suit- able for academic, enterprise, and research applications while promoting interpretability and factual integrity in real-world deployments.

title: Multiple-Class Skin Cancer Diagnosis by Deep Convolutional Neural Model on Dermoscopical Images
authors: Boopathy K, Karthikeyan K, Sathiyapriya A, Kiruthika P, Sureshkumar N, Vinothkumar E
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: The timely and accurate diagnosis of skin cancer is vital for improving patient outcomes and lowering the death rate linked to this illness. This paper centers on a skin cancer diagnosis system that employs deep convolutional neural networks (DCNN) for analyzing dermoscopic images. The aim of this new framework is to effectively identify different skin variants, such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign keratosis, by consistently extracting deeper features from the image data. One of the most important aspects of this research is the technology implementation of image preprocessing, for instance, contrast improvement, and segmentation of a lesion during the process of an image searching for regions of an interest. The DCNN scheme is created in a very careful way with a couple of convolution and pooling steps that are then followed by fully-connected ones for classification, allowing the network to understand complicated patterns of skin lesions with high generalizability. On top of that, adapted learning with adjustments is used here, where pretrained models are the basis to speed up the training and get better results on the small dataset. The system is tested on a public dataset of dermoscopic images and shows better results in terms of classification accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity against traditional machine learning approaches. This paper introduces a robust, automated tool designed to assist dermatologists in the early detection of skin cancer and ensure accurate diagnoses. By doing so, it can significantly minimize errors and facilitate timely treatment for patients.

title: Unified Time and Frequency Domain Power Flow Analysis of Triple Active Bridge DC-DC Converter
authors: N Sugumar, Aditya Chakraborty, Indrajit Sarkar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: The triple active bridge converter (TABC) is a three-port isolated dc-dc converter topology capable of bi-directional power transfer between the ports, making it highly suitable for energy harvesting and storage systems. This work presents a time domain analysis (TDA) and General Harmonic Analysis (GHA) of power flow of a TABC in single phase shift (SPS) and penta phase shift (PPS) modulation techniques. The converter’s circuit configuration, operating principles, and mathematical modeling are discussed to explain the influence of phase shifts on power exchange among the ports. SPS is examined for its simplicity and suitability in port power control, while the PPS is analyzed for its enhanced flexibility in independent multi-port power and circulating power regulations. Mathematic analysis and derivations are presented along with simulation results to validate the theoretical models and highlights the accuracy differences between TDA and GHA. These findings provide guidance for the appropriate selection of power flow analysis method to enable effective design of the TABC for various applications.

title: Between the Cores: Covert Communication in Modern Cloud Workloads
authors: Akshat Kumar, Shelly Sachdeva
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: Covert channels are an ongoing and dynamic danger to the confidentiality of contemporary cloud computing infras-tructures. This work investigates CPU-based timing channels that leverage shared physical resources, processor cores, memory hierarchies' and scheduling logic to facilitate illicit communication in logically isolated virtual machines and containers. Based on previous research, we propose an improved attack strategy that uses load modulation and timing variance to develop a consistent covert communication channel in multi-tenant cloud infrastructures. Our experiments on the shared virtualization platforms provide evidence that such channels can sustain bit rates of up to 95 bps with an average accuracy of decoding 93% while being strongly stealthy and resistant to traditional detection tools. We also place our results in a general taxonomy of modern-day covert channel methods, such as GPU-based translation lookaside buffer (TLB) channels, processing-in-memory (PIM) boosted attacks. We underscore the increasing sophistication and stealth of such attacks in a comparative execution analysis, several of which now piggyback on normal system behavior and span heterogeneous compute domains. Our research highlights the shortcomings of conventional defenses like pinning the core and noise injection and necessitates a next generation of detection infrastructures enabled through telemetry-aware adaptive machine learning. We conclude with an analysis of the security architecture implications in the cloud and map out directions towards forward-looking mitigation in future computing platforms.

title: Study on Quantum Image Representation Schemes: A Comparative Analysis
authors: Ritwika Das, Shouvik Das, Tanmay Bhattacharya
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: This study presents a comparative evaluation of three quantum image representation techniques: FRQI, QPIE, and NEQR. The Baboon image is used as a benchmark, and performance is assessed through PSNR, MSE, SSIM, and execution time. Simulations were conducted using a noiseless quantum simulator to ensure fair comparison across methods. Results indicate that QPIE achieves the best reconstruction quality, with PSNR = 117.97, MSE = 1.037, and SSIM = 0.975 for the 8 x 8 image, while maintaining strong performance for larger sizes. In contrast, FRQI shows lower quality and high execution time. NEQR is computationally faster (4.85 s) but delivers moderate quality (PSNR = 79.38, SSIM = 0.859). Additionally, QPIE requires fewer qubits, making it more resource-efficient. Overall, QPIE emerges as the most effective representation, striking a balance between image quality, computational cost, and qubit utilization. These findings highlight QPIE as a promising candidate for future quantum image processing tasks such as compression, edge detection, and negative transformation.

title: Spatio-Temporal Graph Learning for Rail Traction Power Flow Prediction
authors: Ali Jamali, Mustafa Kabak, Nasrin Aslshahrivar, Bahram Bolandparvaz, Reyhaneh Hesami, Hamid Khayyam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Urban rail traction power systems experience fast-changing, high-magnitude, and nonstationary loads, which make real-time situational awareness difficult. While supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) systems usually provide substation measurements, such data may be unavailable, incomplete, or delayed. We propose a two-stage, topology-aware machine-learning pipeline for real-time estimation of electrical parameters at both substations and trains. In Stage 1, XGBoost maps operational and kinematic features to first-pass substation parameter estimates (voltage, current, power). In Stage 2, a spatiotemporal graph neural network (GNN) refines these estimates by encoding the railway's electrical topology, representing trains as moving nodes and substations as fixed nodes. This design captures network-wide interactions such as regenerative energy reproduction and feeder coupling, yielding accurate substation- and train-level electrical parameters at time t. To prioritize robustness under operational stress, an Operational Criticality-Weighted loss (OCW-Loss) upweights errors in low-voltage / high-current conditions. Validation of one year of operational data from a high-demand urban rail corridor shows high accuracy, with a mean substation-voltage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{\mathrm{2}}$</tex> of 0.952 across 20 substations. The method provides a practical, data-driven alternative to SCADA for substation monitoring and a basis for “what-if” operational analyses without re-engineering a physics simulator.

title: Unsupervised VideoSAR Sparse Imaging Using Physically Interpretable Video Tensor Decomposition-Based Deep Prior
authors: Min Li, Linhong Jiang, Xinyu Liu, Weibo Huo, Junjie Wu, Jiashu Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Video Synthetic Aperture Radar (VideoSAR) is an advanced microwave imaging technology that can be applied to the motion platforms such as unmanned aerial vehicles. It overcomes the limitations of traditional SAR imaging, enabling dynamic high-frame-rate observation. VideoSAR has significant advantages in applications such as traffic monitoring, disaster prediction and environmental surveillance. However, the huge storage and computational overhead caused by high-frame-rate imaging pose challenges to the application of VideoSAR, particularly in small platforms like drones. Conventional VideoSAR imaging methods require Nyquist-rate sampling and consumes a lot of resources. VideoSAR sparse imaging techniques, such as tensor recovery and deep learning, have been proposed to address this issue. However, existing sparse imaging methods exhibit limitations in computational efficiency, adaptability, and data dependency. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an unsupervised VideoSAR sparse imaging method based on physically interpretable deep prior. Firstly, a physically interpretable deep prior for VideoSAR data is presented, which can leverage the redundancy of VideoSAR data for sparse imaging. Secondly, an unfolded VideoSAR sparse imaging network is constructed. Finally,an unsupervised learning framework is developed to eliminate reliance on labeled data. By combining unsupervised learning with deep prior modeling, the proposed method can achieve efficient high-frame-rate imaging under sparse sampling conditions. Besides, the proposed method significantly enhances the robustness and adaptability of VideoSAR sparse imaging. Experimental results demonstrate superior performance compared to existing tensor recovery-based and supervised deep learning-based methods.

title: Energy-Efficient Online Federated Learning in Wireless Networks
authors: Jaemin Kim, Junsuk Oh, Wonjong Noh, Sungrae Cho
abstract: In this work, we study energy-efficient online federated learning. First, we analyzed the convergence performance of device selection in dynamic and non-stationary conditions, deriving the upper bound of the convergence rate. Building on this, we formulated an optimization problem that minimizes energy consumption while ensuring convergence and meeting latency constraints. Second, we developed an online allocation and scheduling-based iterative strategy (OASIS). Here, we designed a combinatorial upper-confidence-bound-based device scheduling algorithm with a newly designed reward function. We also derived a Lambert function-based power allocation to the scheduled devices in closed form. We performed a dynamic regret analysis, which reveals that the proposed algorithm effectively adapts to dynamic environments and maintains near-optimal decisions over time. It also demonstrates that the proposed algorithm achieves sublinear regret in a slowly changing dynamic environment and optimal regret in a static environment. Experimental results show that the proposed OASIS achieves faster convergence and provides significantly lower energy consumption than existing conventional baseline strategies. We also confirmed that the performance gain increases as the target accuracy level becomes higher. These results validate the energy efficiency and robustness of the proposed approach in realistic and time-varying federated learning environments.

title: Doppler Shift Keying Modulation for Uplink Multiple Access Over Doubly-Dispersive Channels
authors: Xuehan Wang, Jintao Wang, Hai Lin, Jinhong Yuan, Xu Shi, Hengyu Zhang, Jian Song
abstract: The delay- Doppler (DD) domain modulation has been regarded as one of the most competitive candidates to support wireless communications for emerging high-mobility applications in the sixth-generation mobile networks. Unfortunately, most of the existing designs for DD domain modulation suffer from high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) and unbearable detection complexity under uplink transmission since large time duration and bandwidth are required to guarantee high DD resolutions. To address these issues, the Doppler shift keying (DSK) modulation based on the orthogonal delay Doppler division multiplexing modulator is proposed in this paper, where the input-output characterization in the DD domain is fully exploited. The principle of the DSK transceiver is first established with the one-hot mapper and low-complexity iterative successive interference cancellation-maximum ratio combining detector for point-to-point scenarios. The proposed scheme is then generalized to the zero auto-correlation sequence-based implementation, which benefits the extension of multi-user (MU) uplink DSK frameworks. For uplink DSK transmission, Zadoff-Chu (ZC) sequences are adopted as the basis sequences. We optimize the assignment of ZC roots to different user equipments (UEs) by minimizing the maximum inter-user interference. This optimization process, which analyzes the root allocation, directly assigns a specific ZC sequence to each UE. The PAPR and bit error rate performance of the proposed DSK modulation with the low-complexity detector is finally verified by extensive simulation results under doubly-dispersive channels, which demonstrates the superiority of DSK modulation especially for uplink multiple access over doubly dispersive channels.

title: Low-Complexity AdderCNN Equalizer for 200 Gbaud RRM-Based IM/DD Transmission
authors: Dan Li, Yevhenii Osadchuk, Armands Ostrovskis, Tianyu Jiang, Francesco Da Ros, Darko Zibar, Richard Schatz, Lu Zhang, Xianbin Yu, Vjaceslavs Bobrovs, Xiaodan Pang, Oskars Ozolins
abstract: Neural networks (NNs) have emerged as an effective equalization approach for high-speed intensity modulation and direct detection (IM/DD) optical links, where chromatic dispersion, limited bandwidth, and device-induced nonlinearities degrade signal quality. However, the heavy reliance on multiplications in conventional NNs leads to high computational complexity, limiting hardware deployment. Addition-based convolutional NN (AdderCNN) was originally developed for image classification, where it reduces complexity by replacing multiplications with subtractions and accumulations. In this work, we propose a multiplier-free AdderCNN equalizer for up to 200 Gbaud on-off keying IM/DD links using a 26 GHz O-band ring resonator modulator. After 500 m single-mode fiber transmission, AdderCNN achieves bit error rate (BER) below the 7% overhead (OH) hard-decision forward error correction (HD-FEC) threshold of 3.8 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-3</sup>, outperforming conventional equalizers. Compared to classical CNN, AdderCNN eliminates all real multiplications (RM), reduces bit operations (BOP) by 87.6% and the number of adders and shifters (NABS) by 96.9% per equalized symbol under 32-bit full-precision with comparable performance. With 7-bit quantization, it further reduces BOP by 72.3% and NABS by 71.0% per equalized symbol, while keeping BER below the 7% OH HD-FEC threshold. Therefore, AdderCNN has the potential to become a hardware-efficient NN equalization solution for next-generation optical interconnects.

title: Multiclass Stress Detection Using Psychological Emotion Mapping and Transformer Architectures
authors: Kalyani Pendke, Mayuri Digalwar, Charu Goel
abstract: Stress is a common mental health problem that is apparent at a range of degrees of severity and in different circumstances. The conventional models to detect psychological stress are purely heuristic, mostly only binary classified, and neglect the fact that stress is complex and multi-dimensional. This paper presents a new hybrid deep learning model for multiclass stress detection by combining BERT, RoBERTa, CNN, and BiLSTM models. By leveraging the “GoEmotion dataset”, the authors relate emotions to three different stress levels: Not Stressed, Stressed, and Highly Stressed, drawing on psychological expertise. The model is also tested on two external datasets, VENT and ISEAR, preprocessed and relabeled with the same stress mapping schema for generalizability. A major feature in this work is the use of LIME, which improves interpretability by pointing out the most informative text features. The hybrid model outperforms the baselines with an accuracy and F1-score of 97.84 percent each. In addition to this, the proposed model shows strong cross-domain generalization. In the field of mental health applications, this model proves to be a strong base for an explainable and real-time model for stress detection.

title: A Data-Driven Pre-Diagnosis Framework for Early Detection of Water Imbalance in PEMFC
authors: Haisong Xu, Lei Wang, Zhiyang Liu, Guangzheng Qiu, Hongbo Zhou, Hongye Su
abstract: This paper presents a pre-diagnosis approach for identifying water imbalance faults in proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) system, aiming to provide early warning alarms to support proactive protection measures. Unlike traditional fixed-model methods, the proposed approach introduces a dynamic framework to handle the complexity of PEMFC fault conditions with improved efficiency and accuracy. First, the minimal redundancy maximum relevance (mRMR) algorithm is employed for feature selection to optimally balance input data quality and quantity. Subsequently, multiple encoder layers enhanced by a mixture of experts mechanism are utilized to extract key information. By dynamically activating only the most relevant experts, the model’s adaptability to diverse input is improved, and computational load is reduced. Next, an adaptive multi-task learning (MTL) framework is developed to train time-series forecasting and fault classification tasks simultaneously. Finally, the proposed method is validated on a 5kW PEMFC stack under various operating conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves rapid and accurate identification of diverse PEMFC water management faults, detecting anomalies 25 s before occurrence with approximately 98.0% diagnostic accuracy at a fast response time of 1.15 ms.

title: Drude's Parameters of Heated Crystalline Silicon from Reflection THz-TDS Measurements
authors: Laurens F.E. Beijnen, Martijn D. Huiskes, Andrea Neto, Paolo Maria Sberna
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology
abstract: The spectroscopic properties of crystalline silicon wafers are investigated experimentally as a function of the temperature. To this goal, samples of phosphorus-doped silicon are characterized using Terahertz Time-Domain Spectroscopy (THz-TDS) in reflection. Four different samples span resistivities from ∼0.04 − 50Ω cm, for temperatures ranging from room temperature to 200 C. The measurements confirm that the widely used Drude's theory is adequate also to model the dispersion of silicon at higher temperatures. When comparing the corresponding scattering times obtained here using THz TDS pulses with the scattering time derived from the well accepted DC based empirical model of the mobility, differences emerge depending on the doping level. The scattering times predicted and measured are on the same order of magnitude and the anticipated reduction of the scattering time with increasing temperature has also been confirmed by the high frequency measurements. The absorptivity of the samples is also estimated accurately as a function of the frequency up to 1 THz

title: Artificial Soiling Replication of Field Losses on Commercial Photovoltaic Modules
authors: Fang Li, David C. Miller, Sai Tatapudi, Govindasamy TamizhMani
abstract: This study demonstrates the capabilities of an indoor artificial soiling approach developed to closely replicate the natural, cyclic soil accumulation processes in the field—dust suspension, deposition, and sedimentation/cementation—for a subtropical climate. In this work, a near-field environment is replicated in an artificial soiling cubic chamber through controlled regulation of humidity, temperature, dust type, and dust concentration, based on site-specific historical climate data. Two different models (MA and MB) of full-size commercial photovoltaic modules from a single manufacturer, installed side by side in the mid-Atlantic United States, were retrieved and subjected to artificial soiling experiments and various characterization measurements, including short-circuit current, colorimetry, reflectance, X-ray fluorescence, laser diffraction, and optical microscopy. Both in the field and in our improved field-representative artificial soiling tests, the MA model experienced roughly twice the soiling loss as the MB model. To closely replicate the field soiling losses for a site-specific climate, it is critical to include: 1) The use of field-collected dust with identical dust chemistry and particle distribution instead of standardized ISO 12103 Arizona Road dusts, 2) the use of only a small amount of field-collected dust inside the chamber during the deposition process (e.g., 0.15 g), and 3) the preconditioning of the surface coating for the partial/full dose of UV stress as experienced in the field during sunlight exposure and the abrasion as experienced in the field during regular module cleaning activities, if/as needed. The field-representative artificial soiling method developed here could potentially be adopted for rank ordering of various antisoiling coatings developed by researchers and industry stakeholders.

title: Unsupervised Autoencoder-Based Anomaly Detection Under Limited Failure Data For Oil Industry
authors: Victor Z. Ferreira, Eduardo M. Brito, Guilherme M. F. Da Cunha, Felipe A. R. Perez, Erico D. W. Dos Reis, Edfram R. Pereira, Francisco Louzada
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper presents a deep autoencoder-based methodology for unsupervised anomaly detection in centrifugal pumps under limited failure data conditions, focusing on real-world applications in the oil and gas industry. The autoencoders are designed to reconstruct input sequences from SCADA data, including pressure, temperature, vibration, and electric current, with anomaly detection derived from the reconstruction error. Anomaly detection is based on a moving-minimum anomaly score (MMAS), designed to suppress transient fluctuations and highlight consistent deviations. Each autoencoder was trained on pre-fault operational windows and evaluated on sequences encompassing registered failures. The autoencoder detected anomalies up to 74 days before the actual fault detection by the maintenance team, with consistent warnings of two to three days in additional cases. The architecture, training parameters, and data preprocessing steps are proposed, including pressure differential computation and idle-state filtering. The findings demonstrate the viability of unsupervised deep learning in industrial fault detection scenarios characterized by limited failure annotations in an industrial environment.

title: ARGP: Adaptive and Recoverable 3D Gaussian Splatting Pruning for Efficient Real-Time Scene Reconstruction
authors: Xinyu Liu, Dahua Gao, Wenlong Wang, Yuxi Hu, Yihao Chen, Guangming Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as an efficient explicit representation for real-time novel view synthesis. However, repeated densification during training often leads to uncontrolled Gaussian growth, causing heavy memory overhead and slow convergence. This redundancy stems from two limitations: (1) opacity-based sparsification that fails to maintain consistent pruning pressure under evolving distributions; (2) irreversible pruning strategies that risk eliminating essential Gaussians. To address these, we propose Adaptive and Recoverable Gaussian Pruning (ARGP), a training-integrated framework that suppresses redundant growth while preserving reconstruction quality. During densification, we employ Adaptive Opacity Pruning (AOP), which removes a fixed proportion of low-opacity Gaussians based on quantiles, effectively suppressing redundancy. In the fine-tuning stage, we introduce Iterative Recovery Pruning (IRP), which selectively reinstates critical Gaussians using a gradient-informed recovery score, thus preventing over-pruning and preserving reconstruction quality. Extensive experimental results on Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks & Temples, and Deep Blending validate the effectiveness of our proposed ARGP. For instance, ARGP achieves a 90.2% reduction in Gaussian counts and a 1.33× training speedup, while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality on Mip-NeRF 360. Our code is available at https://github.com/Sinyo-Liu/ARGP.

title: The Online Quench Detection System Based on CNN for Superconducting Magnets of CiADS and HIAF
authors: Donge Liu, Qinggao Yao, Xianjin Ou, Tongjun Yang, Shijun Zheng, Beimin Wu, Wentian Feng, Yujin Tong, Daotong Ming, Zeen Yao
abstract: To address false quench detection issues in superconducting magnets during online operation of the High-Intensity Heavy Ion Facility (HIAF) and the China Initiative Accelerator Driven System (CiADS), caused by interference, power supply oscillations, fluctuations, and non-absolute balance in the bridge circuit, this study proposes a modeling and discrimination method for superconducting magnet quench behavior based on a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The model is quantized and compiled using the Vitis AI toolchain. Leveraging the Deep Processing Unit (DPU)—a neural network-specific IP core introduced by AMD/Xilinx—it is deployed onto the ZYNQ embedded device, enabling hardware acceleration of CNN inference on the Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA). The developed CNN hardware-software platform for quench detection was validated during the trial operation of the HIAF superconducting magnets and the CiADS excitation conditioning phase. The algorithm achieved 97.97% accuracy in quench detection classification tasks with detection latency below 1 millisecond, demonstrating exceptional precision and real-time performance. The ZYNQ's ARM processor integrates an Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System (EPICS) Input/Output Controller (IOC). By acquiring key parameters through this IOC, a dynamic quench detection strategy covering the entire magnet operation cycle was developed, further enhancing detection accuracy and ensuring magnet safety.

title: Collaborated with Hallucination: Enhancing Egocentric Grounded Question Answering via Error Demonstrations
authors: Shenshen Li, Xing Xu, Fumin Shen, Zhe Sun, Andrzej Cichocki, Heng Tao Shen
abstract: The grounded question answering in egocentric videos (Ego-GQA) aims to identify the relevant temporal window and generate corresponding responses in natural language given a textual question. Compared with third-person videos, egocentric video understanding requires more advanced human-centric thinking capability. However, existing Ego-GQA approaches often fail to distinguish the inherent limitations of dynamic egocentric context understanding, treating both first-person and third-person perspectives equally. This oversight leads to hallucinations and a lack of proper egocentric reasoning in first-person video understanding. To address this issue, we propose a novel Collaborated with Hallucination (CoHa) framework for the Ego-GQA, which quantifies the hallucinations generated by an Ego-GQA model and further leverages them as error demonstrations to constrain the model’s reasoning process, encouraging it to ground predictions in egocentric visual cues instead of relying on biased pretraining priors. Specifically, we first employ Subjective Logic to quantify the degree of uncertainty in unreliable answers. We then generate diffusion-based noisy visual inputs to amplify the hallucinations as error demonstrations, which are used to append appropriate constraints to the model according to the uncertainty. These constraints effectively steer predictions away from the unreliable semantics induced by inherent drawbacks in egocentric thinking. Additionally, we incorporate an interactive refinement module to facilitate the model to explore more fine-grained cues observed from the first-person view. Extensive experiments on two widely used benchmarks demonstrate that our CoHa method outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/Mrshenshen/CoHa.

title: Design and Implementation of an Uncrewed Aerial-Aquatic Vehicle With Antenna Retractor_supp1-3669797.mp4
authors: Yunhua Jiang
abstract: Uncrewed aerial-aquatic vehicles (UAAVs) can operate in both aerial and aquatic domains and repeatedly cross domains, thereby presenting considerable application prospects. This letter introduces ‘Diving Hawk-2 (DH-2),’ a forward-tilting coaxial three-axis six-rotor uncrewed aerial-aquatic vehicle (UAAV) that solves the persistent problem of real-time communication for submerged vehicles via an antenna retractor. We detail the vehicle's mechanical and control co-design, which ensures multi-domain agility without actuator redundancy. Experimental results from field trials in polar, lake, and high visibility pool environments confirm both the system's capability for real-time information transmission and control during underwater locomotion, and the vehicle's ability to perform repeatable, cross-domain transitions with stable multi-mode locomotion. The DH-2 platform, with its high modularity, offers a practical and replicable model for future research in real-time control of UAAVs.

title: Trainable Synchrosqueezed Chirplet Transform for IF-crossover Signals with Very Close Chirp-rates
authors: Xu Yan, Lin Li, Qingtang Jiang
abstract: In positioning, navigation and timing (PNT) systems based on low earth orbit satellites (LEO-PNT), extracting frequency information from densely deployed LEO satellites is a challenging task that requires processing two signals with crossover instantaneous frequency (IF) curves and very close chirp-rates. Existing methods, such as generalized linear chirplet transform and synchrosqueezed chirplet transform (SCT), are limited by a 'bubble phenomenon' caused by slow decay along the chirp-rate axis, which distorts the time-frequency-chirp rate (TFC) representation near crossovers and invalidates conventional local search and fixed-threshold strategies. To overcome this challenge, we propose a trainable SCT (TSCT) framework, which first utilizes adaptive detection techniques to dynamically generate noise spectra and detection thresholds, and then incorporates supervised learning for the decision-making task in high-dimensional parameter reassignment. Additionally, an improved version of the SCT frequency estimation operator based on dual spectral centroid method is designed to improve the stability of the algorithm. Through numerical simulations and validation with measured Starlink data, the proposed method demonstrates excellent performance in handling signals with complex intersecting IFs.

title: µShare: Non-Intrusive Kernel Co-Locating on NVIDIA GPUs
authors: Wenhao Huang, Zhaolin Duan, Laiping Zhao, Yuhao Zhang, Yanjie Wang, Yiming Li, Yihan Wang, Yichi Chen, Zhihang Tang, Kang Chen, Deze Zeng, Wenxin Li, Keqiu Li
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: The hardware scheduler on NVIDIA GPUs is highly inefficient in utilizing micro-architectural hardware resources. It places blocks from the same kernel within the same GPU Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) core, resulting in a stacking colocating problem, where identical blocks are placed within the same SM core, saturating only a subset of intra-SM hardware resources while leaving others underutilized. The primary challenge in addressing this issue is that the NVIDIA hardware is closed-source, preventing us from directly modifying the hardware scheduler. To bridge the semantic gap between the resource demands of kernels and the scheduler, we introduce µ Share, which enables intra-SM scattered colocating of kernels through a non-intrusive half-plus blocksize shaping method. It shapes the blocksize of kernels to a halfplus blocksize (i.e., slightly more than half of the SM's thread capacity), scattering identical blocks of the same kernel across different SMs. It further adopts a time-shifted launching method to reduce intra-SM resource contention. Compared to state-of-the-art systems, µ Share does not require intrusive modifications to hardware or kernel code, yet it can still improve inference throughput by 26.90%-54.09% and increases low-level hardware utilization by 38.53%-61.15%.

title: An Adaptive Predictive Threshold (APT) Algorithm for Load Balancing in Cloud Environments
authors: P V Anusha, Ms Y. Avanija, D. Venkatesh, M. Mallikarjuna Rao
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Cloud computing environments necessitate effective load balancing strategies to manage the growing volume of high-demand applications. This paper introduces an Adaptive Predictive Threshold (APT) Load Balancing Algorithm aimed at improving performance and energy efficiency through smart decision-making. The APT algorithm utilizes two thresholds that are updated dynamically-an adaptive overload threshold and a dynamic underload threshold-to precisely identify overloaded and underloaded Virtual Machines (VMs). Additionally, a predictive assessment enhances the mechanism by anticipating future VM loads, thereby averting sudden overloading and ensuring consistent task assignments. In contrast to traditional static algorithms, APT implements a consolidation strategy where an overloaded VM must finish a designated number of tasks before shifting to an underloaded state. Experimental findings indicate that the APT algorithm significantly surpasses commonly used methods such as Round Robin, First Come First Serve, and Equally Spread Load Balancing, especially under substantial task loads. Its adaptive, prediction-based methodology results in decreased instability, enhanced resource utilization, and improved overall system performance in practical cloud environments.

title: BitDecoding: Unlocking Tensor Cores for Long-Context LLMs with Low-Bit KV Cache
authors: Dayou Du, Shijie Cao, Jianyi Cheng, Luo Mai, Ting Cao, Mao Yang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: The rise of long-context Large Language Models (LLMs) amplifies memory and bandwidth demands during autoregressive decoding, as the Key-Value (KV) cache grows with each generated token. Low-bit KV-cache quantization (e.g., 4-bit or 2-bit) can reduce memory footprint while preserving accuracy, but existing systems suffer from slow decoding due to their exclusive reliance on CUDA cores, neglecting Tensor Cores—the primary source of compute on modern GPUs. We present BitDecoding, a new long-context LLMs inference system with low-bit KV cache. BitDecoding enables efficient low-bit KV cache decoding by cooperatively leveraging CUDA Cores and Tensor Cores. It introduces methods for automatically inducing optimized layouts to exploit Tensor Cores, along with novel warp-level parallelization strategies for dequantization. For unified system support, BitDecoding includes a query transformation module supporting diverse attention variants, a quantization kernel to support both tensor-wise and channelwise scaling used in various quantization algorithms with high performance, and a dequantization kernel with a softwaredefined pipeline to coordinate CUDA and Tensor Cores execution for mix-precision operations. In addition, architecture-specific optimizations leverage Hopper's warpgroup tensor instructions and Blackwell's native low-precision tensor formats to maximize decoding throughput on the latest GPU generations. Evaluated on Blackwell, Hopper, Ada, and Ampere architectures, BitDecoding attains on average a 7.5× decoding speedup over FP16 FlashDecoding-v2, and further reaches up to 8.6× with native MXFP4 formats on Blackwell, while surpassing the state-of-the-art low-bit system QServe by up to 4.3×. On LLaMA-3.1-8B with a 128K context, BitDecoding reduces singlebatch decoding latency by 3×, demonstrating substantial improvements for long-context generation, and is open sourced at https://github.com/OpenBitSys/BitDecoding.

title: ARIADNE: Adaptive UVM Management for Efficient GPU Memory Oversubscription
authors: Hyunkyun Shin, Seongtae Bang, Hyungwon Park, Daehoon Kim
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: Unified Virtual Memory (UVM) simplifies GPU programming and supports memory oversubscription, but suffers from severe performance degradation under high memory pressure due to page fault overhead and thrashing. Existing approaches such as prefetching, access counter-based migration, and dynamic Zero-copy offer limited benefits and often require hardware or compiler modifications, undermining UVM's portability and ease of deployment. We present ARIADNE, a runtime UVM management framework that preserves UVM's GPU memory abstraction while ensuring high and robust performance under memory oversubscription. ARIADNE is guided by three principles: (1) pipelined fault handling to hide migration latency, (2) Sharing Degree, a runtime metric that captures thread-level access locality without requiring hardware or compiler changes, to inform placement decisions, and (3) dynamic placement of memory regions between GPU memory and Zero-copy based on real-time access patterns. Implemented entirely within NVIDIA's UVM driver, ARIADNE requires no recompilation or hardware modifications and applies transparently to any executable or closed-source GPU UVM applications. Our experimental results show that ARIADNE delivers average speedups of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.9 \times, 5.0 \times$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.8 \times$</tex> over a state-of-the-art method at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 3 0 \%, ~} \text{1 7 5 \%}$</tex>, and 300 % oversubscription, respectively, while effectively preventing thrashing and maintaining near-linear performance scaling.
