title: Deep Learning-Based Multi-Angle Classification of Tree Species from UAV Images
authors: M Sobhana, JRS Aditya Vardhan, P Srilakshmi Sravanthi, K Uma Santosh Reddy
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: Accurate identification of tree species from aerial images is crucial for forest monitoring, biodiversity evaluation, and ecological planning. However, the high variability introduced by different camera viewpoints, such as nadir and oblique angles, poses a significant challenge to conventional deep learning models. In this work, we propose a novel fusion-based approach to robustly classify tree species from drone-acquired RGB images by leveraging view-specific feature extraction and integration. We train four state-of-the-art convolutional architectures, ConvNeXt and EfficientNetV2S for nadir images, ResNet50V2 and DenseNet121 for oblique images, using ImageNet-pretrained weights and perform extensive evaluation across in-domain and cross-domain settings. While individual models perform well within their native domains, they exhibit substantial generalization gaps when evaluated on unseen viewpoints. To address this, we develop a feature-level fusion model that combines EfficientNetV2S (nadir) and DenseNet121 (oblique) backbones, followed by global pooling and fully connected layers. Our fusion model achieves 90.04 % overall accuracy and an F1-score of 0.9003 on the combined test set, significantly outperforming all standalone models. These results highlight the effectiveness of cross-view feature fusion in mitigating domain gaps and improving tree species classification under real-world aerial imaging conditions.

title: Sensitivity Analysis of a Pem Electrolyzer in a Solar Pv Powered Green Hydrogen Production System
authors: Israt Jahan, Narottam Das, Omar Farrok, Md. Rabiul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: This study focuses on the design, modelling, and evaluation of a standalone hydrogen production system powered by solar photovoltaic (PV) energy. This system includes a PV array, a maximum power point tracking (MPPT) controller, a DC/DC converter, an energy storage unit, and a polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) electrolyzer. To assess the operational behavior of the system, a repeated seven (07) day solar irradiance profile is employed in the MATLAB/Simulink environment. The MPPT controller, employing the perturb and observe algorithm, ensures optimal solar power extraction, while the battery supports continuous hydrogen production during periods of low irradiance. Simulation results indicate that the system produces 197 kg of hydrogen during the analysis period, with an average energy consumption of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$63.67 \text{kWh} / \text{kgH}_{2}$</tex>. Parametric analysis shows that hydrogen yield and system efficiency are substantially improved by the reduction of internal resistance, the expansion of the membrane area, and the number of cells of the PEM electrolyzer. This method shows that off-grid hydrogen generation using solar energy may be done reliably and without emissions.

title: I-POP: Ignite Positive Prefetchers
authors: Yiquan Lin, Wenhai Lin, Yiquan Chen, Jiexiong Xu, Shishun Cai, Jiarong Ye, Zonghui Wang, Wenzhi Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: Hardware prefetching is a well-established technique for bridging the processor-memory performance gap. To improve cache miss coverage, modern processors often integrate multiple prefetchers. However, multi-prefetcher systems without proper management often suffer from suboptimal performance due to a surge of useless prefetches. Several techniques have been proposed to select appropriate prefetchers for issuing requests, but they all face limitations. Specifically, existing (1) static schemes lack feedback regulation mechanisms and suffer from inflexible prefetcher selections; (2) reinforcement learning (RL)based schemes incur high overhead and suffer from adjustment lag; and (3) performance-counter-based schemes rely on inefficient runtime metrics that fail to accurately and clearly reflect a prefetcher's true impact on performance. In this paper, we propose I-POP, a high-performance and lowoverhead prefetcher management scheme for multi-prefetcher systems. I-POP introduces a novel runtime metric, Prefetch Effectiveness (PE), which aggregates each prefetch request's beneficial and harmful effects to precisely quantify the impact of a prefetcher on performance, effectively overcoming the limitations of prior metrics. To compute and leverage this metric, I-POP incorporates two key components: the Metric Collector, which periodically calculates each prefetcher's PE, and the Control Engine, which dynamically manages all prefetchers based on their PE values. Specifically, I-POP ignites (enables) prefetchers with positive PE values, adaptively tuning their aggressiveness, and disables those with non-positive PE. We evaluated I-POP on numerous workloads, and the results show I-POP outperforms two state-of-the-art approaches, Bandit and Alecto, by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4. 2 \%}$</tex> and 3.5 % across three benchmark suites in a single-core system, and 6.6 % and 8.6 % in a 16 -core system, while incurring only 1.46 KB of storage overhead.

title: Learning Similarities in EV Charging Behaviors Using Siamese Neural Networks for Idle-Time Reduction and Smart Charging Policy Design
authors: Rahul Sagwal, Sri Niwas Singh, Janakrajan Ramkumar
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: The rapid adoption of Electric Vehicles (EVs) in urban cities is creating new challenges to effective usage of existing limited charging infrastructure. Idle parking of EVs after completion of charging remains one such major bottleneck. The conventional approaches to analyze EV charging behavior often fail to capture underlying EV users' charging behavior patterns as these dominantly rely on the descriptive statistics or standard clustering. This study proposes Siamese Neural Network (SNN) based novel framework for learning similarities in EV charging behavior through similarity embeddings obtained from charging sessions of Palo Alto dataset, followed by K-Means clustering to identify distinct EV user groups based on idle-time behavior. The proposed SNN-KMeans integrated framework resulted in identification of three user groups: (i) efficient users who vacate chargers soon (low idle ratio), (ii) moderate users with partial idle behavior, and (iii) inefficient users with prolonged idle parking behavior (high idle ratio). Furthermore, efficacy of SNN integration has been validated against Agglomerative Clustering and Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) in terms of Silhouette Score. Proposed approach produced well-separated and compact clusters also evident by enhanced Silhouette Score. Improvement by nearly 29% for K-Means, 28% for Agglomerative, and significantly improved from near zero to 0.75 for GMM (K=2); a detailed comparison is also presented in the study against different numbers of clusters. Study thus provides a novel data-driven tool for identification of inefficient users, idle time reduction and enables smart charging policy recommendations to discourage overstaying while aiming at sustainable & efficient charging infrastructure utilization.

title: Integrating Biomass Forecasting with Techno-Economic Analysis for Spirulina Cultivation: A Predictive Modeling Review
authors: Hemlata Parmar, Parth Gupta, Abhinav Mukherjee, Singh Manas MukundKumar, Utsav Krishan Murari
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: This review paper provides a comparative analysis of predictive modeling and techno-economic assessment in Spirulina cultivation. As a nutrient-rich microalga, Spirulina presents a sustainable solution to global food demands, yet its commercial viability hinges on optimizing production yield while minimizing costs. A significant challenge lies in the disconnect between dynamic biological forecasting and static economic analysis. Being able to predict biomass yield early and accurately-while also tracking costs in real time-gives producers the ability to make smarter, data-driven choices that improve both efficiency and profitability. This paper traces how predictive models have evolved, moving from Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) to more advanced Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) models, and reviews techno-economic studies that highlight the main cost drivers in Spirulina cultivation. We look closely at the datasets, performance metrics, and real-world uses of these two research areas. A key gap we identify is the absence of a unified framework that connects yield prediction with cost forecasting. To address this, we propose an integrated model that links predicted biomass directly to production costs, offering a practical path forward for building intelligent, costaware decision support systems to support sustainable microalgae farming.

title: Fuzzy-Based Reliability Analysis of Machine Repairable Systems with Service Imperfections
authors: Amit Kumar, Diksha Sharma
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: This paper presents a fuzzy reliability analysis of a two-unit machine repairable systems that encompass service imperfections and parameter vagueness. The system comprises two active unit, and a single repair facility, where repairs may be either perfect or imperfect. To model real-world imprecision in failure and repair rates, fuzzy set theory is employed, representing uncertain parameters as trapezoidal fuzzy numbers. A continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) framework is used to describe system behavior, while key performance metrics-such as steady-state availability and mean time-to-failure (MTTF)-are analyzed using the α-cut method and Zadeh's extension principle. Numerical simulations illustrate the impact of imperfect repairs and fuzzy uncertainties on system performance under both optimistic and pessimistic scenarios. The proposed methodology offers a realistic and flexible approach for evaluating the dependability of repairable systems operating under uncertainty."

title: Design of Axial Flux Machine for EV Traction Considering Effects of Pole-Slot Combinations and Winding Type on Eddy Current Loss in Winding
authors: Watanabe Yu, Ren Tsunata, Masatsugu Takemoto, Jun Imai, Kosuke Izumiya, Tatsuki Saito, Tomoyuki Ueno
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: This paper offers a design guideal flux permanent synchronous machine (AFPM) for electric vehicle (EV) traction considering the eddy current loss in winding. In automotive applications, the eddy current loss in winding of traction machines tends to be large and affects electricity consumption. However, it is not easy to design AFPMs while considering the eddy current loss in winding because three-dimensional finite element analysis (3D-FEA) is required. Furthermore, the eddy current loss is strongly affected by poleslot combinations and winding type. Hence, there are almost no examples of investigation into pole-slot combinations and winding type that can realize suitable characteristics in AFPM for EV when the eddy current loss in winding is considered. In this paper, comprehensive performance such as torque and losses including the eddy current loss in winding is compared in AFPMs having different pole-slot combinations and winding type using 3D-FEA. In this investigation, the round copper and rectangular copper wires are compared. The cross-sectional area of each wire is different because the number of series and parallel circuits changes depending on the pole-slot combination, resulting in different eddy current loss in winding. Finally, this paper concludes that an AFPM using round copper wire with 16-pole 18slot (16p18s) combination is a suitable candidate based on 3D-FEA results. Additionally, experimental verifications using a prototype of the AFPM show its high performance.

title: Shelf Image Analysis Using YOLO11 and YOLOv12 for Product Recognition
authors: Saminathan R, T Bagyammal
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Effective product identification and inventory tracking by analyzing the shelves are vital for streamlining operations, better profitability, and improved customer experience in the retail supermarkets. The aim of this study is to identify and count the number of products on the shelves using annotated images of 27 product class labels in Grozi-3.2k dataset, which has a diverse set of images in varied angles and layouts of shelves. The latest models of the You Only Look Once (YOLO) algorithm, such as YOLO11 and YOLOv12 in Small (S) and Medium (M) variants were used for training the dataset and Nvidia A100 Graphical Processing Units (GPUs) in Google Colab was used as the hardware configuration for training this model. It was observed that the YOLO11-S model achieved better results when compared to other models with 0.7962 for mAP@0.5, 0.79 for precision, and a test accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 4. 8 9 \%}$</tex> for IoU@0.5, which is closely followed by the YOLOv12-S model, whereas the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$M$</tex> variants of both models lagged compared to the S variants in performance. This work highlights the YOLO model's potential to be successful in real-time retail inventory management at the supermarkets when compared to manual identification and counting of the products. This study lays the foundation for further advancements in the field of AI-driven systems for retail inventory management.

title: Carecast: Predictive Modeling For Two Chronic Diseases Using Machine Learning
authors: Urmila N, Srinivasa Harshavardhana N, M S Srividya
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Chronic diseases are a leading cause of mortality, disability, and economic burden worldwide, often requiring long-term care. Conventional diagnostic methods frequently fail to provide early detection, resulting in delayed interventions and higher healthcare costs. This study presents a Machine Learning-based framework for the early detection of high-impact chronic diseases, specifically Type 2 Diabetes and Heart Disease. The framework analyzes health, lifestyle, and demographic information to offer personalized risk predictions through a web-based interface, enabling accessible and proactive non-invasive healthcare. Separate structured datasets from Kaggle were used for each disease. The Type 2 Diabetes dataset contained 169,651 records, with only 8.66% representing diabetic cases, indicating considerable class imbalance, while the Heart Disease dataset contained 40,954 records. The proposed framework achieved 98.98% accuracy for Type 2 Diabetes using a Stacked Ensemble with LightGBM and Logistic Regression and SMOTE, and 93.30 % accuracy for Heart Disease using a Cascading Ensemble of Random Forest and CatBoost using TabNet feature selection. Comparisons with alternative resampling techniques and stacking ensembles for Type 2 Diabetes, and multiple cascading ensembles for Heart Disease, show that the proposed Stacking Ensemble using LightGBM and Logistic Regression with SMOTE and the cascading ensemble of Random Forest and CatBoost achieve the highest predictive performance.

title: Predicting Network Congestion in Mobile Networks: A Machine Learning Approach for Traffic Management
authors: Sonam Sharma, Deepanshu Patial, Anjali Goswami
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Mobile data usage expansion has propelled severe network congestion management issues which create service performance declines and longer responses together with lost connections. The goal of this study is to use ML methods for mobile network congestion prediction. Based on analyzing historical traffic information, signal strength, network parameters, we come up a predictive model, which can predict the congestion events in real-time. The model reliably uses traffic load information combined with network utilization data and environmental parameters which leads to 98 prediction accuracy of congestion events. Through this system traffic management becomes possible early on for better user experiences by transferring network resources dynamically. The experimental analysis demonstrates that implementing this model results in considerable advancement of predictive capabilities alongside traffic congestion reduction. The research results will help optimize mobile network infrastructure because 5G and its successor networks will experience unprecedented explosive demand.

title: Report on the 16th Conference and Labs of the Evaluation Forum (CLEF 2025): Experimental IR Meets Multilinguality, Multimodality, and Interaction
authors: Jorge Carrillo-de-Albornoz, Guglielmo Faggioli, Nicola Ferro, Alba García Seco de Herrera, Julio Gonzalo, Laura Plaza, Josiane Mothe, Florina Piroi, Paolo Rosso, Damiano Spina
abstract: None

title: 6.5–20-GHz Reconfigurable Phased-Array Receive Beamformer Utilizing Switchable Networks for 6G Applications
authors: Yingtao Zou, Gabriel M. Rebeiz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a 6.5–20-GHz reconfigurable phased-array receive (RX) beamformer channel in the GlobalFoundries (GF) 22-nm FD-SOI, consisting of a low-noise amplifier (LNA), a 5-bit phase shifter (PS), and a 5-bit variable gain amplifier (VGA). The reconfigurable channel has three different band configurations, utilizing amplifiers with various switchable networks and a switchable quadrature all-pass filter (QAF) for I/Q generation. The channel results in a measured gain of 19–24 dB, with 3-dB bandwidths (BW) of 6.5–9.5 GHz, 9.5–14.5 GHz, and 14–20 GHz for three different bands. A measured noise figure (NF) of 3.4–4.8 dB and an input 1-dB compression point (IP1dB) of −25 to −23 dBm are achieved for 6.5–20 GHz, with a gain tuning range of 17.5–18.5 dB and a DC power consumption of 75 mW. The measured rms phase and gain errors are, respectively, <3° and <0.5 dB at 6.5–18 GHz, and <5° and <0.8 dB at 6.5–20 GHz. This reconfigurable beamformer demonstrates performance comparable to state-of-the-art wideband beamformers, suitable for 6G FR3 applications.

title: Safe Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Privacy-Aware Edge-Device Collaborative DNN Inference
authors: Hong Wang, Xuwei Fan, Zhipeng Cheng, Yachao Yuan, Minghui Min, Minghui Liwang, Xiaoyu Xia
abstract: As Deep Neural Network (DNN) inference becomes increasingly prevalent on edge and mobile platforms, critical challenges emerge in privacy protection, resource constraints, and dynamic model deployment. This paper proposes a privacy-aware collaborative inference framework, in which adaptive model partitioning is performed across edge devices and servers. To jointly optimize inference delay, energy consumption, and privacy cost under dynamic service demands and resource constraints, we formulate the joint problem as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) that integrates model deployment, user-server association, model partitioning, and resource allocation. We propose a Hierarchical Constrained Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization with Lagrangian relaxation (HC-MAPPO-L) algorithm, a safe reinforcement learning-based framework that enhances Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) with adaptive Lagrangian dual updates to enforce long-term delay constraints. To ensure tractability while maintaining coordination, we decompose the CMDP into three hierarchically structured policy layers: an auto-regressive based model deployment policy, a Lagrangian-enhanced user association and model partitioning policy, and an attention-based resource allocation policy. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that HC-MAPPO-L consistently satisfies stringent delay constraints while achieving a superior balance between energy consumption and privacy cost, outperforming representative baseline algorithms across varying problem scales and resource configurations.

title: Compact Attention-Augmented Neural Decoder for Chaos-Based Wireless Communications
authors: Marco Siino, Stefano Mangione, Ilenia Tinnirello
abstract: Chaos-based modulation offers strong interference resilience but decoding remains challenging under low SNRs and fading channels. Existing deep learning receivers achieve promising results but are often too large for resource-constrained systems. We propose Ultra-CAN, an ultra-light convolutional–attention decoder for Chaos Shift Keying (CSK) that combines efficient convolutional feature extraction with a compact attention block to capture long-range dependencies. Our design achieves superior Bit Error Rate (BER) performance compared to state-of-the-art demodulators in AWGN and Rayleigh channels, with a footprint of 220.4 kB. Results demonstrate the viability of compact attention-based decoders for chaotic communications.

title: A Low-Latency Decoder for Sliding-Window FEC on ARM-FPGA Heterogeneous Systems_supp1-3670155.mp4
authors: Ye Li
abstract: Sliding-window forward erasure correction (FEC) can provide reliable and low-latency packet transmissions over long-distance lossy channels such as those in space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGINs). However, software-based FEC decoding suffers from high latency, which has become a critical performance bottleneck. The bottleneck primarily stems from the intensive computational cost of Galois field arithmetic and the waiting delay of collecting packets to start decoding. To address this problem, this paper presents a heterogeneous FPGA-ARM implementation of a low-latency FEC decoder which can be naturally embedded in communication systems. We implement an on-the-fly Gaussian elimination (OF-GE) algorithm that processes packets upon arrival, together with multi-lane parallelized Galois field arthmetic based on dual-half-lookup-table that reduces BRAM consumption. Experimental results on a Zynq-7020 SoC demonstrate that the proposed decoder reduces 77% latency and achieves 3.4x decoding speed compared to software implementation.

title: Accelerating Pharmaceutical Innovation through AI: A Systematic Review of Machine Learning and Deep Learning Technologies for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction
authors: Zeynep Barut, Turgay Tugay Bilgin
abstract: Drug–Drug Interactions (DDIs) pose a significant challenge to patient safety and therapeutic efficacy. Traditional experimental methods for detecting DDIs are labor-intensive, costly, and cannot scale to the vast number of potential drug combinations. To address these challenges, Artificial Intelligence (AI) approaches—particularly Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL)—have emerged as effective computational tools for predicting DDIs and supporting clinical decision-making. This study systematically reviews the application of ML and DL methods to DDI prediction based on the available English-language literature, evaluating their contributions, methodological strengths, limitations, and future opportunities. Following PRISMA guidelines, a Systematic Literature Review (SLR) of 75 primary studies was conducted, analyzing commonly used datasets, model architectures, algorithms, and evaluation metrics, with an emphasis on clinical relevance and potential to prevent adverse drug events. Similarity-based, network-based, and deep learning approaches dominate the field. While DL models often achieve higher predictive accuracy, they face challenges such as overfitting and limited interpretability, reducing their clinical trustworthiness and adoption in real-world settings. AI-driven methods have significantly advanced DDI prediction and demonstrate strong potential to enhance drug safety monitoring and clinical decision support. However, critical gaps remain, particularly in applying advanced AI paradigms such as transfer learning and multi-agent systems. Future research should prioritize larger, more balanced datasets and improved interpretability to ensure reliable, clinically actionable outcomes and foster wider adoption in healthcare practice.

title: Dynamic Path Planning for Unmanned Ground Vehicles Based on LSTM and Distributed PPO in Off-road Environments
authors: Qingyun Liu, Xiong You, Jian Yang, Jiwei Zuo, Xin Zhang, Xiangtian Bai, Tingting Li, Jia Li
abstract: Path planning for unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) in off-road environments faces challenges such as inadequate trafficability assessment, local optima issues, and limited dynamic obstacle avoidance. This paper presents a dynamic path planning algorithm called LSTM-DPPO, which combines long short-term memory (LSTM) and distributed proximal policy optimization (DPPO). Firstly, a UGV trafficability map was created that incorporates three environmental factors, which provides reliable environmental representation for UGV. Secondly, a comprehensive reward function was developed, which combines dynamic obstacle avoidance with trafficability assessment. This function facilitates the collaborative optimization of both path safety and overall trafficability. Additionally, to address the issues of ineffective dynamic obstacle avoidance and the challenges of model convergence commonly found in reinforcement learning algorithms, we introduced an LSTM network to build a dynamic obstacle prediction module. We also employed a distributed PPO architecture to enhance the training speed of the model. Finally, a comparative simulation experiment was conducted between the LSTM-DPPO algorithm and six path planning algorithms, and the generalization ability of the LSTM-DPPO algorithm was verified in three new scenarios. The results indicate that the average inference time of the complete LSTM-DPPO algorithm is 1.74ms, which meets the requirements of realtime path planning. Although its planning time is slightly longer than DPPO algorithm and CV-DPPO algorithm (Constant Velocity Model, CV), it is much faster than the other four algorithms. The LSTM-DPPO algorithm excels in obstacle avoidance, has the highest success rate, and demonstrates good generalization in new scenarios.

title: A Transfer Learning-Based State of Health Estimation Framework for Supercapacitor Systems Utilizing Tram Field Data
authors: Caiying Xu, Can Zhang, Mingxia Wu, Zhongxun An, Hengzhao Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper proposes a transfer learning-based state of health (SOH) estimation framework for the onboard super-capacitor systems utilizing the field data collected from a tram. The SOH labels of the supercapacitor systems are obtained from the selected discharging fragments of the field data. In total, 24 health features related to the power, time, current, and voltage of the supercapacitor systems are extracted from the selected charging fragments of the field data. The proposed framework builds on a light gradient boosting machine (LightGBM) model and incorporates two transfer learning strategies: a domain adaptation strategy based on transfer component analysis (TCA) and a fine tuning strategy based on dynamic learning rate adjustment. Specifically, TCA is employed to align the standardized features in the source and target domains while dynamic learning rate adjustment is implemented to accelerate the parameter tuning process. The proposed framework is evaluated using the field data of the four supercapacitor systems in a tram. On average, the SOH estimation errors are less than 1.8%, 1.5%, and 1.5% in terms of the root mean square error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), respectively, which clearly demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

title: A Novel Diagnosis Framework for Maglev Levitation Control Faults Based on Contrastive Self-Supervised Feature Learning
authors: Gao-Feng Jiang, Su-Mei Wang, Yi-Qing Ni
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The performance of levitation control is critical for maglev trains during operation. However, it is highly susceptible to external interferences, leading to faults. It is traditionally assessed solely based on levitation gap data which is often unavailable. In contrast, an acceleration-based, data-driven fault diagnosis presents a more comprehensive and reliable approach. However, such data-driven diagnosis faces challenges due to feature learning difficulties, including large datasets, limited labeled data, and experimental constraints. Consequently, a novel diagnosis framework for maglev levitation control faults is developed utilizing a contrastive self-supervised learning algorithm. The accuracy of the proposed framework is validated using accelerations collected from an in-situ condition monitoring system. Furthermore, the framework effectively extracts discriminative features associated with normal and fault conditions caused by load changes, rail irregularities, and resonance. The contrastive self-supervised learning algorithm additionally offers insights into the underlying features of the data, and the model classifies with accuracy comparable to that of fully supervised models, facilitating feature learning from large-scale unlabeled datasets.

title: Dual Consistency Learning Network for Multimodal Semi-supervised Oriented Ship Detection
authors: Fei Wu, Hongsen Zhang, Wei Wu, Jian Xu, Yimu Ji, Xiao-Yuan Jing
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: 抓取摘要失败: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='ieeexplore.ieee.org', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: /document/11421457/ (Caused by ProtocolError('Connection aborted.', ConnectionResetError(104, 'Connection reset by peer')))

title: Context2Context: A Zero-Shot SAR Image Speckle Filter_supp1-3669951.pdf
authors: Si-Wei Chen
abstract: Advanced speckle filters can well preserve textural features and fine details in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images, facilitating subsequent interpretations and applications. In recent years, a multitude of deep learning-based despeckling methods have been developed. However, most of them belong to supervised learning fashion trained on simulated datasets which may affected by the domain gap between simulated and real measured SAR data. Meanwhile, two hinds of self-supervised learning methods have been reported. The first hind mainly requires multiple SAR images of the same region for training, while these images are costly or not always available. The second hind mainly constructs lower resolution image pairs for training with sublook decomposition, which may degrade final speckle reduction performance. In order to fulfill aforementioned gaps laying in self-supervised learning methods, a zero-shot SAR image speckle filter is proposed. Its innovation contributions are three-fold. Firstly, the theoretical principle that model training with speckle-speckle image pairs is equivalent to speckle-clean image pairs is further manifested. Secondly, a similar pixel bank scheme is established which can collect sufficient similar pixels and construct plenty of independent training samples based on contextual information without necessity of multiple observation images and image resolution degradation. Finally, a zero-shot speckle filter framework is developed and well trained. Extensive experiments are performed using various SAR datasets in terms of diverse frequency bands and spatial resolutions from Radarsat-2, MiniSAR, and ALOS-2. Comparisons with state-of-the-art methods demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method. Experimental results show that the proposed Context2Context speckle filter can effectively smooth homogeneous regions while preserving target characteristics from both visual quantitative examinations effects.

title: Modeling and Mitigation of Thermal Drift in Reflective Fiber-Optic Current Sensors with a Spun Quarter-Wave Plate via Eight-State Square-Wave Modulation
authors: Jundong Tian, Shunyang Liu, Can Li, Yuan Ke, Peng Xiang, Li Xia
abstract: In reflective fiber-optic current sensors (FOCS), the polarization transfer of a quarter-wave plate (QWP) plays a decisive role in the interferometric visibility. Its temperature sensitivity can cause visibility to drift, which can lead to demodulation errors in current measurement. In this work, we derive a round-trip Jones-matrix model for a spun QWP fabricated from gradient-spun polarization-maintaining (PM) fiber. We then perform temperature and wavelength sweeps to quantify the resulting parameter drift. The results indicate that such drift can markedly change the return state of polarization, and consequently lead to visibility variations under external perturbations. Because conventional two-state and four-state square-wave schemes do not readily provide online observability of visibility variations, we propose an eight-state square-wave modulation and demodulation method. The proposed method is compatible with closed-loop current-phase feedback while enabling simultaneous estimation and compensation of half-wave voltage drift and interferometric visibility changes. Controlled heating experiments were conducted on the spun QWP from 20 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>C to 80 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>C in 10 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula>C increments, with a 10 minutes stabilization at each temperature point. The experiments were repeated under two SLD drive currents to verify robustness against optical power changes. After compensation, the full-scale normalized coefficient of variation (FS-CV) decreases from 6.37% to 0.67% (@165 mA SLD drive current), corresponding to an improvement of approximately one order of magnitude. Moreover, the temperature dependence of the projected return power measured by a polarimeter closely tracks the uncompensated current drift. This agreement indicates that the dominant thermal error originates from spun QWP induced polarization changes that modulate the interferometric visibility. Finally, simulations incorporating a 30 dB extinction ratio reproduce the observed nonlinearity. The simulated response is well described by a sinusoidal fit, which provides practical guidance for component selection and system optimization. Taken together, this work mitigates demodulation errors caused by the thermally sensitive spun QWP through coordinated modeling, algorithm design, and experimental validation. The proposed approach offers a reproducible route toward wide-temperature, high-stability fiber-optic current sensors.

title: DPIU: Dynamic Pedestrian Intention Understanding Through Cognitive Decision-Making
authors: Jiaheng Xiao, Zhihui Li, Mingxin Wang, Yu Xie, Qin Ma, Xin Wang, Yu Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Accurate prediction of pedestrian motion is crucial for autonomous driving, particularly in path planning and collision avoidance applications. Most current methods concentrate on spatiotemporal feature parameters (e.g., velocity continuity, social force parameters, and poses) extracted from historical trajectories to model pedestrian movement. However, these methodologies fail to adequately capture pedestrian intent and do not dynamically account for behavioral heterogeneity, leading to significant discrepancies with real-world observations. To address this issue, a dynamic pedestrian intention understanding (DPIU) framework is proposed, which links future intentions to historical experiences. Grounded in cognitive decision-making mechanisms derived from human physiology, the DPIU framework is designed to predict pedestrian motion by inferring inherent movement intentions. To establish a comprehensive historical perspective, a multiscale detail feature module is employed, incorporating a time-scale-based trajectory segmentation strategy to enhance the representation of pedestrian states. Subsequently, a goal intent prediction module is introduced, employing a probabilistic model to estimate pedestrians’ inclination toward the spatial scope of their intended goals. This module assesses the similarity between the current scene and historical experiences, thereby optimizing the utilization of time-fragmented information. Finally, a dynamic optimization module is developed, which superimposes intent point probabilities and applies a Bayesian-based density estimation method to ensure that the predicted outcomes closely align with real-world behaviors. Experimental evaluations on the Stanford drone drones (SDD), ETH-UCY, and ApolloScape datasets demonstrate that the proposed DPIU framework outperforms existing methods in predicting future trajectories and optimizing multimodal forecasting outcomes. The method substantially improves predictive performance in dynamic scenarios, providing a valuable tool for autonomous driving applications.

title: An Efficient Ternary In-Memory Computing Architecture with Application in Phonocardiogram-Based Driver Stress Detection
authors: Amirhossein Fathollahi, Nima Eslami, Mohammad Hossein Moaiyeri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computing
abstract: Traffic accidents remain a leading global cause of injury and fatalities, with driver stress identified as a significant contributing factor. Traditional stress detection methods, such as ECG-based systems, suffer from bulky designs and limited battery life, restricting their practicality for real-time wearable applications. To address these limitations, this paper presents a novel ternary in-memory computing (IMC) architecture for efficient PCG-based stress classification in power-constrained wearable devices. The proposed design introduces ternary full adder and multiplier circuits based on emerging technologies, enabling energy-efficient neural network acceleration. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed ternary full adder and multiplier designs reduce overall energy consumption by 98% on average compared to existing ternary counterparts. Furthermore, implementing a 1-D neural network for efficient stress classification, the proposed design achieves a 99% reduction in energy-delay-product (EDP). The results indicate that the proposed architecture is well-suited for power constrained wearable devices in PCG-based stress detection applications.

title: Urban Air Quality Prediction with Infinite Width Convolutional Networks
authors: Mohammed Mallik, Guillaume Villemaud, Emilio Ramos Sorroche, Esteban Egea Lopez
abstract: In air-pollution studies, accurate estimation of pollutant levels at unmonitored locations is essential due to the spatial mismatch between fixed monitoring stations and experimentation. When dealing with multi pollutant datasets, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is commonly applied to extract a low rank structure, which serves as a prior reducing dimensionality and improving the accuracy of subsequent spatial prediction models. However, both classical interpolation techniques and recent deep learning approaches fail to provide reliable estimates under conditions of data sparsity and low spatial sampling rates. To address this, we present a fast and accurate infinitely wide convolutional neural network approach to perform matrix imputation for air pollutant estimation in an urban environments. Speed comes from the convolutional neural tangent kernel computed from the infinite width network whose training dynamics can be fully described by a closed form formula. Experiments on two real world air pollution datasets demonstrate that our strategy accurately estimates from uneven data and significantly outperforms traditional interpolation methods.

title: High-Performance and Energy Efficient Computing-In-CAM Design for Binary Neural Network Acceleration
authors: Sureum Choi, Fatia Uftiani Putri, Juheon Lee, Chanyeong Choi, Hasna Nur Karimah, Yeongkyo Seo
abstract: The importance of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) is increasing in various domains, including image processing and natural language processing. While powerful processors enable efficient training and validation, achieving high-throughput inference on resource-constrained embedded systems remains challenging. Binary neural networks (BNNs) simplify computation by reducing model complexity and can be efficiently implemented using content-addressable memory (CAM), which replaces convolution computation with search operations. However, prior CAM-based BNN accelerator (BNN-CAM) requires many cycles for input reordering leading to performance and energy degradation. To overcome this drawback, we propose a novel BNN-CAM that integrates a multi-bitline structure and a single-cycle reordering technique, reducing reordering cycles by sixfold while improving energy efficiency through an overwrite termination feature. The proposed design is implemented on the second convolutional layer of the LeNet-5 model using 28-nm CMOS technology. The results show a 53.4% latency improvement and a 16.2% gain in energy efficiency compared with conventional BNN-CAM, leading to a 41.0% enhancement in the figure of merit and an overall power efficiency of 417.0 TOps/W.

title: DOA-DOD Estimation for Bistatic MIMO Radar: Entropy-Based Tight Closed-Form Lower Bound
authors: Yushan Xie, Dazhuan Xu, Xiaofei Zhang, Jiaqi Li, Jinke Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The performance analysis and evaluation criteria of parameter estimation in bistatic multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) radar are critical. Current approaches primarily rely on Bayesian bounds as performance benchmarks, but their computation involves high-dimensional integration or test-point optimization, increasing complexity. Additionally, closed-form expressions lack explicit characterization of system parameter influences on estimation performance. This work presents an information entropy theory-based framework for evaluating joint direction-of arrival (DOA) and direction-of-departure (DOD) estimation errors in bistatic MIMO radar. By constructing a normalized posterior differential entropy (DE) model across signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regimes, closed-form entropy error bound (EEB) for joint estimation is derived. The model quantifies relationships between estimation accuracy and key system parameters, including antenna counts, snapshot numbers, and array aperture width. Simulations confirm EEB convergence to Cram´ er-Rao bound (CRB) at high SNR regime and the a priori bound (APB) at low SNR, establishing a unified performance benchmark. Results offer theoretical criteria for optimizing radar array configurations.

title: Guaranteed Global Optimal Path Planning Through Convex Segmentation and Homotopy guidance_supp1-3669766.mp4
authors: Xiangrong Xu
abstract: Trajectory planning and optimization in three dimensional space remains fundamental challenges in high dimensional motion planning. This paper presents ConvexSegTopoChainOpt (Csto), a trajectory optimization framework that integrates convex decomposition and topological guidance. The proposed method partitions obstacle space into a sequence of connected, collision-free convex polyhedral via geometric decomposition. Homotopy vectors are introduced to enforce topological consistency, ensuring that the trajectory traverses a continuous chain of convex regions. A global convex optimization problem is formulated using an auxiliary variable relaxation strategy, enabling efficient computation of the optimal trajectory. Csto has been implemented and validated on the Kinova Jaco2 robotic platform, demonstrating its effectiveness in physically realistic experimental settings.

title: Adaptive X-Band Permittivity Sensing Using a Waveguide-Integrated 3D-Printed All-Dielectric Slotted 1D Photonic-Crystal Resonator
authors: A. Alhaj Abbas, I. Al-Naib
abstract: This paper presents an all-dielectric, 3D-printed permittivity sensor based on a slotted one-dimensional photonic crystal (1D PhC) resonator embedded inside a standard WR90 waveguide. The basic resonator is made of alternating cyclic olefin copolymer (COC, ε<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</sub> = 2.3) and air layers forming Bragg reflectors around a central defect. Among the supported resonant modes, the even mode is utilized for sensing, as it exhibits a high electric-field confinement at the defect region with additional lower-power side lobes distributed in the COC layers along the resonator axis. The operating principle of the proposed sensor relies on introducing five thin longitudinal slots: a central slot located at the field maximum and two pairs of side slots positioned at locations where the electric field side-lobes are approximately 5 dB and 10 dB lower. By selectively filling individual slots or slot combinations with the material under test, the sensitivity can be adaptively tailored to materials with different permittivity and loss. Higher perturbation is achieved by filling slots at high-field regions in order to enhance the sensitivity for low-loss and low-permittivity materials such as polymers. In contrast, lower-power perturbation reduces the interaction with high-loss materials, which preserves resonance visibility and prevents excessive attenuation in the measured response. Moreover, each selected slot configuration produces a distinct resonance shift, enabling multi-response permittivity sensing from a single resonant mode excitation. Full-wave simulations and experimental measurements using several solid and liquid materials validate the proposed concept, demonstrating reliable material discrimination and adaptable sensitivity. A high sensitivity of 320 MHz per unit permittivity is achieved when all slots are utilized.

title: Radar-Based Hand Gesture Recognition: Leveraging Raw ADC Data through Independent Component Analysis and Spiking Neural Networks
authors: Muhammad Arsalan, Muhammad Ghufran Janjua, Umm-e- Habiba, Avik Santra, Vadim Issakov
abstract: In this paper, we introduce a novel low-power radar-based gesture sensing system specifically designed for portable devices with stringent energy efficiency requirements. Traditional approaches often rely on range-Doppler images and deep learning models, which can be computationally intensive and power-hungry. Our system directly processes raw ADC data from radar signals, employing independent component analysis (ICA) to extract essential features for gesture recognition. These features are utilized by a novel spiking neural network (SNN), which is inherently energy-efficient due to its sparse time encoding and event-driven operation. Experimental results demonstrate that our system achieves a remarkable accuracy of 99.98% while maintaining a compact model size, making it highly suitable for deployment in portable devices.

title: Analysis-Driven Synthesis: Evaluating Transformer and TimeGAN Models for Generating Realistic Energy Time Series Data
authors: Ram Krishna Singh, Shardha Porwal
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: The demand for high-quality time-series data in energy analytics and building applications is often at odds with data availability and privacy limitations. This paper proposes a Transformer generative model for generating realistic multivariate time-series data, with environmental attributes like temperature (T1–T4) and relative humidity (RH_1–RH_4) from an authentic energy dataset. The model employs positional embedding and self-attention to capture temporal patterns, trained in two data subsets for computationally efficient and generalized training. The synthetic data were tested with statistical summaries, PCA, t-SNE, fastDTW distances and loss function, with resemblance to real data both in form and distribution. The model achieved minimal reconstruction loss, with an overall Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 0.1647 and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.2560. Although temporal factors like temperatures closely followed real data, more unstable humidity values revealed greater DTW variability. These findings affirm the efficacy of the proposed model for generating high-fidelity synthetic data, applicable for privacy-preserving machine learning tasks for smart energy systems.

title: Malware Detection System-A Machine Learning approach for Real-Time Threat Analysis and Sandbox Isolation
authors: Sonali Antad, Vaidehi Jalwadi, Ravina Jagtap, Chaitanyaa Deokar, Aditya Gandhi
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: As we rely more and more on digital systems and the internet, the threat landscape has grown a lot, making users more vulnerable to different types of malware attacks. One of the most common and dangerous ways for malicious software to spread is still through Windows executable (.exe) files. Dynamic analysis is a common way to find malware. It involves running the executable file in a controlled or virtual environment to see how it acts. But this method can use a lot of resources, take a long time, and be dangerous, especially if the isolation is broken. The proposed system gets around these problems by using a new static analysis-based malware detection framework that aims to make things safer and more efficient. At the heart of this system is a classification model that uses machine learning and the XGBoost algorithm. This model works by looking at structural and metadata-based features taken from the Portable Executable (PE) header of .exe files. A total of 19,615 Windows PE files were analyzed using 27 static metadata features extracted via the pefile library, including header sizes, security flags, and entry points carefully selected for stability, relevance, and resistance to manipulation. The system looks at patterns and traits in the files that are statistically linked to either good or bad behaviour without actually running them. This method makes sure that the analysis is quick, simple, and doesn't need the extra work or risks that come with running files. A secure Docker-based sandboxing environment is also added to make the system better at dealing with files that are suspicious or unknown. This separate container lets advanced users or analysts open, look at, and further investigate files that seem suspicious without putting the main operating system's security or integrity at risk.

title: Coordinated DC-Link and Rotor Control for Power Smoothing with Rotor Stress Analysis in PMSG Wind Turbines
authors: Gyumin Hwang, Jungsoo Jang, Chaelim Kim, Hyunwook Kim, Umar Fitra Ramadhan, Minhan Yoon
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: This paper proposes a coordinated control strategy that combines rotor speed control and DC-link voltage control to mitigate power fluctuations in PMSG-based wind turbines. In the proposed method, the rotor handles slow, low-frequency variations, while the DC-link capacitor suppresses fast, highfrequency changes, enabling stable output without the need for external energy storage. Simulation results show that the coordinated control outperforms either method used alone in reducing fluctuations, while maintaining similar levels of mechanical stress on the rotor. This approach improves power quality and grid stability, with future work planned for application to multiturbine wind farms.

title: KPPENet: A Lightweight C2f and Self-Attention Based Network for Accurate Tiger Key Point Pose Estimation
authors: Ch Ravindra, N Sirisha, P Uma Maheswari, V G S Abishikth, Y Anusha, K Srinivas
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: Pose estimation of wild animals, particularly tigers, presents unique challenges due to complex body articulation, varying fur patterns, and occlusions in natural habitats. This paper proposes a deep learning-based framework for tiger pose estimation, leveraging a modified YOLOv8 architecture optimized for small and irregular poses. The model integrates a lightweight yet expressive backbone composed of GhostConv, C2f, and C3TR modules to enhance semantic feature representation while maintaining computational efficiency. The neck module employs feature pyramid concatenation and up sampling to effectively fuse multi-scale information, while the head incorporates a refined C2f-based structure for accurate localization of key points. A dedicated Pose head predicts the positions of critical joints with high spatial precision. Experimental results on a curated dataset of tiger images show that the proposed architecture outperforms existing state-of-the-art pose estimation methods in terms of accuracy and robustness. The proposed model yields a high mean Average Precision (mAP) of 98.5%, an inference speed of 96.16 FPS, and a GFlops of only 8.1.

title: Computer Vision Intelligence for Medication Safety: Pill Instance Segmentation in Philippine Healthcare
authors: Cheryll Eliza G. Kiwang, Justine Nicolo D. Dimagiba, Maui Michelin V. Battung, Lysa V. Comia
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Medication errors arising from pill misidentification remain a significant concern in healthcare, particularly in the Philippines where generic and branded medicines frequently share similar visual characteristics. This study introduces a computer vision-based system for automated identification and classification of locally distributed pharmaceutical pills using the YOLOv12 instance segmentation model. A localized dataset consisting of 20 Philippine medicine classes and approximately 6,000 high-resolution images was curated and annotated using Roboflow's smart segmentation tools. The proposed model achieved a segmentation mAP@0.50:0.95 of 0.935 and a near-perfect mAP@0.50 of 0.980, demonstrating strong accuracy, generalization capability, and robustness under varied lighting conditions and partial occlusions. These results outperform several existing international pill-recognition systems while addressing regionspecific challenges in the Philippine pharmaceutical landscape. The model's efficiency, precision, and deployment readiness highlight its potential for real-world use in medication verification, supporting patient safety initiatives through accessible, AI-driven, mobile-compatible healthcare solutions tailored to low-resource environments.

title: Enhanced Spectral and Power Efficiency in MIMO f-OFDM Systems Using Hybrid LS-PCHIP Channel Estimation with Jakes Fading Conditions
authors: Manasa S, Swetha Rani L
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
