title: Understanding Data Influence With Differential Approximation
authors: Haoru Tan, Sitong Wu, Xiuzhe Wu, Wang Wang, Bo Zhao, Zeke Xie, Gui-Song Xia, Xiaojuan Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Data plays a pivotal role in the groundbreaking advancements in artificial intelligence. The quantitative analysis of data significantly contributes to model training, enhancing both the efficiency and quality of data utilization. However, existing data analysis tools often lag in accuracy. For instance, many of these tools even assume that the loss function of neural networks is convex. These limitations make it challenging to implement current methods effectively. In this paper, we introduce a new formulation to approximate a sample's influence by accumulating the differences in influence between consecutive learning steps, which we term Diff-In. Specifically, we formulate the sample-wise influence as the cumulative sum of its changes/differences across successive training iterations. By employing second-order approximations, we approximate these difference terms with high accuracy while eliminating the need for model convexity required by existing methods. Despite being a second-order method, Diff-In maintains computational complexity comparable to that of first-order methods and remains scalable. This efficiency is achieved by computing the product of the Hessian and gradient, which can be efficiently approximated using finite differences of first-order gradients. We assess the approximation accuracy of Diff-In both theoretically and empirically. Our theoretical analysis demonstrates that Diff-In achieves significantly lower approximation error compared to existing influence estimators. Extensive experiments further confirm its superior performance across multiple benchmark datasets in three data-centric tasks: data cleaning, data deletion, and coreset selection. Notably, our experiments on data pruning for large-scale vision-language pre-training show that Diff-In can scale to millions of data points and outperforms strong baselines.

title: Metal Oxide Nanowire Synaptic Transistor for Self-Powered Visual Tactile Human&#x2013;Machine Interaction System_supp1-3667497.pdf
authors: Fengyun Wang
abstract: The development of tactile human–machine interaction (T-HMI) technology has received widespread attention due to its potential to enhance the operational realism and expand application scenarios of HMI systems. However, a key challenge remains in converting dynamic tactile signals into intuitive and persistent feedback. To address this, a visual-T-HMI (VT-HMI) system with real-time visual display capability is proposed, which integrates a self-powered triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) array and InGaZnO nanowire (NW) transistor array for constructing its bionic artificial tactile sensing component, and a visualization module to display the accurate tactile information. In this system, the TENG array responds sensitively to tactile signals, and the synaptic transistor array preprocesses the tactile responses generated by the TENG, enabling neuromorphic preprocessing of the tactile responses, which facilitates the subsequent decoding and leads to a recognition accuracy exceeding 90% for tactile signals. The designed visual-T-HMI system can be utilized to achieve the control of virtual chess movement directions and the reproduction of handwritten letter trajectories, with excellent recognition accuracy (>90%) for tactile signals. This work provides a promising strategy for developing intelligent and multimodal human–machine interaction systems, with potential applications in wearable electronics, remote control, and immersive interactive technologies.

title: Laser-Induced Single-Event Burnout in GaN Devices
authors: Ani Khachatrian, Stephen P. Buchner, Andrew D. Koehler, Geoffrey M. Foster, Dale P. McMorrow
abstract: Single-event effects (SEEs), specifically single-event transient (SET) and single-event burnout (SEB), in GaN high electron mobility transistors (HEMTs) and vertical GaN diodes were studied using ultrafast laser pulses. By utilizing single-photon and two-photon absorption techniques, we determined the crucial role of material defects and high electric field regions - such as gate edges in the drain access area (GaN HEMTs) or area near the edge of anode contacts (GaN vertical diodes), in enhancing single-event burnout (SEB) susceptibility. Focusing laser light on these sensitive regions significantly increases susceptibility to SEB well below the operational breakdown voltage of the device. We identified the growth-related defects areas acting as high-field "hot spots". The spatial selectivity of pulsed laser allowed targeted irradiation of these defect-rich regions. A focused laser light can induce a rise in leakage current, eventually leading to device burnout. The increased sensitivity to SEB observed in GaN material containing defects suggests that the pulsed laser can be used to screen devices for SEB vulnerability.

title: Structural Priming with High-Frequency Gradient for Mamba in UAV Object Detection_supp1-3666752.pdf
authors: Chaolan Dai
abstract: Low-altitude unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly employed in surveillance and monitoring tasks such as perimeter inspection, urban patrol, and emergency response. Object detection in these scenarios is highly challenging due to the small size of targets, frequent occlusions, cluttered backgrounds, and blurred boundaries from aerial viewpoints. Existing detectors primarily emphasize semantic abstraction, while overlooking structural cues such as edges and contours, which are often critical for precise localization. To address this gap, we propose SPHMamba (Structural Priming with High Frequency Gradient for Mamba), a boundary-aware detection framework that explicitly integrates semantic and structural information. A dual-stream representation module first extracts complementary features through a Semantic Stream and a Structural Stream, yielding both detection predictions and modulation matrices. These semantic and high-frequency structural priors are subsequently injected into a Structure-Primed Mamba Block (SPMB), where the selective scan mechanism adaptively propa gates boundary-sensitive information and suppresses background noise, producing a High-Probability Correction Matrix (HPCM). The refined representation is then incorporated by a Boundary Refinement Module (BRM), optimized with a Structural Alignment Loss, to enhance localization precision and achieve tighter alignment with object contours. Extensive experiments on challenging UAV benchmarks demonstrate that SPHMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art detectors in both detection accuracy and boundary localization, providing an efficient and generalizable solution for intelligent UAV-based monitoring in complex environments.

title: Reachable Set of a Planar Vertical Take-Off and Landing Model With Applications to Quadcopters_supp1-3662611.pdf
authors: Aaron Becker
abstract: Quadcopters are increasingly popular for robotics applications. Being able to efficiently calculate the set of positions reachable by a quadcopter within a time budget enables collision avoidance and pursuit-evasion strategies. This article examines the set of positions that a quadcopter can reach within a specified time limit using a planar vertical take-off and landing (PVTOL) model for 2-D quadcopter dynamics. This popular model is used to determine the set of candidate optimal control sequences to build the full 3-D reachable set at final time <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$T$</tex-math> </inline-formula> in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(x,z,\theta)$</tex-math> </inline-formula> phase space or rotated to form the reachable set in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(x,y,z)$</tex-math> </inline-formula> Cartesian space. We calculate the analytic equations that exactly bound the set of positions reachable in a given time horizon for all initial conditions. We use these bounds to: escape a bounded region, avoid a collision, find the collision set, determine the closest point to the reachable set, reach a goal <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(x,z)$</tex-math> </inline-formula> in the reachable set, and for drone countermeasures.

title: Toward Safe and Energy-Efficient 5G NR V2X Communications in Rural Environments
authors: Zhanle Zhao, Son Dinh-Van, Yuen Kwan Mo, Siddartha Khastgir, Matthew D. Higgins
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Connected braking can reduce fatal collisions in connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs) by using reliable, low-latency 5G New Radio (NR) links, especially NR Sidelink Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X). In rural areas, road side units are sparse and power-constrained, so energy efficiency must be considered alongside safety. This paper studies how three communication control factors including subcarrier spacing (SCS), modulation and coding scheme (MCS), and transmit power (Pt) should be configured to balance safety and energy consumption in rural scenarios in light and heavy traffic scenarios. Safety is quantified by the packet receive ratio (PRR) against the minimum communication distance Dcomm, defined as the distance that the vehicle travels during the transmission of the safety message. Results show that, under heavy traffic, increasing Pt and selecting a low-rate MCS at SCS = 30 kHz sustains high PRR at Dcomm, albeit with higher energy cost. In light traffic, maintaining lower Pt with low MCS levels achieves a favorable reliability-energy trade-off while preserving acceptable PRR at Dcomm. These findings demonstrate the necessity of adaptive, energy-aware strategy to guarantee both safety and energy efficiency in rural V2X systems.

title: Observer-based Nonlinear Consensus Control of Virtually Coupled Heterogeneous Trains Incorporating Train-following Interactions
authors: Zhongqi Li, Zhenyu Guo, Hui Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: To address the control challenges of virtual coupling (VC) for heterogeneous permanent magnetic maglev trains with time-varying delays and uncertain disturbances, an observer-based distributed nonlinear consensus controller is proposed for a third-order nonlinear model. Specifically, a state observer is utilized to estimate the leader’s acceleration information, thereby reducing the communication burden. To compensate for disturbances, a fixed-time-convergence disturbance observer is proposed and theoretically demonstrated. In particular, the train-following interactions are implemented to avoid negative spacing errors as well as unreasonable acceleration and deceleration. Subsequently, the single-train and string stability are analyzed, and an upper bound on the allowed communications delay is provided. Finally, simulations demonstrate that the proposed controller exhibits strong robustness and anti-interference performance, enabling the VC to operate safely, comfortably, and reliably in the presence of communication delays and disturbances.

title: FlexiGen : An Automated AI Accelerator Generation Framework With Decoupled-Access-Execute and Dynamic Dataflows
authors: Xiaoling Yi, Man Shi, Joren Dumoulin, Jiacong Sun, Yunhao Deng, Ryan Antonio, Fanchen Kong, Yu Zhang, Yuqi Jiang, Marian Verhelst
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Modern tensor applications, especially artificial intelligence (AI) applications, are evolving rapidly, posing a significant demand for agile hardware design. While numerous hardware generators have been developed, they suffer from three significant limitations: 1) they are either limited to a single dataflow/data type generation, failing to cater to the computational requirements of diverse workloads; 2) or focus only on the array level optimization, omitting system-level effects, such as the influence of on-chip memory bandwidth and contention; and 3) customized workload mapping/configuration is needed, resulting in increased programming complexity. To address these challenges, we propose FlexiGen, a flexible and extensible hardware generation framework, which targets diverse deep neural networks (DNN) tensor applications and can generate a complete synthesizable acceleration system at the RTL level with arbitrary dataflow and its combinations. Our key contributions are threefold: 1) we incorporate decoupled-access-execute architecture inside FlexiGen, enabling full system generation while maintaining flexibility and efficiency; 2) we propose a versatile spatial core generator that supports dynamic spatial dataflows and multiple data precisions in the same array and a compatible data streaming engine generator that can support arbitrary temporal dataflows and $N$ -dimensional data access patterns; and 3) we leverage a uniform programming interface and provide a customized kernel library, enabling agile configuration programming. We conduct an intensive evaluation to demonstrate the versatility of FlexiGen in dataflow accelerator generation and show the trade-offs of performance, area, and power across a wide range of dataflows and workloads at both the array level and system level. Our case study experiment shows FlexiGen’s usefulness as a hardware generator to rapidly generate desired dataflow acceleration systems. Compared with the state-of-the-art (SotA) hardware generation framework LEGO, FlexiGen achieves 36.79% and 57.16% less area and power when generating the same dual spatial dataflow design. FlexiGen is open-source and available at https://github.com/KULeuven-MICAS/snax_cluster

title: A Unified Framework for Temporal Similarity: Coupling Time-Scale Effects and Composite Temporal Objects in Geoscientific Retrieval
authors: Zugang Chen, Xinyu Chen, Kai Wu, Linhan Yang, Hang Feng, Jing Li, Guoqing Li, Hengliang Guo, Shaohua Wang
abstract: Temporal similarity is critical for retrieving and integrating geoscientific data. Existing approaches often neglect the effect of time scale and lack efficient models for handling composite temporal objects composed of multiple points and intervals. To address these limitations, we propose a unified temporal similarity framework that explicitly incorporates both time-scale effects and composite temporal topology.To evaluate the scale-sensitive model, we compile the Geoscientific Data Retrieval and Ranking Dataset (GDRRD), containing 105 datasets organized into themed query groups with expert rankings. On GDRRD, our method improves the Spearman correlation between system ranking and expert ranking (from 0.905 to 0.976 compared with a representative baseline) and shows superior discrimination for disjoint relations under decade-scale and century-scale settings.To extend the framework to composite time, we introduce a sparse matrix representation based on temporal transitivity and a most-similar matching model that aggregates topological and metric similarities with length-weighted integration. Experiments on composite-time cases derived from gridded population density datasets (2000–2015, 1 km resolution) and remote sensing image sequences demonstrate that our method reduces redundant relation storage by over 40%, while yielding more precise similarity scores for complex temporal queries such as “2000, 2005, 2010” versus “2000–2010”.By jointly achieving scale-awareness and form-completeness, the proposed framework provides a robust foundation for temporal similarity computation, enabling more accurate and rational retrieval in large-scale geoscientific data repositories.

title: Special Topic on Modeling and Simulation of Emerging Materials, Devices, and Circuits for Energy-Efficient Computing
authors: Sumeet Gupta
publish: IEEE Journal on Exploratory Solid-State Computational Devices and Circuits

title: Mean Square Exponential Stability of Dynamic Memristor Neutral Stochastic Cellular Neural Networks With Time-Varying Delays
authors: Song Zhu, Kun Deng, Huaicheng Yan, Mouquan Shen, Xiaoyang Liu, Shiping Wen
abstract: This article investigates the mean square exponential stability for dynamic memristor-neutral stochastic cellular neural networks with time-varying delays (DM-NSDCNNs). Unlike general neural networks (NNs) analyzed in the voltage–current domain, DM-NSDCNNs are studied in the flux-charge domain, offering a significant advantage: all current, voltage, and power consumption vanish when the system reaches a steady state. In particular, dynamic memristor store the results of computation. To better utilize these properties, two distinct stochastic stability analysis techniques are considered, depending on the memristor’s constitutive relations. For piecewise linear constitutive relation, the stability criteria are obtained by a novel approach based onthe comparison principle and reductio ad absurdum. Moreover, the stability criteria for cubic nonlinear constitutive relation are established via stochastic analysis employing Lyapunov functional techniques. Finally, several numerical examples with different constitutive relations of DM-NSDCNNs are provided to verify the effectiveness and potential of the proposed results.

title: A Generic Transient DC Bias Elimination Method for Dual- and Triple-Active Bridge Converters
authors: Hui Cao, Peyman Darvish, Xianfeng Jiang, Yue Zhao
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Conventional single-phase-shift (SPS) modulation developed for dual-active bridge (DAB) converters can induce a significant DC bias current during transient due to the abrupt update of the phase-shift angle. This DC component in the transformer’s AC current may result in serious saturation of the magnetic components. Though many modulation strategies have been proposed for DAB converters, the DC bias current during the transient cycle can only be reduced but not fully eliminated. In this work, an intermediate phase-shift (IPS) modulation method is proposed to effectively eliminate the DC bias current during the transient cycle without scarifying simplicity. The proposed method is generic, which can be applied not only to the DAB, but also the multi-port DC-DC converters, such as a triple-active bridge (TAB) converter. Both theoretical analysis and experimental studies for DAB and TAB are presented to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: CUE-YOLO: An Efficient and Robust Detector for Metal Surface Defect Detection with Adaptive Attention and Task Alignment
authors: Xiaonian Yang, Tingcheng Li, Tingting Yang, Shuchen Ding, Zhengyang Lyu, Dong Li, Zhe Lu
abstract: Reliable detection of surface defects on metal materials is critical for ensuring product integrity and maintaining high manufacturing standards. However, existing object detection models often struggle with (a) subtle defect representation, (b) classification–localization misalignment, and (c) severe class imbalance, particularly in industrial scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose CUE-YOLO, a task-driven architectural refinement of the YOLOv11 framework, specifically tailored for efficient industrial surface defect inspection. Specifically, the novel C3K2 with Adaptive Sampling Attention (C3K2-ASA) module introduces an attention-guided feature refinement strategy to amplify subtle and fine-grained defect patterns. The designed Unified Task-Aligned Head (UTAH) mitigates task misalignment by harmonizing classification and localization branches through a unified prediction structure. Furthermore, the proposed Exponential Moving Average SlideLoss (EMA-SlideLoss) dynamically adjusts IoU thresholds to provide adaptive supervision, particularly for low-confidence detections and instances from underrepresented classes, thereby improving robustness in the presence of class imbalance. Experiments on the challenging GC10-DET dataset demonstrate that CUE-YOLO achieves a state-of-the-art performance of 74.2% mAP, outperforming the YOLOv11 baseline by 11.9%. Cross-dataset evaluations on aluminum and NEU-DET further verify the generalization ability of the proposed method across diverse industrial domains.

title: Decision-Oriented Fixed-Time Control for Multi-USVs in Pursuit–Evasion Game Subject to Exogenous Disturbances
authors: Yuan Zhao, Jiapeng Liu, Cheng Fu, Hak-Keung Lam, Jinpeng Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This article investigates the pursuit–evasion (PE) game under the pure pursuit (or evasion) strategy for multi-uncrewed surface vehicles (USVs), along with the decision-oriented fixed-time velocity regulation controller (FTVRC) design. A novel exponential-type function approximation (ETFA) method is utilized to construct a differentiable performance index (PI), which reflects the decision-making basis for the player. Based on the differentiable PI, the player autonomously decides to generate the expected pursuit and evasion velocity, which includes size and direction information. To enable the player to execute its decision result, the FTVRC with a simple structure and low computational burden is designed to track the generated expected velocity. To cope with the unknown exogenous disturbances, a fixed-time disturbance observer (FTDO) is proposed to estimate the exogenous disturbances in real-time. According to the Lyapunov theory, all errors can achieve fixed-time convergence and the settling time is upper bounded. Finally, the simulation results show that the control scheme proposed in this article can achieve the PE target, and the superiorities of the ETFA method and the fixed-time control (FTC) method are demonstrated in the comparative simulations.

title: Measurement-Based Large-Scale Indoor Channel Modeling for Hovering UAV-to-Ground Wireless Links
authors: Haythem Bany Salameh, Osamah Mulhem, Hamed Al Meqbaali, Ibrahim Salem, Malik Mohamed Umar
abstract: Indoor autonomous drones rely on robust wireless connectivity to operate in GPS-denied environments; however, indoor radio-frequency (RF) propagation is strongly affected by multipath, blockage, and device instability. These effects become particularly critical for hovering unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), known as drones, where vibration and orientation variations can significantly alter the wireless channel. This paper presents a measurement-based characterization of large-scale indoor RF propagation, explicitly comparing stationary ground-to-ground links with hovering UAV-to-ground links under identical indoor conditions. Using a purpose-built ESP32-based measurement platform operating at 2.4 GHz, extensive RSSI and RSSI-referenced signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) proxy measurements are collected across a realistic office environment. Based on spatially averaged measurements, log-distance path-loss models are derived for both scenarios. Unlike prior indoor channel modelling studies that consider ground-to-ground communications, this work quantifies the effect of hovering-induced vibration and antenna orientation on large-scale indoor propagation, showing that hovering UAV-to-ground links exhibit a substantially higher path-loss exponent (2.46) than stationary ground-to-ground links (1.50), along with increased shadowing variability (approximately 6.9 dB versus 6.1 dB). In some indoor locations, UAV hovering introduces RSSI and SNR proxy degradations of up to 20 dB relative to the stationary baseline. These findings demonstrate that conventional indoor propagation models can significantly underestimate attenuation and variability in indoor UAV deployments, highlighting the need to explicitly account for hovering-induced effects when designing and planning indoor UAV communication systems.

title: MoEPlan: A Lazy Learned Query-Selection Optimizer via Mixture of Optimizer Experts
authors: Suchen Liu, Jun Gao, Yinjun Han, Yang Lin

title: Development of Novel Flight Modes for Underwater Drones
authors: Shiho Oshiro, Masaki Shibuya, Hiroaki Kawasaki
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: In this paper, we propose the development of novel flight modes for underwater drones. Currently, most underwater drones are operated manually using a controller. Although each operation method has its advantages and disadvantages depending on the intended application, our target use case is tasks such as seafloor mapping, where operation within a predefined area is required. In such cases, the movement itself is relatively simple, but human operators are still necessary. To address this issue, we developed an autonomous navigation system capable of moving in a predetermined pattern over a fixed distance, thereby reducing the need for human labor and imnroving operational efficiency. The underwater drone assumed for practical use in this study is the BlueROV2, which allows for system development and integration. The development was carried out using ArduPilot, an open-source software platform, specifically utilizing its underwater drone-dedicated code, ArduSub. The simulation environment was constructed using Mission Planner. The results of Simulations 1 to 4 are presented below. Simulation 1 was designed for applications such as seafloor mapping, and a square flight mode was developed. As a result, the drone was able to move forward the specified distance, rotate its body, and move forward again repeatedly. By repeating this sequence four times, it successfully followed the predetermined square path. Simulation 2 aimed for slightly more complex movements, and a pentagonal flight mode was developed. The drone moved the specified distance, rotated at each vertex, and successfully traced the defined pentagonal path. Simulation 3 tested whether more complex maneuvers could be achieved by developing a diamond-shaped flight mode inspired by playing cards. The drone moved forward while slightly curving, adjusted its orientation at each point, and was able to trace the diamond shape. Simulation 4 was designed to verify smooth movement under continuous curvature, for which a cardioid-shaped flight mode was developed. The drone smoothly followed a curved trajectory, successfully forming the heart-shaped pattern characteristic of a cardioid. The simulations demonstrated that it is possible to develop a system capable of autonomously executing the pre-defined flight patterns. This challenges the conventional notion that underwater drones must be operated manually using a controller. In the future, we plan to conduct experiments to verify whether the system can be implemented and operated on actual hardware. As a next step, we aim to develop coordinated operations using multiple drones for swarm control.

title: Adaptive Power Control of GFM E-STATCOM Based on Negative-Sequence SCR Estimation
authors: ChaeLim Kim, JungSoo Jang, DongHun Lee, BongGyun Son, MinHan Yoon
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: The stability of converter-dominated power systems is increasingly challenged by declining short-circuit ratio (SCR) caused by large-scale renewable energy integration. Conventional fixed-parameter control of grid-forming (GFM) enhanced static synchronous compensator (E-STATCOM) fails to provide robust voltage support under varying grid conditions. This paper presents a negative-sequence-based SCR estimation method and an adaptive reactive power control strategy for GFM ESTATCOM. The proposed method injects a small negativesequence current to estimate system impedance in real time without requiring prior network models, enabling accurate SCR evaluation even under unbalanced operating conditions. Based on the estimated SCR, the control gain is adaptively tuned through a virtual-impedance-based framework, thereby maximizing reactive power support in weak grids while preventing overcompensation in strong grids. Electromagnetic transient simulations validate the proposed approach, demonstrating precise SCR tracking, improved low-voltage ride-through capability, and superior post-fault voltage recovery compared with fixed-gain control. The results confirm the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed strategy for future weak-grid scenarios with high penetration of converter-interfaced generation.

title: Secret Caching Sauce for High-Performance Secure Memory
authors: Xu Jiang, Xueliang Wei, Yifei Qu, Dan Feng, Yulai Xie, Wei Tong
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: In modern secure memory systems, confidentiality and integrity protection add additional metadata (called security metadata) that are accessed during memory data accesses. Security metadata are cached in on-chip metadata caches to reduce the access cost. However, prior studies overlook the differences between security metadata caching and data caching. Metadata caches are managed similarly to data caches. We identify three special features of security metadata caching. Due to these features, existing management methods face two problems. First, there are frequent security metadata accesses and many metadata cache misses, because the best metric (i.e., reuse distance) used to select replacement victims in data caches is suboptimal for metadata caches. Second, the miss penalty is large since misses suffer from long security metadata update and verification chains. The two problems significantly increase data access latency, thereby degrading system performance. We propose SECRET, a security metadata oriented cache management framework. SECRET reduces accesses and misses by selecting victims based on a new metric superior to reuse distances, called the connections between cached security metadata. We identify this metric by developing an optimal replacement decision search approach and analyzing the optimal decisions. SECRET reduces the miss penalty by actively breaking update chains and parallelizing verification chains. Experimental results show that SECRET reduces the data read (write) latency by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30.2 \%(59.9 \%)$</tex> and improves system performance by 26.2 % compared with the state-of-the-art designs.

title: C³: CXL Coherence Controllers for Heterogeneous Architectures
authors: Anatole Lefort, David Schall, Nicolò Carpentieri, Julian Pritzi, Soham Chakraborty, Nicolai Oswald, Pramod Bhatotia
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: We introduce <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C}^{\mathbf{3}}$</tex>, a systematic methodology for designing Compute Express Link (CXL) coherence controllers, to overcome interoperability challenges that arise from the mismatch of coherence protocols and memory consistency models in heterogeneous CXL-connected systems. Crucially, CXL lacks a unified heterogeneous computing interface, which can lead to unpredictable and inconsistent behavior when multiple heterogeneous devices decide to share cache-coherent CXL memory. C <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$^{3}$</tex> acts as a pivotal interface between diverse heterogeneous compute units, bridging the semantic differences without necessitating disruptive changes to existing system architectures. Our approach hinges on two key principles: delegating memory operations across coherence domains and enforcing atomicity at domain boundaries, thereby preserving the native memory consistency model semantics of each unit. We implement <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C}^{\mathbf{3}}$</tex> as a generic gem5 model and validate its correctness through exhaustive litmus testing. We also show that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C}^{\mathbf{3}}$</tex> incurs minimal performance overhead compared to unified native coherence protocols.

title: The Cost of Dynamic Reasoning: Demystifying AI Agents and Test-Time Scaling from an AI Infrastructure Perspective
authors: Jiin Kim, Byeongjun Shin, Jinha Chung, Minsoo Rhu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: Large-language-model (LLM)-based AI agents have recently showcased impressive versatility by employing dynamic reasoning, an adaptive, multi-step process that coordinates with external tools. This shift from static, single-turn inference to agentic, multi-turn workflows broadens task generalization and behavioral flexibility, but it also introduces serious concerns about system-level cost, efficiency, and sustainability. This paper presents the first comprehensive system-level analysis of AI agents, quantifying their resource usage, latency behavior, energy consumption, and datacenter-wide power consumption demands across diverse agent designs and test-time scaling strategies. We further characterize how AI agent design choices, such as few-shot prompting, reflection depth, and parallel reasoning, impact accuracy-cost tradeoffs. Our findings reveal that while agents improve accuracy with increased compute, they suffer from rapidly diminishing returns, widening latency variance, and unsustainable infrastructure costs. Through detailed evaluation of representative agents, we highlight the profound computational demands introduced by AI agent workflows, uncovering a looming sustainability crisis. These results call for a paradigm shift in agent design toward compute-efficient reasoning, balancing performance with deployability under real-world constraints.

title: Long-Term Optimal Scheduling of Cascade Hydropower Stations Based on Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient
authors: Xiaorong Sun, Xueping Pan, Jieyang Zhou, Chenglong Yu, Jinpeng Guo
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: In addressing challenges posed by inflow uncertainties and complex operational conditions in cascade reservoirs, traditional mathematical model-based scheduling approaches for cascade hydropower stations faces difficulties in modeling, time-consuming computations, and poor adaptability to uncertain scenarios. Consequently, intelligent scheduling decision-making methods based on deep reinforcement learning (DRL) have garnered significant attention. However, existing Deep Q-Network (DQN) methods are unsuitable for discrete action spaces and exhibit limited precision in continuous control, while Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) methods are sensitive to hyperparameters and initial values and prone to local optima. This paper, therefore, proposes an intelligent scheduling strategy for cascade hydropower stations based on the Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) algorithm. This study establishes an optimization model that maximizes long-term power generation for cascade hydropower stations while considering hydraulic and electrical constraints. Subsequently, the long-term optimal scheduling problem is transformed into a Markov Decision Process, and a DRL-based solution method using the TD3 is proposed. Finally, comparative analyses with existing DQN and DDPG methods demonstrate that the proposed model and algorithm can determine the optimal operational strategy while strictly enforcing power balance constraints.

title: LoCaLUT: Harnessing Capacity–Computation Tradeoffs for LUT-Based Inference in DRAM-PIM
authors: Junguk Hong, Changmin Shin, Sukjin Kim, Si Ung Noh, Taehee Kwon, Seongyeon Park, Hanjun Kim, Youngsok Kim, Jinho Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: Lookup tables (LUTs) have recently gained attention as an alternative compute mechanism that maps input operands to precomputed results, eliminating the need for arithmetic logic. LUTs not only reduce logic complexity, but also naturally support diverse numerical precisions without requiring separate circuits for each bitwidth—an increasingly important feature in quantized DNNs. This creates a favorable tradeoff in PIM: memory capacity can be used in place of logic to increase computational throughput, aligning well with DRAM-PIM architectures that offer high bandwidth and easily available memory but limited logic density. In this work, we explore this capacity-computation tradeoff in LUT-based PIM designs, where memory capacity is traded for performance by packing multiple MAC operations into a single LUT lookup. Building on this insight, we propose LoCaLUT, a PIM-based design for efficient low-bit quantized DNN inference using operation-packed LUTs. First, we observe that these LUTs contain extensive redundancy and introduce LUT canonicalization, which eliminates duplicate entries to reduce LUT size. Second, we propose reordering LUT, a lightweight auxiliary LUT that remaps weight vectors to their canonical form required by LUT canonicalization with a simple LUT lookup. Third, we propose LUT slice streaming, a novel execution strategy that exploits the DRAM-buffer hierarchy by streaming only relevant LUT columns into the buffer and reusing them across multiple weight vectors. Evaluated on a real system based on UPMEM devices, we demonstrate a geometric mean speedup of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.82 \times$</tex> across various numeric precisions and DNN models. We believe LoCaLUT opens a path toward scalable, low-logic PIM designs tailored for LUT-based DNN inference. Our implementation of LoCaLUT is available at https://github.com/AIS-SNU/LoCaLUT.

title: Pulse: Fine-Grained Hierarchical Hashing Index for Disaggregated Memory
authors: Guangyang Deng, Zixiang Yu, Zhirong Shen, Qiangsheng Su, Zhinan Cheng, Jiwu Shu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: By decoupling compute and memory resources into independent pools that are provisioned and managed separately, disaggregated memory (DM) is promising to break the scaling constraints for memory systems and improve resource utilization. However, it also comes with a new challenge to design a high-performance hashing index to manage the vast memory pool with weak computing power. In this paper, we reconsider this problem and find that existing hashing indexes for DM still experience two fundamental yet unresolved limitations: (i) amplifying traffic under high insertion concurrency, and (ii) introducing significantly high insertion latency, stemming mainly from the directory synchronization and item relocation in resizing process. We resolve the above limitations by designing Pulse, a finegrained hierarchical hashing index for DM. Pulse comprises the following three design primitives. It proposes a multi-level index structure, which breaks the conventional flat directory into multiple sub-directories that are organized hierarchically, achieving fine-grained directory synchronization. Pulse also maintains a small portion of hashed keys in the memory pool, which aids in item relocation during resizing, thereby reducing resizing traffic and promising system stability. Pulse finally exploits operation parallelism by tailoring the doorbell batching mechanism with selective signaling. We conduct extensive experiments using a variety of benchmarks, showing that Pulse can improve <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.46 \times$</tex> of the throughput and reduce 76.3% of the tail latency compared to state-of-the-art hashing indexes.

title: Multi-Level Failure Risk Reduction Process of Power System Including Demand Side Management
authors: Guido Andreesen, Madis Leinakse, Mart Landsberg, Jako Kilter
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: This paper proposes a decision-making process for determining the cost-efficient failure risk reduction method for substation equipment. Its objective is to achieve a higher degree of decrease in failure risk with lower associated costs. The process takes into account the failure risk reduction methods related to the power system operator and demand side management. It incorporates four levels - power system, substation, equipment-specific, and client-based. The failure risk mitigation approaches considered are: changing the transmission system's configuration, increasing the rated capacity of substation equipment, increasing the frequency of condition assessments of equipment, and using client-side load reduction as an ancillary service. A case study is conducted for illustrating the process ability for comparing and determining the most cost-efficient risk reduction methods. According with the results, the highest cost-efficiency is achieved by lowering the failure probability of critical equipment. Increasing the substations reliability by changing their schematic can reduce the overall risk more, but this comes at a higher cost. The client-based method also indicated considerable results as an alternative option. The proposed process aids in determining the most practical and economical approach for substation and power system risk reduction. This can be a useful tool for utilities and network owners to reduce substation risk in an evolving power system.

title: Data-Driven Distributionally Robust Optimization for Multi-Microgrid Coordination with Hybrid Game
authors: Li Liu, Yong Li, Chang Li, Gang Lin, Fei Zhao, Yijia Cao
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: To coordinate the power exchange and profit allocation between multiple integrated energy microgrids (IEM) and the distribution network (DN), while solving the impact of renewable energy generation uncertainty on dispatch, a distributionally robust dispatch framework is proposed, incorporating hybrid game mechanisms and data-driven uncertainty characterization. Firstly, a Stackelberg game is formulated between the DN operator and the IEM alliance, to develop the pricing and power exchanging strategies between the IEMs and DN. A generalized Nash-bargaining model and a nonlinear profit allocation function based on the contribution in peer to peer energy transaction, are established to allocate the total profits of alliance. Secondly, the uncertainty inherent in renewable generation is managed via distributionally robust optimization, with conditional generation adversarial network employed to improve scenario representation quality. The coupled Stackelberg-DRO framework is addressed via bisection integrated with column-and-constraint generation, while the alternating direction method of multipliers resolves the generalized game formulation. Finally, comprehensive simulations on a modified IEEE 33-bus network incorporating multiple IEMs demonstrate the framework's validity and performance.

title: A Smart Helmet Solution for Mining Safety Using IoT and Wearable Sensing Technologies
authors: Ranya Riti, Jatin Dhangar, Shivam Tiwari
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Mining workers face constant threats from toxic gases, poor ventilation, high temperatures, and unexpected physical hazards, often resulting in severe injuries or fatalities. This helmet incorporates multi-modal sensors for detecting hazardous gases (CO2, CH4), temperature and humidity variations, heart rate anomalies, motion events, and helmet removal. Sensor data is processed locally using an ESP32 microcontroller and transmitted via long-range LoRa to a cloud-based dashboard with real-time alerts and visualization. Validation under simulated mining scenarios yielded a hazard detection accuracy of 95.87%, with an F1-score of 0.964 and end-to-end communication latency consistently below 150 ms, even at distances of 300 m in obstruction-rich conditions. Environmental sensing achieved high precision (R-squared = 0.988, RMSE for CO2 = 57.2 ppm), while physiological monitoring reported a mean absolute error of 2.27 bpm and ROC AUC of 0.947. The system-maintained communication reliability above 98.2%, with the 4000 mAh battery supporting 27.1 hours of continuous use. Logistic regression confirmed CO2, CH4, and heart rate thresholds as statistically significant indicators of hazardous conditions (p-value < 0.001). The proposed solution demonstrates operational robustness and suitability for deployment in high-risk industrial environments.

title: A Sustainable Model of Security Automation: Approach and Analysis
authors: Chandrasekaran K, Aditya Rathod, Usha Divakarla
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: Cybersecurity defenses must evolve as threats grow more complex and compliance requirements tighten. Traditional rule-based IDS and SIEM platforms often incur high false-positive rates and energy costs [1], [2], making long-term operation unsustainable. We propose an integrated security automation framework that embeds sustainability at its core. Our model combines AI-driven anomaly detection [2] for real-time threat identification, federated learning for privacy-preserving intelligence sharing [3], blockchain-based tamper-evident logging [4], and post-quantum cryptography to future-proof communications [6]. The architecture automates compliance with standards (e.g. NIST CSF, ISO/IEC 27001) and optimizes processing to minimize resource usage. Preliminary case studies in finance and healthcare demonstrate that this approach maintains high detection accuracy while significantly reducing false positives and energy consumption [5]. Overall, our sustainable security automation model enhances resilience and transparency without sacrificing efficiency [1], [5].

title: Multi-Objective Control Design for Grid-Following Converters
authors: Yu Zeng, Shan Jiang, Guibin Zou, Georgios Konstantinou, Josep Pou, Zhige Yuan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: This paper presents an easy transfer reinforcement learning (ETRL) method that integrates deep reinforcement learning with easy transfer learning to enable the adaptation of a multi-objective controller—initially designed for a specific grid-following converter—to other converters with different system parameters. The ETRL method enables efficient knowledge transfer between controllers, providing a scalable approach that eliminates the need for extensive data collection or hyperparameter tuning. By enhancing controller adaptability, ETRL reduces training requirements by 96.4 % while ensuring the stability of converter systems across diverse operating conditions. Experimental results validate the performance of the proposed ETRL method, offering a promising direction for advanced power electronics controller design.

title: Lightweight Blockchain Framework for Secure and Privacy-Preserving IoT Systems
authors: Selvaragavan S, Karuppasamy L, Yuvan Sankar NKR, Rajan S, Sudhakar S, Sankara Narayanan T
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) has significantly transformed critical domain such has healthcare, transportation, industry, and smart cities by connecting billions of devices that can gather and share data. However, as it grows rapidly, IoT systems are grappling with significant issues around data security, user privacy, and trust. Centralized systems are particularly at risk due to single points of failure, latency issues, and potential privacy breaches, which makes them less ideal for large-scale use. While blockchain technology provides a way to decentralize and resist tampering, traditional frameworks can be too resource-heavy and impractical for low-power IoT devices.This paper introduces a Lightweight Blockchain Framework designed for Secure and Privacy-Preserving IoT Systems. It combines energy-efficient consensus mechanisms, trust-based reputation management, and a hybrid approach to on-chain and off-chain storage. By utilizing edge-assisted processing, we can cut down on computational demands, and cryptographic techniques like elliptic curve cryptography and zero-knowledge proofs help maintain privacy during authentication. The framework is built with scalability, low latency, and effective communication in mind, making it suitable for real-time IoT applications.Simulation results show that our proposed framework can reduce communication costs by 35%, lower latency, and improve privacy protection compared to traditional blockchain-based IoT systems. These findings indicate that the framework is not only lightweight and secure but also practical for applications that need privacy and quick responses, such as in healthcare, industrial automation, and smart infrastructure.

title: Backstepping-Based Phase-Shift Control for Mitigating Voltage Variation in Dynamic Wireless Charging
authors: Milad Behnamfar, Arif Sarwat
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Dynamic wireless charging (DWC) offers a promising solution to alleviate range anxiety in electric vehicles by enabling uninterrupted charging while driving. However, fluctuations in mutual inductance during vehicle movement can lead to output voltage instability, reducing battery life and overall system performance. To address this, the paper introduces a phase shift control approach based on backstepping, which adjusts the conduction angle of the semi-active rectifier to regulate output voltage. Unlike conventional methods, this strategy results in a nearly purely resistive equivalent AC load, eliminating the need for extra control to compensate for reactive components. The proposed controller is tested under varying load conditions and reference voltages while the receiver is in motion. Results confirm its ability to suppress voltage ripple to below 1% and maintain stable output regardless of load variations.

title: SmartPlantID: A Multimodal AI System for Plant Species Identification
authors: Vikas Kamra, Anwita Sisodiya, Ayushi Shardul, Divey Khandelwal
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Plant species identification is important for agriculture, ecological research, and biodiversity preservation. The proposed work empirically examined various models that deal with vision transformers, traditional CNNs, and innovative multimodal AI systems to identify plants by using various plant organs. Comparative performance evaluation on datasets like PlantCLEF, LeafSnap, Flavia, and PlantVillage reveals three evolutionary phases: the initial dominance of deep CNNs on controlled datasets, the use of Vision Transformers for increased efficiency, and more recent multimodal approaches that address real-world complexity. Multimodal systems struggle with scalability issues but are more resilient to a variety of plant properties than deep CNNs, which are quite accurate in lab settings. The analysis presents the automatic fusion strategies of the SmartPlantID framework for useful next-generation botanical recognition systems and provides conclusive suggestions for choosing the best identification techniques.

title: IntelliVend Algorithm Based FSM Model for Book Vending Machine
authors: Anjusha Ashish Mahambrey, Putcha Pasyanthi, Paramasivam C.
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: The setting of book vending machines in libraries is of great importance because they offer easy access to reading materials that encourage learning outside of regular library hours. This research uses IntelliVend Algorithm-based RTL(Register Transfer Level)design of a book vending machine which uses Finite State Machine (FSM). This dual purpose Vending Machine has the ability to dispense a book while buying or has a book drop system while renting. This design has a variety of user actions such as book selection, payment processing, rental tracking, and change return. Cadence Incisive tool is used for simulation followed by Genus for synthesis and Innovus for physical design, including placement and routing, to meet the design's timing, power and area constraints. Overall the design provides a reliable and efficient solution for users to purchase or rent books from the machine.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Power Flow Solver for Three-Phase Unbalanced Distribution Networks
authors: M. Furqan Azam, Chris Hermans, Ghulam Mohy-ud-din, Geert Deconinck
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Power flow analysis is fundamental for the reliable operation and planning of modern distribution networks (DNs). However, conventional iterative numerical solvers are often computationally intensive, especially for large networks. In this work, we propose a Physics-Informed Graph Neural Network (PIGNN-<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \Phi$</tex>) approach to speed-up the power flow calculations in three-phase unbalanced DNs. The proposed method directly exploits the network topology and learns the mapping from power injection to bus voltages while respecting the underlying physical laws. Physical consistency is enforced by embedding Kirchhoff's laws and Ohm's law relations into the model objective function, promoting only feasible and interpretable solutions while reducing dependence on large, high-quality labeled datasets typical of black-box deep learning approaches. Furthermore, we quantify model predictive uncertainty using the Monte Carlo dropout technique, which approximates Bayesian inference in neural networks and provides well-calibrated estimates of the model's epistemic uncertainty. The results show that the proposed PIGNN-<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \Phi$</tex> outperforms black-box models and achieves an approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50 \times$</tex> computational gain over traditional numerical PF solvers while achieving comparable accuracy. Furthermore, the framework enables risk-aware decision making by providing calibrated predictive intervals for voltage estimates, enabling system operators to assess probabilities of constraint violations.

title: Cross-Lingual Commonsense Reasoning Using XLM-R
authors: Manish Motghare, Vijay Kumar Joshi, Maria Jamal, Sabnam Kumari, Rakesh Kumar, Ritika Bobhate
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive investigation into cross-lingual commonsense reasoning using XLM-R, with particular emphasis on word-sense disambiguation (WSD) in multilingual contexts. We examine the evolution of WSD approaches from traditional knowledge-based methods to con-temporary neural architectures, highlighting the integration of cognitive models and graph-based techniques. Our study reveals a number of significant challenges in multilingual WSD. The paper concludes with future research directions that emphasize adaptive, multi-modal approaches to cross-lingual commonsense reasoning.

title: Bridging Infrastructure-Less Communications for Public Safety and AI With Distributed Learning
authors: Antonio Izquierdo Manzanares, I. Chen, Venkatesh Ramaswamy, Ballard Smith
publish: 2025 IEEE World Forum on Public Safety Technology (WF-PST)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) are powerful tools today, but they are also prodigious consumers of electricity, broadband internet, computing, and storage resources. Consequently, it is difficult to leverage their new capabilities in many public safety use cases where computers and sensors are distributed spatially with limited communication and little or no infrastructure. To better match the distributed and lightweight nature of many public safety use cases, this paper studies distributed learning architecture and evaluates its communication overhead, as well as technologies to reduce AI/ML model size. Simulation results of communication overhead and reductions in model size demonstrate advances in AI/ML ecosystem are helping to bring the power of AI/ML to infrastructure-less environments.

title: Research on Bi-Level Optimization of Operation of Photovoltaic-Storage-Charging Integrated Power Station Considering Dynamic Electricity Price
authors: Qiwen Li, Yang Liu, Zihan Yue, Jian Xu, Yueyang Shi, Wenzheng Yu
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Aiming at the problems of large load fluctuation and low system operating profit during the operation of photovoltaic-storage-charging integrated power station (PSCIS), a PSCIS bi-level optimization strategy considering dynamic electricity price is proposed. The upper layer is the PSCIS operating load model. The multi-objective particle swarm optimization algorithm based on artificial fish swarm optimization (AFSA-MOPSO) is employed to optimize the multi-objective formulation with the goal of the system load fluctuation and operating income. The lower layer is a user-centric EV charging optimization model, and the optimal solution for user charging satisfaction is obtained. Return to the upper layer, re-select the solution until a reasonable understanding is obtained. Finally, through the simulation example analysis, the load variation amplitude of the charging station load decreased by 11.1%, and the operating income increased by 8.29%. The findings indicate that the proposed strategy outperforms both fixed pricing and peak-valley time-ofuse (TOU) tariffs in guaranteeing user charging satisfaction, mitigating load peak-valley disparities in PSCIS, and enhancing the operational cost-efficiency of charging stations.

title: Ultrasound Image-Based Liver Disease Detection Using Transfer Learning: A Comparative Analysis of CNN and Their Variants
authors: Fatin Israq, Sefatul Wasi, Sazin Bin Noor, Siam Tahsin Bhuiyan, Saadia Binte Alam, Rashedur Rahman
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)

title: An Analysis on Distribution of Power in Parallel Inverters, with Mismatched Feeder Impedance in a Micro grid
authors: Vasamsetti Venkata Lakshmi, Boni Sirisha, Pudi Sekhar, Sumith Yellapu, R. Sundar, Guruvulunaidu Ponnada
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: In droop-controlled inverters within a microgrid, The dynamic relationship between active and reactive power is a key factor in determining the performance and stability of low-voltage microgrids. To separate the real and wattless powers, there are a number of control topologies available, namely the virtual power, virtual voltage and frequency-based control strategies. However, equal sharing of true and wattless power cannot be realized during the parallel operation of the inverters under mismatched feeder impedance. To address the above problem. This work introduces an improved virtual power decoupling approach for droop-controlled inverters operating in parallel, by generating an integrated rotation angle derived from the Rouths stability criterion based on the system stability and dynamics without affecting the parameters of the system. The validity of the suggested method is examined through simulations conducted in PSCAD software. The simulated performance indicates that the proposed control strategy is capable of maintaining equal real and reactive power sharing under mismatched feeder conditions.

title: Real-Time Health Monitoring and Parameter Estimation of PV Systems Using a Data-Driven Digital Twin with DropKAN and MDA-EFO
authors: Rituvic Pandey, Nishant Kumar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: Data-driven Digital Twin (DT) technology enables enhanced reliability and maintainability of grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) inverters. This work develops a DT framework for real-time parameter estimation and health monitoring of a singlestage three-phase grid-tied PV system. The framework combines a high-fidelity MATLAB/Simulink physical model with a Dropout Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (DropKAN) for dynamic state prediction, Magnetic-Domain Alignment Electromagnetic Field Optimization (MDA-EFO) for parameter estimation, and a CSTOG-NSB-CGI filtering stage for accurate grid signal extraction. Continuous tracking of key component parameters enables nonintrusive health assessment of the DC-link capacitor, interfacing inductors, and filter elements. Case studies under degraded conditions show strong agreement between DT predictions and physical responses, achieving low NMSE, high $R^{2}$, and clear diagonal dominance in proportion matrices. These results validate the proposed DT as an effective tool for predictive maintenance and reliable operation of PV inverters under dynamic and aginginduced stress.

title: Empirical Evaluation of Brain Tumor Detection Methodology based on Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) Provision
authors: P. Pattabhirama Mohan, Govindaraj Ramkumar
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: The Detection, Segmentation and Extraction of the contaminated areas in the Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) images is a major issue but a routine and time consuming process done by the radiologist or the clinical experts, depends on their skills. The different anatomical structure of the human organ can be envisioned using the ideas of image processing. The simple imaging methods do not detect abnormal structures in the brain of human beings easily. To detect and segment the brain tumors, a complete autonomic variant of a brain tumor, that is, an Extreme Learning model-based Inspection V3 (EIV- 3) model has been proposed in this paper. In the given work, it was suggested that the entire cerebral venous system should be divided into MRI imaging with incorporation of a novel fully automatic algorithm primarily based on structural, morphological, and relaxometry information. The ability of segmenting is characterized by a high degree of uniformity between anatomy and surrounding brain tissue. EIV-3 is a form of learning algorithm that is formed by one or more hidden layer nodes. These networks find application in regression and classification among others. To train and verify the accuracy of identifications of the tumor images, the probabilistic neural network classification system has been used in brain MRI images. The numeric results indicate the near perfection of nearly 99.39 percent correctness in the accurate detection of abnormal and normal tissue in a Magnetic Resonance Magnetic Image of the brain indicating the efficiency of the system proposed.

title: Classification of Ocular Diseases Using AlexNet & MobileNet
authors: Manal El Harti, Said Jai Andaloussi, Ouail Ouchetto
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Networking, Intelligent Systems &amp; Security (NISS)
abstract: Ocular illnesses cause a substantial amount of vision loss and blindness around the world. Prompt and precise diagnosis is essential to avoid severe consequences. Nevertheless, the limited availability of retinal specialists underscores the importance of developing automated diagnostic systems. This study investigates the performance of two lightweight, pretrained models—AlexNet and MobileNet—for classifying five retinal diseases. These models were chosen for their effective feature extraction capability, moderate computational demands, and compatibility with transfer learning. Previous research has leveraged these models as deep feature extractors. However, this study evaluates their standalone effectiveness, focusing on their ability to deliver accurate classifications in ophthalmology. Multiple datasets encompassing various retinal conditions were employed to ensure a thorough evaluation. Advanced preprocessing and data augmentation approaches were used to improve model generalization and address challenges related to small datasets. The results reveal that MobileNetV3 outperforms MobileNetV2 and AlexNet, achieving exceptional specificity of 100%, 97.44%, 94.48%, 99.79%, 98.60%, and 98.11% in detecting hypertensive retinopathy, toxoplasmosis chorioretinitis, diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma, and cataracts, respectively. These findings highlight MobileNetV3's potential for real-world applications, particularly in ophthalmology.

title: A Framework for Seamless Transition to Post-Quantum Security in SDN
authors: Omesh Kumar, Deepak Rai, Sumit Badotra
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: Quantum Computers are disrupting the traditional cryptographic algorithms, like RSA and ECC. These Traditional cryptographic algorithms are generally used for network communication, basically like SDN’s (Software Defined Networking). Now to address these Challenges the NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) proposes post quantum algorithms (PQC) which are designed to resist the Quantum Computers. Now, the transition from traditional algorithms to PQC in SNDs is no easy task, it needs to properly address issues of transitioning like higher processing demands, compatibility issues with existing infrastructure, and no proper guidelines to deploy PQC which is secure and scalable. This paper proposes a hybrid approach, i.e. a stepping stone to help SDNs shift from traditional cryptography algorithms to quantum-safe environments. It includes a step by step transition plan, from layer to layer to help the environment transition to PQC without any major disruption to existing networks.

title: The Sustainable Algorithm Leveraging AI-Driven Data Analytics for Environmental Resilience and Equitable Development
authors: Shashipurna Kurapati
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: The convergence of artificial intelligence (AI), data mining, and big data analytics presents a paradigm-shifting opportunity to address pressing global sustainability challenges. This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the adaptation of advanced AI methodologies to mine and analyze large-scale, heterogeneous datasets for sustainability objectives. We investigate how machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), and optimization techniques are leveraged to optimize resource use, mitigate environmental degradation, and foster social equity. The study meticulously examines applications in sustainable energy, precision agriculture, climate science, and circular economies. Crucially, the paper critically addresses the significant challenges inherent in this integration, such as the computational carbon footprint of large models, data biases, and model opacity. We propose that a multidisciplinary, ethically-grounded approach is imperative to ensure these technologies are developed and deployed sustainably and equitably. The analysis is supported by mathematical formulations of key algorithms and conceptual models of proposed AI frameworks.

title: Malaria Detection with Convolutional Neural Networks: An Accurate and Automated Framework
authors: V Sumalatha, Soumyasis Das, Saurabh Fulara, Chandra Kishore
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: This Study suggests a well-designed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)for the automatic recognition of infected red blood cells from malaria. The model takes 100x100x3 RGB images and uses a series of convolutional layers with 64 filters (3x3), ReLU activations, max pooling, batch normalization ,dropout, and dropout for avoiding overfitting. A global average pooling layer is succeeded by two dense layers, with output sigmoid activated and separating parasitized and uninfected cells. The 27,558 high-resolution microscopic images on Kaggle and evenly distributed between the two classes provide very strict model testing. The offered method has a lot of potential. potential to accelerate the diagnosis of malaria with an overall accuracy. of approximately 93.32%.The simplicity and the efficiency of the architecture point to a strong movement towards construction of automaticdiagnosis machines that would be of paramount significance inlimiting human error and resource-limited settings diagnosis time.

title: Building a Vietnamese Query Completion System Using Statistical Language Model
authors: Duc-Hong Pham
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Automatically completing queries in the search function is considered particularly important in every ecommerce system. By automatically returning the top completed queries for users to choose from, it helps them quickly find information about the products that they want. However, the challenge for each system is the time to automatically complete each query and must be performed in real-time. Many previous studies using neural network language models lead to very high training costs, and the time to calculate the completion of queries is also a problem with these models. In this paper, we propose a simpler and more effective method for completing Vietnamese word level and query level based on statistical language models. In our experiments, we use dataset of queries in search logs and product titles of Vietnamese ecommerce platforms.

title: Understanding Discrimination in AI Recruitment: A SHAP-Driven CNN Analysis
authors: Anju B. Nandrajog, Honoria Samson, Palki Sharma, Rajesh Sachdeva, Shiva Mehta
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: This paper discusses how Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) could enhance fairness in recruitment systems that use AI. The CNN model, when fitted on artificial data of 10,000 anonymised resumes, displayed an accuracy of 86.4% and an F1-score of 0.84, demonstrating its efficiency in distinguishing between adequate and unqualified candidates. The training and validation accuracies are stabilised at 87% and 83%, respectively, with training and validation losses that are declining steadily across 20 epochs. The ROC-AUC value was 0.89, indicating good classification. SHAP analysis also depicted feature-based biases, which the strongest ones were related to gender and ethnicity. The model in question provides a predisposition to negative SHAP values in the occurrence of female pronouns and ethnic minority names, with insignificant impact on the result, with -0.174 and -0.118, respectively. A Demographic Parity Difference indicator reduced by 0.21 to 0.08 when biased features had been removed, hence proving the rise in fairness. The findings indicate that despite the CNN model producing effective and precise predictions when used to predict what organizations require during the recruitment process, there is an implicit bias that should be mitigated in AI models. An integrated system of high performance and model interpretability, through SHAP, provides a viable and just system of detecting and remedying the implicit biases in AI-driven recruitment systems. It is notable that the paper is critical, given that solutions that are sensitive to unfairness need to be incorporated into AI systems to foster diversity and inclusivity during the recruiting process.

title: Few Shot Intrusion Detection Using Siamese Networks for Zero Day Threats
authors: MD Fahim Nafis, Mohamed Rahouti, Yasmine Medjadba, Hamza Drid
publish: 2025 IEEE World Forum on Public Safety Technology (WF-PST)
abstract: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) are crucial for maintaining network security, yet conventional machine learningbased IDS approaches often struggle with imbalanced datasets and limited ability to detect novel or zero-day attacks. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a few-shot learning (FSL) approach using a Siamese Neural Network for intrusion detection. Leveraging the CICIDS-2017 dataset, we construct positive and negative traffic pairs with minimal training samples and apply correlation-based feature selection alongside standardization techniques to preprocess the data. The Siamese network architecture enables the model to learn effective similarity metrics, allowing accurate classification even with limited data. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms traditional classifiers such as SVM, Decision Trees, and Random Forests, achieving an accuracy of 97.92%, precision of 99.23%, recall of 95.83%, and F1-score of 97.50%. Furthermore, the model exhibits promising generalization capabilities for zero-day detection, showcasing the potential of FSL for robust, lightweight IDS deployment in real-world scenarios.

title: Assessing Power Grid Resilience Under Wind-Flood Multi-Hazard Stressors
authors: Ramya Macheri, Samson Yu, Shama Islam, Hieu Trinh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Modern power systems are increasingly vulnerable to compound climate hazards, where simultaneous events such as windstorms and flooding increase infrastructure failure risks. Existing fragility models often assess these hazards in isolation, overlooking their dependence and the cascading effects on grid performance. This study proposes an integrated resilience assessment framework that incorporates wind-flood dependence through a Gumbel copula, estimates component failures through empirical fragility curves, and simulates cascading grid failures using a PSS®E. The framework is demonstrated on the IEEE 24-bus system, illustrating how hazard intensity and system dynamics interact in complex, nonlinear ways. Contingency analysis under compound hazard scenarios shows voltage collapse, severe line overloads, and significant load shedding, thereby exposing critical system vulnerabilities. The proposed methodology provides a practical tool for system planners and grid operators to support decision making and grid hardening decisions against growing climate threats.

title: Change Detection in Synthetic Aperture Radar Imaging using Siamese Convolutional Neural Network(Siamese CNN)
authors: Archana Chuadhari, Ratnali Pawar, Saish Pisolkar, Harsh Suryawanshi, Shubham Bhosale
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imaging has an influence on change detection, which plays a key role to evaluate land cover shifts, city expansion, forest loss, and disaster impacts. SAR data stands out because it can capture images at any hour and in any weather giving it an advantage over regular optical imagery to observe Earth. Our research introduces an automated system to detect changes. This system uses a Siamese convolutional neural network (CNN) to study SAR images taken over time and spot major changes. We use Sentinel-1 SAR images from the Copernicus program comparing decades-old images with new ones to find out how land use has changed.We train the Siamese CNN model with a contrastive loss enabling it to recognize similarities and differences between image pairs, which is key to spot changes . Our tests show that this deep learning method works better than old-school methods like image subtraction and thresholding in terms of accuracy and flexibility. We see big improvements in spotting both small and large-scale changes making this method great for keeping an eye on the environment. disaster response, and urban planning.
