title: An Enhanced Architectural Model for Graph based Multiple Instance Learning for Re-Identification of Histopathology Data
authors: G. Ramkumar, Ahmad Abdelhafiz Ali Samhan, Anandhi R J, T Aswani
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Deep Learning methods are used for numerous applications with the usage of histopathology data where it mainly concentrates on the achievement of maximum accuracy or the primary origin of metastases. Here to achieve maximum accuracy we concentrate on the development of Enhanced Architectural Model for graph based multiple instance learning with the re identification of histopathology data (EGMILR). The core modules of this research are graph based multi-instance learning, data preprocessing and loss function analysis. The simulation demonstration of this model is performed in Python and the matrix used for the outcome analysis are accuracy and loss analysis, ROC analysis and comparative fitness value analysis. The validation accuracy attained by EGMILR is 94% and its training accuracy is 97%, the ROC performance of EGMILR is around 94% which helps to achieve maximum overall performance in the usage of histopathology data.

title: Smart Water Irrigation Using IoT and Artificial Intelligence, and Machine Learning Techniques
authors: Prathmesh Waghmare, Amit Gudadhe, Pradnya Pravin Zode, Rajendra Rewatkar, Harshraj N. Gadbail, Veer Bobade
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: Agriculture requires irrigation and is very important in attaining maximum crop growth. However, the traditional use of irrigation has many limitations, where it leads either to excessive use or improper utilization of water. At present, the growing demand for the population with lack of adequate water resources worldwide emphasizes the need for a water-efficient and sustainable irrigation technology. This article explores how Artificial Intelligence (AI) and the Internet of Things (IoT) are reshaping the future of irrigation. By combining machine learning algorithms, wireless sensors, and IoT-based monitoring systems, these technologies make farming more precise, data-driven, and automated-ultimately improving both resource efficiency and crop yields. The discussion highlights different AI approaches in irrigation, the application of machine learning models, and the role of IoT in monitoring soil conditions and weather patterns. It also examines key challenges in current irrigation practices, such as fluctuating water requirements during different crop growth stages and the reliance on stable internet connectivity. The article further traces the evolution of irrigation techniques and explains how modern communication tools and cloud technologies support smarter farming methods. A special focus is given to AgriSens, an innovative IoT-powered irrigation system that addresses these challenges by offering real-time monitoring, adaptive irrigation scheduling, and a user-friendly interface. Designed to work across diverse farming environments, AgriSens combines advanced technology with practical usability, marking a big step toward more sustainable and efficient agriculture.

title: Design of an IoT based Integrated Model for Real Time Monitoring, Automation, and Optimization of E-Waste Management Process
authors: Vineet Rana, Yogesh Mohan
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: The resulting spread in discarded electronic waste or e-waste has become one of the worldwide pressing problems, as the discarded devices have been causing harm to the environment and the human health. The increasing population of e-waste (electronic waste) has become a serious threat to sustainable environmental future, resource recovery and human health. The non-advanced forms of waste management are normally without any real-time control, no satisfactory segregation and no statistical optimization, which inevitably lead to inefficient recycling techniques, and, a large degree of environmental risk. In this study, we hypothesize the development of an IoT-integrated model, augmented with machine learning, to make real-time monitoring, automations, and optimizations of e-waste management systems possible. The methodology will use IoT-equipped sensors used to track the dumpsters, cloud-based data transfer to central location for processing, and machine learning models to make automatic classifications, predictions and decisions. The final verification of the suggested framework is demonstrated by means of simulation and experimental realization, which illustrated the potential of the framework to decrease the inefficiencies of its operation and improve the rates of recycling.These findings indicate the viability of the model on various scales: the appropriate waste sorting by machine-learning classifiers, the optimized collection routes with the use of the predictive algorithms, and the transparency real-time monitoring with the help of the IoT sensors. Based on experimental analysis, it indicated better efficiency in terms of collection, accuracy in segregating materials as well as resources recovered as opposed to customs approaches. Moreover, the scalability analysis proves that the suggested system is flexible in relation to the urban smart city implementation. The given work lies down solid ground in the field of sustainable and intelligent e-waste management, filling the gap between monitoring through IoT and the optimization through AI, and participating in the circles of initiatives to make the economy more circular and contribute to environmental protection.

title: An Improved Feedforward-Based DC Link Voltage Control Method for Single Phase PWM Rectifiers
authors: Samir Gautam, Anant Kumar Verma, Dylan Dah-Chuan Lu, Waqas Hassan, Yuba Raj Kafle, Foad Taghizadeh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: This paper presents an approach to improve the accuracy of the double-frequency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \omega$</tex>) ripple reduction in feedforward-based outer voltage control loop for a single-phase PWM rectifier. In the feedforward technique, the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \omega$</tex> ripple in the DC link capacitor (DCL) voltage, caused by the singlephase AC-DC power conversion, is estimated and subtracted from the measured DCL voltage before being fed to the voltage controller. However, the estimate depends on the capacitance and its equivalent series resistance (ESR), both of which have tolerances and can vary over time. The feedforward control approach aims to increase the control loop bandwidth for a quicker response, but this results in lower attenuation of the remaining <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \omega$</tex> ripple coming from inaccurate estimation. In this work, the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \omega$</tex> ripple estimation is periodically updated to account for the uncertainties associated with the variation of capacitance and ESR. The proposed idea is simulated in MATLAB/Simulink.

title: Enhancement of Convolutional Neural Networks for Image Processing: Deep Feature Extraction for Identifying and categorizing Dermatological Lesion
authors: P. Arunachalam, Poripireddy Sushmitha, Kolla Sahitya, Mamindla Srivitha, Karri Venkata Reddy, Alli. Yugandhar
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Timely diagnosis and treatment of skin conditions such as melanoma and other potentially lethal diseases rely heavily on an accurate discrimination and classification of the dermatological lesions. In typical diagnostic procedures, such as a physical examination or laryngoscope inspection, the results are subjective and highly variable, and visual inspections have limitations in the detection of early diagnosis. In this regard, we explain a refined convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture designed for dermoscopy image analysis to promote a more effective lesion classification using deep feature representation and an improved learning strategy. The model is based on 3-level convolutional networks and deep pooling operations to capture multi-scale skin lesion patterns while preventing models from overfitting or spending more computation in generating redundant features. In addition, the model is augmented with attention-derived modules, and transfer learning from large dermatological datasets ensures that the model generalizes well to different types of lesion and to different skin types. The network is trained and tested on public and clinically annotated databases including HAM10000 and ISIC with comparable results in terms of precision, recall, and F1-score than traditional CNN methods. We incorporate data augmentation, adaptive learning rate schedulers, and dropoutbased regularization methods for improved resilience towards image noise and clinical variation. Experiments validate the ability of the system to discriminatively diagnose malignant versus benign lesions with credible confidence, aid dermatologists in their clinical decision-making process and present a scalable solution for tele dermatology applications. This study does not only contribute to the state-of-the-art of automated diagnosis of dermatological diseases, but also lays the groundwork for the application of AI-powered image processing solutions in clinical real-world scenarios. The proposed approach could be extended to personalized skin health monitoring and AI-supported diagnostic systems, especially in unattended areas with shortage of specialized dermatological services.

title: Comparative Evaluation of Machine Learning Models for PolyCystic Ovary Syndrome Prediction Using Phenotypic and Lifestyle Features
authors: Shareena Basheer, Arun Pandian
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Poly Cystic Ovarian Syndrome is a prevalent endocrine disease among women of childbearing age. Its symptoms differs, and the diseases often coexist with other medical conditions, making diagnosis difficult. This study assesses eight machine learning models for PCOS prediction using a few lifestyle and phenotypic characteristics. To train and test eight machine learning models, standardized data is utilized. Performance of each model is compared using different Evaluation metrics. According to the evaluation, the best accuracy (0.83) and balanced performance are provided by the Support Vector Machine, k-Nearest Neighbor, and AdaBoost models. With self-as their appropriateness for early PCOS screening. This strategy might encourage early intervention and lessen long-term health issues.

title: Blockchain-Based Tokenization and Management of Land Documents: A Decentralized Approach for Secure Ownership and Transfer
authors: Prabhat Das, Diptadeep Roy, Kaveri Banerjee, Sajal Saha, Bodhi Chakraborty, Garima Roy, Dibya Jyoti Bora
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
abstract: Blockchain technology is transforming real estate with its transparency and security. Its decentralized, immutable ledger ensures all transaction records are tamper-proof and visible. This paper addresses a critical flaw in the traditional market: fragmented systems that often overlook approvals from inspectors and lenders. By leveraging the Ethereum blockchain and its smart contracts, Block realty automates agreements, eliminating intermediaries and enhancing efficiency. The paper utilizes the ERC-721 standard to tokenize real estate assets, converting properties into unique digital tokens. This fundamentally changes asset management, enhancing market liquidity and global accessibility. A key feature is a comprehensive three-step approval process, ensuring all stakeholders-buyers, lenders, inspectors, and sellers are actively engaged. Each party's approval is immutably recorded on the blockchain, creating a verifiable chain of custody that boosts transaction integrity. The use of Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs) also facilitates fractional ownership, allowing multiple investors to jointly own a single property. This democratizes real estate investment by lowering the barrier to entry. For data management and cost optimization, Blockrealty integrates the Inter Planetary File System (IPFS), a decentralized storage protocol for securely housing property images and metadata. Additionally, seamless Metamask integration provides a user-friendly interface. Looking ahead, upcoming Ethereum advancements like the Pectra upgrade are poised to enhance Ethereum's scalability and efficiency, further strengthening the foundation for platforms like Blockrealty by enabling them to handle transactions more effectively and reliably.

title: Enhancing Breast Cancer Diagnosis: Explainable, Multi-Modal, and Lightweight Deep Learning Models
authors: Baljit Kaur, Renu Popli
publish: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
abstract: With a lower mortality rate, breast cancer is one of the top harmful cancers for women internationally, thus needing an appropriate diagnosis at early stages for curing. Advanced breast cancer detection techniques using deep learning are reviewed analytically in this study, emphasizing segmentation, classification, and domain adaptation. Although the conventional diagnostic techniques were effective, they are based on expert knowledge, hence, variable results. Our work investigates such hybrid DL methods as explainable AI (XAI) for transparent decision-making, multi-modal data fusion, and lightweight DL to be utilized in environments with low resources. The research points to the necessity to increase the level of transparency of the model, integrate various data sources, and consider the limitations on resources. By overcoming these challenges, this work aims to enhance breast cancer detection with robust and explainable solutions that are usable in actual clinical settings.

title: Topology-Aware Deep Kernel Learning for Distribution System State Estimation
authors: Mengzhao Duan, Wenyi Zhang, Zhao Xu, Xinyang Su, Wuyou Xiao, Yun Dong, Yiting Huang, Zhipeng Meng, Zijian Lin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Accurate and reliable distribution system state estimation (DSSE) is crucial for system management and optimization. However, with the increasing integration of renewable energy sources and flexible loads, the network topology of distribution systems changes more frequently, posing new challenges to traditional state estimation methods. Existing approaches typically treat state estimation under different topologies as a multi-task learning problem. To address this, we propose a topology-aware data-driven state estimation method based on Deep Kernel Learning (DKL). Specifically, by embedding topology encodings into the input features, the proposed approach reformulates the multi-topology estimation problem into a unified single-task learning framework, enabling information sharing and joint training across topologies. Input features are extracted by a neural network, and voltage magnitudes and phase angles are modeled using Gaussian Process Regression (GPR). The proposed method can achieve accurate state estimation with limited training samples. The approach is validated through experiments on the IEEE 33-node test system, demonstrating high accuracy and robustness under varying topologies.

title: Enhancing Credit Risk Assessment with Machine Learning Algorithms: A Comparative Study of Label Encoding and One-Hot Encoding Across Multiple Classifiers
authors: Safae Ndama, Oussama Ndama, En-Naimi El Mokhtar
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Networking, Intelligent Systems &amp; Security (NISS)
abstract: This study focuses on using machine learning for credit risk assessment, which is crucial in making financial decisions. As financial systems become more complex, traditional methods are no longer enough to accurately assess risk. Machine learning models offer the ability to uncover hidden patterns and relationships within the data, providing a more reliable and efficient way to assess credit risk. In our research, we compare the performance of several machine learning models to understand how they perform with different data preprocessing techniques. Specifically, we compare the effects of Label Encoding and One-Hot Encoding on these models. The study highlights the importance of preprocessing, especially when working with imbalanced data, which is common in credit risk assessment. XGBoost yields the best results, with CatBoost also showing strong performance. By examining these models and encoding methods, this research offers valuable insights into improving predictive accuracy and ensuring more reliable credit risk evaluations.

title: CNCL: Optimized Lupus Disease (LD) Detection Principle by using Convolutional Neural Classification Logic
authors: Flemitha Mary D, Govindaraj Ramkumar
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: This research presents an automatic framework for the detection of Lupus Cutaneous Disease (L-CD) based on a unique Convolutional Neural Classification Logic (CNCL) model. L-CD has the symmetrical skin involvement also in specific localizations such as the cheeks or hands that have specific visual patterns essential for the correct diagnosis. The CNCL model is uniquely developed to capture and categorize these patterns through advanced image analysis methods and is a potentially effective alternative to conventional, manual diagnostic approaches. The core of our method consists of Transfer Learning, a strong deep learning approach utilizing the ability of pre-trained neural networks to extract features. In this way the authors can use models that were originally trained on massive datasets to increase the efficiency of learning in specific tasks, e.g. medical image classification, for which the availability of labelled data is often limited. Transfer learning allows users to converge faster and to achieve better accuracy by fine-tuning the general visual features to ones more specific to the considerable subtitles present in L-CD detection. For empirical evidence, a large and carefully selected dataset was created, in a way that it included images retrieved manually from common social networks, besides verified medical databases like Kaggle. This heterogeneous collection of images will facilitate the representation of a wide array of presentations and severity of the disease, thus the generalizability and robustness of the model. By including the credible sources in dataset construction, the proposed method was extensively evaluated by a modified CNN architecture specific to the L-CD classification task. The modified architecture has some special adaptations to help it distinguish small differences of skin rashes and thus enhance the accuracy in classification. Experimental results showed a high precision rate of 0.987, illustrating the power of the proposed model in identifying Lupus Cutaneous Disease and suggesting its application possibility in clinical.

title: Cost Optimization Analysis of Hybrid Renewable Energy Systems Using Various Techniques
authors: Madhab Chandra Das, Sarat Chandra Swain, Ranjeeta Patel, Biranchi Kumar Mahala, Sunil Kumar Bhatta
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: Hybrid Energy Systems (HES) are difficult to size optimally to satisfy customer power demand and meet reliability requirements. Availability of generated power and the cost of equipment are two fundamental constraints preventing widespread adoption. It is essential to design these hybrid renewable energy systems to fulfill all load requirements while requiring the least amount of investment and running expenses as possible. This research presents a productive approach to the economic management of grid-connected systems, which addresses the aforementioned issues. Real-time data on electrical load and solar irradiance are taken into account. The Social Group Optimization (SGO) technique is applied to determine the most efficient sizing of the hybrid renewable energy system, aiming to maximize economic benefits by minimizing energy costs. A time-of-use pricing model is incorporated, where electricity rates fluctuate based on demand levels

title: A Robust Physics-Informed Data-Driven Method for Li-Ion Battery Degradation Estimation
authors: Tianjing Wang, Chao Ren, Zuliang Huang, Zhao Yang Dong
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: The estimations of state of health and remaining useful life (RUL) are essential for battery health management, closely linked to current and future capacity estimations. However, limited research on high-accuracy capacity estimation across varying horizons constrains RUL prediction accuracy. Additionally, models are vulnerable to disturbances like missing data and noise, further reducing reliability. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a robust physics-informed battery degradation estimation method. The feature-wise neural controlled differential equations (CDE) generate attention-weighted features to capture critical battery characteristics, while the learning-wise neural CDE models differential relationships. The empirical model further refines the degradation trend. Experimental results demonstrate the model's superior performance across different estimation horizons, missing data, and noise scenarios compared to existing methods. Notably, even with 50 % missing data and a 100 -cycle horizon, the proposed physics-informed data-driven method can achieve an estimation error of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{0. 0 1 5 6}$</tex>.

title: An Improved Droop Control Strategy for Coordinated Power Sharing between Synchronous Generators and Grid-Forming Inverters
authors: Fujian Li, Wanrong Li, Jin Ma
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: This paper addresses unequal power sharing and stability issues in hybrid power systems where synchronous generators (SGs) and grid-forming inverters (GFMIs) coexist and interact within the same network. We develop consistent smallsignal state-space models for SGs, grid-following inverters (GFLIs), and GFMIs, and embed them in a system to enable systemlevel eigenvalue analysis and time-domain validation. Observing that the conventional GFMI <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P$</tex> - <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$f$</tex> droop uses a low-pass filtered power measurement that mismatches the proportional governor characteristic of SGs, we redesign the GFMI droop structure by adjusting the frequency setting of the low-pass filter (LPF) and introducing a feedback compensation, so that the GFMI's frequency-power map emulates an SG governor. The resulting governor-equivalent droop achieves per-unit equal power sharing between SGs and GFMIs while preserving good regulation of the remaining devices. Analytical results on the full 55-state model show a leftward shift of the rightmost eigenvalue across operating cases, indicating enhanced damping and an increased small-signal stability margin. Time-domain simulations under the same disturbance confirm accurate power equalization among SGs and GFMIs and suppressed frequency excursions. The method is communication-free and readily implementable, and robust across operating points, offering a practical pathway to stable SG-GFMI coordination in systems with high renewable penetration.

title: A System Identification Approach for Developing Dynamic Models of Inverter-Based Motor Loads
authors: Harshit Varshney, Himanshu Jain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: With the rising use of inverter-driven electrical appliances, power system modeling faces challenges due to the lack of openly available dynamic models for such devices. These models are typically proprietary and not included in standard simulation libraries. This paper introduces a System Identification (System ID) approach to derive dynamic models of inverter-based motor loads using transfer functions. This technique enables manufacturers to represent their devices accurately without revealing confidential inverter control logic. By injecting test signals from the AC input side and capturing voltage and current responses, we identify system behavior through data-driven methods. The resulting models demonstrate more than 94% accuracy in testing scenarios. When integrated into the IEEE 13-node feeder for validation, our models replicate the behavior of detailed simulations while achieving a 15-fold reduction in computational time. This method offers an efficient solution for analyzing systems with high penetration of inverter-based appliances in microgrid and distribution networks.

title: Multi-objective Optimization of Layout and Characteristics of Bidirectional Converter in Metro Traction Substation
authors: Chengcheng Fu, Can Luo, Yunjia Li, Pengfei Sun, Qingyuan Wang, Xiaoyun Feng
abstract: Enhancing the stability and energy efficiency of metro power supply systems through advanced traction technologies and upgraded infrastructure represents a critical pathway toward sustainable urban rail transit. Current systems face challenges of catenary voltage fluctuation, which compromise operational safety and stability, alongside high energy consumption that undermines economic performance. Bidirectional converter (BC)-equipped traction substations offer a promising solution by stabilizing grid voltage and recovering regenerative braking energy, yet their efficacy hinges on optimal deployment and tuning. This study presents an integrated optimization framework for the siting and droop characteristics of BCs retrofitted from existing rectifier substations. A dynamic power flow computation platform is developed to simulate multi-train operations under heterogeneous traction power supply conditions, enabling the accurate calculation of system-level electrical behavior. Based on this, a multi-objective optimization model is formulated to minimize the number of BC deployments, the maximum contact line voltage deviation, and the total energy consumption. An enhanced multi-objective whale optimization algorithm incorporating an adaptive elite evolutionary strategy is proposed to efficiently solve the resulting high-dimensional, non-convex problem. Based on the data of Wuxi Metro Line 1, numerical experiments demonstrate that the optimized BC configuration (deploying 8 BCs) reduces peak voltage fluctuation by 33.9% and system-wide energy consumption by 11.5%, while yielding a 7.4% reduction in overall costs considering investment expenses. The proposed methodology provides a systematic and economically viable approach to significantly enhancing the safety and energy efficiency of metro power-train integrated systems.

title: Talk with Your Fingers: A Depth-Aware Benchmark for Air-Writing Recognition
authors: Meiqi Wu, Yuzhong Zhao, Xuchen Li, Shiyu Hu, Yuanqiang Cai, Jiahong Wu, Weiqiang Wang, Kaiqi Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Air-writing has emerged as a promising communication modality for AR/VR and metaverse environments, enabling quiet, non-contact text input by translating finger movements into natural language. However, existing approaches typically project in-air writing onto a virtual 2D plane and assume characters are formed with a single continuous stroke–an oversimplification that neglects the rich 3D structure inherent in natural handwriting. In this work, we challenge the “single-stroke 2D” paradigm and explore the role of depth cues in enhancing air-writing recognition. To this end, we present DAAWBench, the first large-scale Depth-Aware Air-Writing dataset, featuring 8.8 million RGB-D frames annotated with 3,755 Chinese characters from the GB2312-80 Level-1 set. Our analysis reveals consistent depth variations at stroke boundaries, indicating that stroke segmentation and character recognition can benefit from depth modeling. Based on these insights, we propose DARec, a novel 3D trajectory-based recognition model that effectively leverages depth-aware priors. Extensive experiments across in-domain and out-of-domain settings, including evaluations with vision-language models (e.g., GPT-4o, Qwen-VL) and human baselines, show that DARec significantly outperforms 2D-only counterparts, achieving 87.73% accuracy versus 9.05%. Our findings demonstrate the critical importance of depth modeling in human-computer co-creative interfaces, and we will publicly release our dataset and code at https://github.com/wmeiqi/DAAWBench.

title: Transmission and Stability in a Quasi-Parity-Time-Symmetric WPT System for CC/CV Operation
authors: Jingjing Yang, Yuqing Huang, Shumin Ran, Xujian Shu, Yanwei Jiang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: The quasi-parity-time-symmetric wireless power transfer (Quasi-PT-WPT) system incorporates virtual impedance for soft-switching and enhanced system efficiency. This letter presents a thorough analysis of its transmission characteristics as a function of the gain–loss ratio, followed by a stability assessment via the describing function method (DFM). Furthermore, conditions for achieving constant current (CC) and constant voltage (CV) outputs are derived, and a load identification method is introduced to enable automatic CC/CV mode transition. Experimental results from a 30 V input prototype confirm stable CC and CV output at 2 A and 29 V, validate the stability criterion, and demonstrate its efficiency improvement.

title: Adaptive Sampling Based Surrogate Modeling Approach for Efficient Antenna Characterization
authors: Yongchao Liu, Xiaoming Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Surrogate models for antennas have been extensively adopted in design optimization and uncertainty quantification. However, developing accurate surrogate models often necessitates large-scale training datasets, which require substantial computational resources and time. Herein, a novel adaptive sampling approach is proposed for more efficient surrogate model construction. Firstly, a uniform Latin hypercube sampling method based on the maximin criterion is proposed to generate initial and candidate sample points with improved the global coverage and sampling diversity. Subsequently, a dual-criterion adaptive sampling strategy, which combines uncertainty expectation and gradient sampling, is introduced to guide new samples towards those regions of high prediction uncertainty and rapid response variation, thereby enhancing global accuracy and local sensitivity. Moreover, a distance-based penalty factor mechanism is also incorporated to prevent oversampling in regions with clustered sample points and thereby enhance global exploration. The proposed method has been verified with a series of nonlinear functions, demonstrating favorable model accuracy and sampling efficiency can be obtained with different surrogate models (Kriging and deep neural network). Further, the effectiveness of the method is demonstrated with two types of antennas: a Ka-band microstrip antenna and a Planar Inverted-F antenna. Results demonstrate that the method reduces the required number of sampling points by up to 38% compared to the existing methods, while maintaining high predictive accuracy (relative error < 4%).

title: Multifunctional High-Efficiency Transmissive/Absorptive/Reflective Metamaterial Characterized by Wideband Responses
authors: Zhefei Wang, Zhen Geng, Chao Li, Qingsheng Zeng, Jiahui Fu, Mingxin Song, Lixin Ran, Tayeb A. Denidni
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a novel absorptive multifunctional metamaterial with switchable high-efficiency and wideband in-band response while providing stable out-of-band shielding performance. Distinct from conventional cascaded rasorber-based designs requiring resonant bands alignment between lossy/lossless arrays, an innovative filtering cavity, which could confine and manipulate the incident energy within it, is developed to achieve exceptional bandwidth and efficiency behaviors. The polarization-selection and ohmic-loss characteristics of each cavity’s spectrum-distinct array could be independently controlled, and with an appropriate biasing strategy, three distinct mechanisms of second-order filter, wideband absorption, and multireflective shielding can be obtained and switched through one structure. These functionalities are realized through four polarization-independent and three all-polarized control modes, and each state presents nearly identical performance in its corresponding mode. Therefore, high-transmittance windows with the minimum insertion loss of 0.1 dB are realized in the filter mode with the 95% fractional bandwidth of 40%. When switching to the absorption mode, the incident energies within the passband can be efficiently absorbed by the structure (the bandwidth of 95% absorption rate is 30%). In the reflection mode, the metamaterial acts as a perfect multireflector throughout the operating band. It is worth noting that, attributing to the quasi-symmetric structure, the proposed method not only independently provides the aforementioned performance under both TE and TM incidences without degrading the performance of other modes, but also enables all-polarized functions of filter, absorber, and reflector. Finally, a prototype is fabricated for measurements, and the calculated, simulated, and measured results validate the proposed design.

title: Sensitive and Reliable Eddy Current Array System for Micro Defects in Turbine Blades
authors: Jinming Guo, Binhua Cao, Fengshan Sun, Mengbao Fan, Bo Ye
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The defects including cavities, inclusions, and microcracks are prone to be formed during the directional solidification process of superalloy turbine blades. Eddy current testing is challenged by low sensitivity and lift-off noise due to the low conductivity and geometric changes of curved surfaces. An eddy current array system is designed in this work, integrated a self-adaptive elastic probe and signal processing algorithms. Firstly, an in-plane differential probe based on the can-shaped magnetic core is developed, where the eddy current density is directionally enhanced through magnetic field shielding to amplify defect response, while lift-off noise is compensated using a self-adaptive elastic structure. Secondly, an adaptive wavelet threshold denoising algorithm and multi-channel fusion algorithm based on array topology prior knowledge, enabled effective separation of defect signals from noise. Finally, a hardware system based on multi-channel demodulation is developed to improve detection speed and capability. Experimental results demonstrate that the system achieves reliable detection of 0.1 mm defects at scanning speed of 80 cm/s, achieved a detection probability of 98%. This performance significantly outperforms existing methodologies and provides an effective solution for identifying micro-defects in low-conductivity materials, particularly in critical components such as turbine blades.

title: Measurement of Film Thickness and Profile Using Synchronous Phase-Shifting White-Light Spectral Interferometry with Vibration Resistance
authors: Danqing Bian, Tong Guo, Xinyuan Guo, Yifu Wan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The thickness and microstructure of films play a critical role in determining the optical and mechanical properties of semiconductor devices. Therefore, the accurate measurement and characterization of these parameters are of fundamental importance in both device performance evaluation and process control. In this paper, a white-light spectral interferometry system based on synchronized phase-shifting is built and a method that can simultaneously measure both the surface topography and the thickness of film samples within a single exposure is proposed. By integrating a line spectrometer with a polarization camera, four spectral interferograms can be captured concurrently and the phase information of the sample is extracted. The method significantly reduces data acquisition time and effectively suppresses the adverse effects of environmental vibrations on the interferometric performance. The accuracy of the system is verified through measurements of the line profile of a step height standard. Additionally, the system’s anti-vibration capability is experimentally confirmed by introducing external vibrations. Repeated measurements on film samples demonstrate high accuracy and sub-nanometer-level repeatability, with the relative error in film thickness measurements consistently remaining below 0.5 %. Furthermore, the measurement of a stepped film and the subsequent topography reconstruction of the film structure confirm the system’s excellent spatial resolution. Through uncertainty assessment of the proposed system, the expanded uncertainty values for film thickness and absolute distance were estimated to be 0.70 nm (k = 2) and 4.80 nm (k = 2).

title: Impedance Myography Circle Mapping: A Geometry-Based Multi-Frequency EIM Framework for Quantitative Assessment of Post-Stroke Muscle Impairment_supp1-3669773.pdf
authors: Yue Ming Gao
abstract: Post-stroke neuromuscular impairment arises from heterogeneous structural and electrophysiological alterations, including disrupted membrane integrity, fiber atrophy, and increased intramuscular fat. These changes substantially alter the resistive–capacitive behavior of muscle tissue, yet conventional electrical impedance myography (EIM) metrics remain limited in capturing subtle or spatially heterogeneous abnormalities. Building upon the bio-tissue equivalent circuit model, this study proposes a geometry-based framework termed Impedance Myography Circle Mapping (IMCM), which reformulates multi-frequency EIM spectra in the complex plane to extract two physiologically interpretable parameters: the circle center (IMCM-C), reflecting global conductivity balance and the short axis (IMCM-S), associated with membrane capacitance and microstructural integrity. A cross-sectional experiment was conducted on three upper-limb muscles—the biceps brachii, pronator teres, and flexor digitorum profundus—in 33 healthy controls and 50 stroke patients using multi-frequency ( 3 kHz–1 MHz) impedance measurements. Ten supervised machine learning models were employed to compare IMCM features with conventional single-frequency and Cole-model parameters. IMCM consistently achieved superior classification across all algorithms, reaching a maximum accuracy of 88.6% in binary (healthy vs. stroke) and 93.9% in multi-muscle classification tasks. The IMCM-S feature exhibited a significant negative correlation with the Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS) ($p< 0.001$), demonstrating its potential as a quantitative biomarker of spasticity severity. These findings establish IMCM as a physiologically grounded, model-consistent extension of EIM that integrates biophysical interpretability with data-driven robustness. By linking impedance geometry to muscle pathology, IMCM offers an objective and scalable framework for quantitative neuromuscular assessment and spasticity monitoring in stroke rehabilitation.

title: N-DIPPER: A Distributed Inter-Die Peak Power Management Network for Nand Systems
authors: Jinwoo Park, John Kim
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: As NAND flash memory continues to scale, the increasing word-line (WL) stack height and higher program voltage requirements have led to severe peak-current overlaps across dies within a package. Such overlaps cause power-management-IC (PMIC) limit violations, voltage droops, and reliability degradation. Traditional static solutions - such as controller throttling or slope control - cannot fully address these issues because dynamic process, voltage, and temperature (PVT) variations and wear out-induced drift cause both nondeterministic interdie timing mismatches and peak-current magnitude fluctuations, forcing conservative guardbands that limit system parallelism and performance. To overcome these challenges, we propose N DIPPER (NAND Distributed Inter-die Peak PowER Management Network), a cooperative runtime inter-die scheduling framework that dynamically schedules high-current (HC) phases through lightweight in-package (inter-die) fabric. N-DIPPER is based on token-based scheduling across the fabric to serialize scheduling of HC phases while device-aware information is leveraged to avoid utilizing worst-case guardbands. Our evaluation results show that N-DIPPER eliminates PMIC-limit violations (100%) while sustaining 97% of the baseline throughput (without any peak power management) across diverse workloads and die configurations.

title: The Last-Level Branch Predictor Revisited
authors: David Schall, Mária Ďuračková, Boris Grot
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: Branch prediction is critical for high-performance CPUs, with mispredictions causing significant execution inefficiencies. Modern server workloads exacerbate the challenge due to expanding instruction and branch working sets, while predictor capacities remain limited to avoid latency increases. A recently introduced hierarchical branch predictor design, LLBP, demonstrated a way to reduce misprediction rates by augmenting an unmodified TAGE-based predictor with a decoupled high-capacity metadata store. Despite using a large amount of storage, LLBP was shown to achieve only a fraction of the accuracy gain of an equal-sized (but impractical) TAGE-based predictor. This work provides a detailed analysis of LLBP, identifying sources of its accuracy loss. Chief among these are contention within certain sets of LLBP's high-capacity metadata store (namely those containing patterns for hard-to-predict branches), as well as duplication of patterns, which leads to prolonged training time. To address these issues, we propose dynamic context depth adaptation, an enhancement to the original LLBP design, which yields a significantly better distribution of patterns for hard-to-predict branches, thereby reducing both pattern set contention and pattern duplication. Our proposed design that realizes dynamic context depth adaptation requires only small modifications to the baseline LLBP while increasing its accuracy by 0.8-11.5 % (average 3.6 %).

title: IOT-Enabled Cloud-Based Smart Door Locking with Unauthorized Intruder Detection and Protection
authors: Jay Kumar Atal, Shreya Sonakshi Rana, Abhirup Sen, Lipsa Subhadarshini, Nirnoy Mandal Ravi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: This paper introduces a Cloud-Enabled Smart Biometric Door Lock system designed to overcome critical limitations in existing IoT access control solutions. Conventional smart locks exhibit vulnerabilities to spoofing attacks, power inefficiencies, cloud dependency, and static security protocols. Our architecture integrates multi-sensor fusion (capacitive fingerprint scanning, AI-driven facial recognition, and passive infrared motion detection) with edgeAI analytics to enable real-time intrusion detection and adaptive threat response. Crucially, the system pioneers remote fingerprint enrollment via secure web dashboard—eliminating physical presence requirements for user registration.Hybrid energy harvesting (solar and piezoelectric subsystems) significantly extends operational autonomy, resolving persistent power constraints in Wi-Fi/Bluetooth-dependent locks. Security is reinforced through military-grade AES-256 encryption and local microSD storage, ensuring biometric data integrity during network outages. Comparative analysis against existing literature confirms superior performance in spoofing resistance, environmental adaptability, and operational resilience. The solution reduces false alarms and maintenance demands while supporting regulatory compliance.By unifying remote biometric management, energy sustainability, and zero-trust security principles, this work establishes a new paradigm for intelligent access control in residential, commercial, and industrial deployments.

title: A Deadlock-Free Bridge Module for Inter-Chiplet Cache-Coherent Communication in an Open Chiplet Ecosystem
authors: Zhiqiang Chen, Wenwen Fu, Yongwen Wang, Hongwei Zhou
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA)
abstract: The envisioned open chiplet ecosystem promises significant reductions in chip design complexity and cost by enabling designers to rapidly assemble standard chiplets from diverse vendors. Constructing such an open chiplet ecosystem requires support from Network-on-Chip (NoC) routing algorithms, as integrating multiple chiplets onto an interposer can potentially lead to inter-chiplet deadlock. Prior work avoids deadlock through methods like turn restrictions, virtual channel isolation, or packet injection control, or recovers from deadlock using mechanisms such as escape channels or bubble flow control. These approaches achieve a favorable balance regarding modularity, performance, and cost. However, they still rely on designers possessing detailed knowledge of the internal NoC architecture within each chiplet. This requirement impedes the development of a truly open ecosystem, as it constrains chiplet interoperability and vendor independence. Addressing this limitation, we propose a Deadlock-Free Bridge Module (DFBM) designed to resolve interchiplet deadlock without relying on the specifics of individual chiplet NoC implementations. The DFBM infers the transmission behavior of inter-chiplet packets by analyzing the dependency relationships among coherence protocol transaction flows. It then employs a packet injection control mechanism to isolate inter- and intra-chiplet packets, thereby preventing deadlock. DFBMs can be seamlessly interconnected between arbitrary chiplets to achieve deadlock-freedom, eliminating the need for modifications to their internal NoC architectures. Experimental results demonstrate that DFBM incurs only 2.5% area overhead, while achieving a performance improvement ranging from 1% to 7%.

title: Evaluating Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, and Random Forest for Predicting Stroke Risk
authors: Carmel Mary Belinda M J., Kannan E, Alex David S., Anstey Vathani T, Ruth Naveena N, Nuti Dhruv
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: Stroke is still a leading cause of death and long-term disability worldwide, and therefore, early and accurate prediction is required for immediate intervention. This paper provides a comparative analysis of three supervised Learning models, Logistics Regression (LR), Decision Tree (DT) and Random Forest (RF) for stroke prediction using an open accessed healthcare dataset. After preprocessing involving missing value imputation, label encoding, feature scaling and balancing the classes by SMOTE, each model was fitted and evaluated on stratified dataset. The models were assessed through basic classification indicators including accuracy, precision, recall and F1-score. The Logistic Regression model achieved a fair accuracy (79.18%) and was chosen based on its interpretability. The Decision Tree model yielded good accuracy (89.46%) with improved sensitivity and well-balanced precision-recall characteristics. The Random Forest's test accuracy was the highest of all three models at 95.01%, demonstrating that it effectively reduced the false positive and negative rates in ensemble learning. Additionally, the confusion matrices confirmed the capability of Random Forest to properly recognize cases of stroke at a low rate of misclassification. The graphing of model metrics using comparison bar plots illustrated the best performance of Random Forest in comparison to other evaluation metrics. Logistic Regression provides interpretability and efficiency, Decision Tree provides a clear decision rule, and Random Forest is the most accurate and reliable for practical clinical use.

title: Harnessing CNN-LSTM Networks for Non-Invasive Thyroid Dysfunction Detection through Voice Analysis
authors: Suresh C, N. Hindumathy, M. Revathy, B. Gopinathan, R. Sujitha, S. P. Santhoshkumar
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: A common endocrine condition, thyroid dysfunction can lead to major side effects like obesity, infertility, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive decline if left untreated. Traditional diagnostic methods use biochemical assays, which are time-consuming, expensive, and invasive despite their accuracy. This paper suggests a completely automated, non-invasive screening framework that uses human voice acoustics as a thyroid dysfunction biomarker in order to overcome these difficulties. Subjects with both normal and abnormal thyroid conditions had their voice recordings taken, and they were pre-processed using spectral analysis, noise reduction, framing, and the feature extraction of Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs). In order to capture both long-term temporal dependencies and local spectral structures in the vocal signal, a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network–Long Short-Term Memory (CNN-LSTM) model was created. While LSTM layers modeled temporal variations associated with vocal fold vibrations influenced by thyroid abnormalities, CNN layers extracted hierarchical representations from spectral features. Experiments showed that the CNN-LSTM architecture outperformed conventional classifiers like Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forests (RF), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) by achieving an overall classification accuracy of over 98%. The results show how voice can be a trustworthy, non-invasive biomarker and how deep learning models can be used to create thyroid dysfunction diagnostic tools that are affordable, scalable, and easy for patients to use.

title: Unsupervised LSTM-Autoencoder Approach for Early Detection of Sensor Failures in High-Dimensional Monitoring Environments
authors: Amit Sharma, Kasa Vasavi, Naresh Kumar Sripada, P. Packiyalakshmi, Thella Preethi Priyanka, S. Kaliappan
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mobile Computing and Sustainable Informatics (ICMCSI)
abstract: The contemporary industrial systems are growing to be dependent on the high-dimensional sensor networks, thus exposing themselves to unknown faults that threaten the reliability and safety of operations. Conventional threshold based and supervised learning models fail in these settings particularly when labeled fault information is scarce or non-existent. As a remedy to these shortcomings, this paper introduces an unsupervised LSTM-Autoencoder model that can be used to detect abnormalities and learn to identify sensor failures at an early stage without using annotated data. The model is fit on regular-operation sequences only sampled off of the Kaggle Sensor Fault Detection Dataset, where reconstruction error is used as the anomaly score. The results of the experiment prove the constant decrease of reconstruction loss, high detection rate of 95.3%, and significant reduction of false-alarm rates, as well as good early fault detection. The model is useful in identifying slight pattern deviations on a temporal pattern way before major sensor failures take place. Altogether, the suggested LSTM-Autoencoder is a scalable, robust, and label-free tool to facilitate sensor health in a complex and high-dimensional industrial setting.

title: AI-Driven Anomaly Detection in Petabyte-Scale Data Lakes
authors: Nihari Paladugu, Raunaq Malik, Ranjith Goud Karvanga, Avinash Reddy Pothu, Arpan Srivastava
publish: 2025 2nd Asia Pacific Conference on Innovation in Technology (APCIT)
abstract: The booming digital ecosystem development has resulted in the emergence of petabyte-scale data lakes which act as single repositories of structured, semi-structured and unstructured information. Although they allow more sophisticated analytics, these platforms also pose major problems in the detection of anomalies that are well concealed within extensive, non-homogeneous and fast data streams. Conventional anomaly detectors have limited ability to scale and they are not always sensitive to minor but context-driven anomalies. The paper suggests an AI-based anomaly detection framework which can be customized to large-scale petabyte data lakes and uses deep learning structures, graph models, and ensemble learning to detect both familiar and unfamiliar anomalies in real-time. In this framework, it combines distributed computing, metadata-sensitive indexing, and adaptive sampling, to guarantee scalability, accuracy and low latency to a wide range of workloads. Extensive testing on synthetic benchmarks and real-world datasets show best performance in terms of detection accuracy, reduction of false-positives and throughput over traditional statistical and machine-learning baselines. The suggested solution emphasizes the potential of the AI-based anomaly detection to improve trust, resilience, and operational intelligence in the context of the large data lakes.

title: A Single-Switch High-Gain Switched-Capacitor Inductor Cell Nonisolated DC-DC Converter
authors: Ananya Pritilagna Biswal, Krishna Roy, Arnab Ghosh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: An ultra-high gain DC-DC converter consisting of a switched-capacitor inductor cell is derived. The proposed high-gain, high-efficiency circuit features a single switch, diodes, inductors, and capacitors. The high-gain converter is suitable for sustainable energy technologies. Steady-state characteristics are investigated under conditions of continuous and discontinuous conduction. The voltage stress experienced across the components remains low. Stress analysis of the switches and diodes is performed to ensure appropriate device selection. The ripple voltage and ripple currents are formulated to properly design capacitors and inductors, respectively. The mathematical analysis is performed for gain and efficiency calculation. The derived topology is capable of achieving higher gain without operating at excessively high duty ratio. A comparative study is carried out for various performance parameters. The prototype is validated experimentally to verify the efficacy of the proposed switched-capacitor inductor cell (SCLC) converter.

title: Fuzzy Logic-Controlled Virtual Impedance for Improving LVRT Performance in Grid-Forming Inverters
authors: Masood Mottaghizadeh, Siavash Yari, Shayan Soltani, Innocent Kamwa, Abbas Rabiee, Seyed Masoud Mohseni Bonab
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: Power systems are undergoing a significant transformation from conventional fossil fuel-based generation—primarily driven by synchronous generators (SGs)—toward renewable energy resources that are integrated via power electronic inverters, commonly referred to as inverter-based resources (IBRs). A critical requirement for grid-forming (GFM) IBRs is the ability to maintain stable operation during low-voltage conditions, known as low-voltage ride-through (LVRT). To address this challenge, this paper presents a novel control strategy for GFM inverters based on a fuzzy logic-controlled virtual impedance framework. The proposed method aims to improve both AC-side fault current limiting and preserve DC-link stability during grid faults. Unlike conventional approaches that assume fixed virtual impedance parameters, the proposed control dynamically adjusts virtual resistance and reactance in real time using fuzzy inference rules. This enables the inverter to respond effectively to both symmetrical and asymmetrical faults. Additionally, the approach explicitly considers the dynamic behavior of the DClink, a factor often neglected in previous works. The performance of the proposed fuzzy-based virtual impedance is validated on the IEEE 9-bus system in the MATLAB/Simulink environment.

title: A Holistic Iterative Optimization Approach for Optimal Renewable Energy Resources-Based DG Placement and Sizing in Distribution Networks
authors: Yazdan H. Tabrizi, M. Nasir Uddin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: The paper presents a novel methodology, termed the Holistic Iterative Distributed Generation Placement (HIDGP) Optimization method, for the optimal placement and sizing of Distributed Generation (DG) units within the IEEE 33-Bus distribution system. Utilizing a Python-DIgSILENT interface, the method conducts iterative power flow analyses by systematically evaluating DG penetration at individual buses and combinations of up to four buses. This approach ensures convergence to technically optimal DG configurations. Simulation results demonstrate that HIDGP significantly enhances voltage stability and reduces power losses compared to systems without DGs, and it outperforms conventional optimization methods across multiple performance metrics.

title: Dynamic Synchrophasor Estimation under Frequency Deviation and Oscillations using Power Spline Adaptive Filter
authors: Kuber Saxena, Subhransu Ranjan Samantaray, Sarita Nanda
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
abstract: Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) provide real-time synchrophasor, frequency and rate of change of frequency measurements but often face challenges in accurately tracking dynamic events under noise, oscillations, and abrupt system changes. This paper proposes a new Blake-Zisserman (BZ) based Power Spline Adaptive Filter (BZ-PSAF) for dynamic synchrophasor and frequency estimation in P-Class PMUs. The spline framework enables piecewise polynomial modeling for precise tracking of fast-varying amplitudes, phases, and frequency, while the BZ functional introduces a saturating cost to suppress transients and noise without oscillation during sharp transitions. The performance of the proposed BZ-SAF is validated through IEEE/IEC 60255-118-1a standard cases, as well as multi-interference signals. Furthermore, power swing oscillation analysis in the IEEE 9-bus system confirms its applicability for real-world P-Class PMU deployment in RTDS platform, showing superior accuracy, fast response, and enhanced robustness compared to conventional algorithms. The results highlight BZ-SAF as a reliable solution for synchrophasor-based monitoring, control, and protection in modern power systems.

title: Cascade Second-Order Terminal Sliding Mode Control Strategy for Grid-Forming Converters
authors: Amin Zarei, Ali Hassannia, Yashar Mousavi, Arash Mousavi, Rashin Mousavi, Afef Fekih
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Energy Technologies for Future Grids (ETFG)
abstract: The stability of microgrid voltage and frequency has gained importance due to the rise of inverter-based distributed generation from renewable sources. Grid control faces challenges related to control loops and their interactions with components like converters. This paper presents a robust control approach for grid-forming converters using second-order terminal sliding mode control (2TSMC). The proposed methodology addresses parametric uncertainties and unknown disturbances inherent in microgrid operations through a novel cascade control architecture. New sliding surfaces based on current and voltage error integrals are introduced. The terminal sliding functions are defined to ensure the convergence of sliding surfaces to zero in finite time, enhancing transient response characteristics. The 2TSMC approach is applied in the inner control loop, and the outer control loop the direct droop control is designed. The droop control manages both voltage and frequency. Theoretical analysis demonstrates the stability and boundedness of the sliding functions using the Lyapunov theorem. Comparative numerical simulations in MATLAB verify the effectiveness of the proposed approach, showing significant improvements in point of common coupling (PCC) voltage quality, power tracking accuracy, and robustness against filter uncertainties compared to traditional sliding mode control methods.
